Route: Paths from Greater Granite to Big Bend on the Going-to-the-Sun Road.
Observation Summary: Over the past week, we experienced a significant glide avalanche cycle and a small-distribution but large-sized wet slab avalanche cycle. We expect instability to taper off with cooler temperatures on the horizon this evening and moving into the weekend. However, it is still noteworthy that the snowpack has endured a week of rapid temperature change. Avoiding large terrain with glide cracks/areas harboring isothermal snow with poor structure remaining from the winter/cornice hazards will still be prudent tomorrow.
Got creative out in the whitefish range today.
Weather Summary: Wet. Hot.
Instability Comments: Numerous recent avalanches on the north face of moose peak. We dropped into the north face at noon today. Snow was past prime at this hour, but not terrible considering the lack of freeze last night. First of 3 skiers triggered a wet loose slide on their first couple of turns. This slide ran 400 feet or so before loosing energy as it traveled into older debris on the slope. Size 2.

Route: Up rescue, out Skiumah
Observation Summary: Good corn skiing thanks to what seemed like ..the last freeze up for awhile?  Rescue trail fully skiiable down to old 2.. skiumah trail… not so much.
Instability Comments: one manageable  d1 wet slab and a couple medium small pinwheels around noon

Observation Summary:
I visited the Middle Fork Sunday and noted a breakable surface crust at all elevations early morning.

Above 5500 feet, we found 4-6 inches of moist snow below the surface resting on a thick supportable crust.
The light winds through the day transported a thin layer of dry surface snow on northerly aspects, and we encountered up to 16 inches of dry wind-blown snow in an upper elevation north-facing chute.
By mid-morning, a loud, rumbling Wet Loose avalanche cycle began on steep southerly slopes receiving direct sun. These slides mainly were D1.5 with several D2 and continued through our 2:00 exit.
We stuck to northerly slopes and noted large fields of recent rollerballs and Loose Wet slides, which looked like a groomer had been at work removing all of last week's snow. We initiated rollerballs and small Wet Loose slides on our descent. The riding was fun, but a workout in the heavy snow that needed more time before developing into a corn consistency.
Instability Comments: Wet Loose avalanches and rollerballs developed throughout the day.

Route: Up from the Loop to Granite Park Chalet then back the way we came.
Observation Summary: We found 6-16" of fresh storm snow, variable cloud cover, and increasing temperatures during our brief tour today. We observed many small dry loose (and wet loose) avalanches within the new snow on steep slopes on all aspects and observed localized cracking within the new snow while skinning and skiing on short steep slopes. We intentionally triggered 3 small wet loose avalanches. We expect the avalanche hazard to increase over the weekend with additional snowfall Friday, clearing skies Saturday, and warming temperatures Sunday, but instability will be confined to the recent storm snow.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Variable cloud cover (OVC to BKN) with temperatures climbing to 38*F at 6700' at 1pm and widespread green housing observed.
Instability Comments: Instability limited to new snow atop the old surface melt freeze crust. Localized cracking in the new snow on steep slopes and a storm slab "upside down" structure to the new snow by midday on all aspects below 6700'.

Observation Summary: 2-8'' of new snow between valley and 7500 feet. Surface snow is wet to 5500 feet. Above 6000 feet snow felt nice and dry. Snow near ridge varied from  scoured surfaces to dense windslab. Pockets of windslab were reactive on test slopes to ski cut propagating 5-10 feet by 4-6inches. Dry loose in steep terrain above 6000 feet and wet loose below 5500 feet both initiated from ski turns.
Weather Summary: Snow continued throughout the day partially filling in our skin track between laps.
Instability Comments: Reactive pockets of isolated windslab 4-6'' deep on west aspect near ridgeline. Harmless wet loose and dry loose initiate from ski turns.

Observation Summary:
We took advantage of the open Camas Road to enjoy a day in the Apgar Range. This road closes yearly on December 31 and reopens to vehicle traffic in early to mid-April. 

Cool overnight temperatures left a surface crust supportable to skis at low and mid-elevations throughout the day. However, this crust was not nearly as strong as what we enjoyed last week and over the past weekend in the Flathead Range. 
Light snowfall overnight and through the day totaled only 2 inches by day's end.
The new snow was moist, and each turn on our steep descent initiated numerous rollerballs, but the riding quality remained good.
The south-facing Huckleberry Lookout hiking trail is beginning to melt out and resulting in a slow exit.
The Apgar Range receives significant wind and often has impressive cornice growth along the Crest and the numerous sub-ridges. The cornices we observed today were relatively small, which we assumed was due to this season's below-average snowfall.
Debris over the Autumn Creek west trail from the bowl below Elk.  Covered the ski trail

location: Northern Swan, East of Crater Mountain
Route: Remote observation
Observation Summary: Glide crack has catastrophically failed.  This crack appeared roughly a week ago above a larger crack first observed late Feb.  Other nearby glide cracks (Aeneas, Strawberry, Peter's Ridge) are currently obscured.
Weather Summary: Several days of above freezing temps at or near this elevation.
Instability Comments: A glide crack has catastrophically failed.

Observation Summary: Day began with rain at the Isaac Walton Inn parking lot which slowly turned to groppel on the ascent. We made it to the top of Repeater Ridge and dug two snow pits on the Northern aspect which showed failure, but no propagation on the persistent week layer about 12 inches down. With this in mind we skied a north facing, lower angle shot without any problems. Feeling emboldened by our findings, we decided to step out and ski a steeper north facing line off Repeater Ridge (~40 degrees). Our first two skiers descended without incident, but our third skier triggered a slab avalanche that luckily they were able to ski out of to safety. This avalanche failed on the buried weak layer that was previously noted, had a crown depth of about 12”, width of ~100 feet and travelled about 150 feet. After this incident, we skied back down the South aspect towards Marion Lake which exhibited typical spring afternoon conditions with rollerballs and heavy snow. Lots of lessons learned today!
New or Recent Snowfall (cm): 3” at the top of Repeater Ridge
Weather Summary: Rain low down with snow above ~6000 feet yielding to sunny conditions in the early afternoon.

Route: Up from the GTSR at Little Granite to the Garden Wall weather station then back the way we came.
Observation Summary: Today was a generally a calm day in the mountains with overcast conditions that evolved into broken clouds with considerable solar heating between 230pm and 4pm. Temperatures climbed quickly in direct sunlight and broke down strong surface refreezes by noon below 6k'. Above 6k' we found variable snow conditions that included hot pow, windboard, and thin melt freeze crusts. Roller balls began to naturally occur between noon and 1pm with a spike in activity during the afternoon clearing and warm up.
Weather Summary: Temperatures climbed from the upper twenties to mid-30s above 6k' and mid-40s at lower elevations.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today. Also observed an old partially filled in large crown on the north aspect of Heavens Peak (pictured) and glide cracks forming on Heavens Peak.

Observation Summary:
Went to the Swan to see how Northerly aspects were holding up:

The top 2" of snow has transitioned to moist on NW-N-E up to 6900 feet (upper extent of my travels).
Below this depth the snowpack remains dry.
Rollerballs and pinwheels ran naturally in rocky terrain or where trees were shedding snow.  They were easy to trigger on steep slopes.
Did not see any recent Loose Wet avalanches in the immediate area.  
Skinning was gloppy and skiing was sticky.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: A couple moments of a intense sun through the breaks.
Instability Comments: Rollerballs and pinwheels

Route: Snowmobilied up to Wind blown section of road. Skinned from there up to camp misery and opted to ski over down to black lake. Traveld primarily on shaded W facing terrain and E facing terrain.
Observation Summary: At 4pm, there was over 5" of slushy snow up to 6500' and higher on certain orientations. Observed wind slabs that had been consolidated, but were not reactive. Quite a few glide cracks, looked like. Cornices reactive to human jumping.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Overcast, but still warm even though the sun was not shining anywhere we were this afternoon. Precip started to move in ~7pm, as rain...
Instability Comments: Unsure when wet slabs occured. No reactivity to note seen through out the day besides slight snails from skis.

Observation Summary: A poor refreeze, glopping, and audible whumfing collapses led to an early turnaround. Whumfing was heard twice, both on east-facing slopes between 6000-7000ft. A poor refreeze (if any?) down low made for sticky travel conditions and roller balls/pinwheels on solar aspects before 10am, and the same on polar aspects after 10am. Ski out was variable and sticky at times.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Observation Summary: Snow from last weekend was too sticky to get above middle elevations.. should have hot  waxed my skins…
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Warm.. too warm. Still praying for snow

Route: Up the GTSR then up the shoulder of 54-1/Red Rock then back down before another quick tour up 54-3 to snag a pit profile.
Observation Summary: We mostly toured on northerly aspects today and found variable surface conditions including breakable crusts, firm wind-affect, loose facets, and consolidated fresh snow. The recently buried crust/facet interface is prevalent wherever we looked although not very reactive in instability tests. Travel conditions are sub-optimal and generally challenging currently as we're still stuck down low and touring through thick alder for the most part on the GTSR
Weather Summary: Overcast all day with increasing winds midday and intermittent S-1 precipitation throughout the day.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability noted today.

Observation Summary:
Traveled to southern Glacier National Park for my final field day of the season.

We enjoyed mostly sunny skies, good visibility, and calm winds. No recent avalanche activity was observed. No cracking, collapses, or other signs of instability.
On solar aspects, there were 4 to 6 inches of new snow resting above a faceting crust, up to just under 7000 feet. The surface snow exposed to the sun was becoming moist at all elevations by early afternoon.
Shaded aspects were strikingly cold, as we observed our sweaty hats freeze while digging our north-facing snow pit.
We skied a northern aspect, that had 12 inches of dry soft snow which transitioned to moist snow about 5700 feet. No signs of instability while skiing.
In both snow pits, the new snow was unconsolidated and we did not get propagating results. We dug on a sunny, east-facing slope and a shaded north-facing slope around 6800 feet. ECTX and no fracture in our compression test.
We observed one glide crack opening on a steep, southeasterly slope at upper elevations.
Weather Summary: Periods of intense sunshine mixed with scattered clouds. Calm winds.
Instability Comments: Skier triggered roller balls on sunny aspects.

Route: •	Climbed the ridge above the Burnout, Shed 4D, Shed 5, and Shed 7 avalanche paths to a high point at 6800’ elevation in Shed 7 West.
Observation Summary: •	No recent avalanche activity observed.•	Snowfall totals from the Apr 1st/2nd storm have settled out to 2-3” on the Canyon floor, 5-6” at 5000’, and 6-10” at 6000’ and above.•	Minimal wind loading today, but the newest crop of wind slabs formed over the weekend were 1-2’ thick and found on easterly and northerly aspects above 6000’.•	Experienced 3 audible collapses/whumpfs. All occurred above 6500’ and on north-facing slopes. Hand pits revealed a 2-3” thick layer of facets right under the most recent snow as the layer of concern.•	On other slopes where the pre-storm surface is a stout melt-freeze crust, the recent snow appears to be bonding well and no signs of instability were observed.•	Snowpack surface was becoming moist at all elevations on slopes exposed to the intermittent sunshine by mid-afternoon.
Weather Summary: •	Clear skies in the morning, then building clouds and mostly overcast by late afternoon. Intermittent snow showers with trace accumulations.•	Winds were light to moderate from the east or northeast with minimal blowing and drifting snow. •	Overnight lows dropped to the single digits °F with the clear skies. But temperatures warmed quickly and reached to mid-30s °F on the Canyon floor and mid-20s°F above 6000’ by mid-afternoon.

Observation Summary:
3-10" of new and drifted snow between canyon floor and 7500 feet. Sitting on last week's snow surfaces, which were mostly melt-freeze crusts on steeper, sun-exposed slopes and old snow on sheltered slopes. 1 audible collapse, 2 recent natural avalanches, and no shooting cracks or propagating test results. Snow felt cakey and a little dense on open, west facing slopes at upper elevation but we found no distinct wind slabs. 
1 glide ctack opening on a steep, southeasterly slope at upper elevations. 
Today's sunny breaks were strong enough to thicken the surface snow on the most open mid elevation slopes. May be a slight crust in the morning.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken clouds, with strong sun when windows were open. Calm to light winds until early afternoon, then winds got gusty when convective cell moved through. A few flurries.
Instability Comments: Isolated short, radial cracks around skis while breaking trail. Crust breaking on soft snow below. 1 audible collapse at ~6600 feet as later skiers approached regroup point. Not loud.

Route: Up GTSR from Avalanche TH then up Red Rock/54-1 shoulder to 4500' then down 54-1 avalanche path back to the road.
Observation Summary: Today was the first day up the road of the 2023 GTSR Spring Opening Avalanche Program. During our quick tour we observed locked-up surface conditions, few clouds, temperatures rising to ~0*C, and calm winds. We also observed recent wolf, fox, and bear tracks.
Weather Summary: Pleasant weather for a quick tour and some road plowing!
Instability Comments: Wide propagating fresh avalanche crowns along the E and SE aspects of Heavens Peak above 8k' that appear to be similar depth and distribution to persistent slab avalanches triggered in the Flathead Range over the weekend.

Observation Summary: -HN24: 3-10'' resting on supportable melt freeze crust-Snow felt cohesionless and test slopes did not fail on skin up-Cornices were reactive to stomps and failed - Cornice drops did not initiate slab activity (several moderate sized cornices were dropped onto steep terrain)
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Wind was variable today from the west light to strong with intense busts of snow.

Observation Summary: Slab avalanche from wind loading on east face at about 6,700 ft.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Instability Comments: Instability found near ridge lines. Plenty of signs of wind loading on and just below ridge particularly on east facing slopes

Observation Summary: Remotely triggered a slab on a wind-drifted feature. Other riders reported similar activity. Slabs were small and not running far distances.

Route: multi-drainage/multi-aspect tour adjacent to WMR.
Observation Summary: Dust on crust/ Slide-for-Life conditions on southern aspects. Northern aspects are better but supportable crust still exists. We found that there was not quite enough new snow to be concerned about sluff management even on the steepest slopes. Spring snow flurries persisted throughout the day with short bouts of sunshine.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Instability Comments: widespread loose-wet debris from the previous warming period on multiple aspects at mid-elevations. Several glide-cracks on southern aspects.

Route: Up Wahoo to ~6500' then back on down.
Observation Summary: We observed touchy and easily triggered wind slabs that stepped down to deeper buried weak layers at mid and upper elevations while touring today. We also found good powder skiing in wind-sheltered lower angle slopes and variable surface conditions throughout (classic spring).
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Variable spring weather with blustery winds observed from E, S and W throughout our outing.
Instability Comments: Observed localized collapses from 4800' on up on N, NW, and NE aspects and localized cracking (up to 2m) in wind loaded terrain features. No recent avalanches observed until we both intentionally and unintentionally triggered avalanches (described in avalanche section).

Route: Toured in Ten Lakes area today and found better than expected conditions.
Observation Summary: Light winds with no drifting snow or wind slab formation. Temps were between -6 and -1C throughout the day. OVC skies with light intermittent snowfall. In standard Upper WF Range fashion, exit had moderately deep frozen moose tracks intermixed with alder and refrozen snowmobile tracks.
Weather Summary: Intermittent snow showers with OVC skies shifting to BRK by the end of the tour. Temps stayed below freezing throughout the day. Light winds at Below and Near Treeline locations.
Instability Comments: Found stable conditions on all aspects. No signs of cracking or collapsing. Traveled on slopes up to 35 degrees.

Observation Summary:
An April Fools' persistent weak layer strikes again! Today we observed many signs of unstable snow. 

While traveling a ridgeline we experienced 5 audible collapses. These collapses occurred on a north-facing ridgeline above 5500 feet. 
We experienced localized and shooting cracks. 
We intentionally and unintentionally triggered small and large avalanches above 6500 feet. Triggered avalanches were on west, north, and east-facing slopes.  

The avalanche we unintentionally triggered was triggered from a 32-degree ridge. The collapse occurred while taking one step off of the ridge. This avalanche propagated 600 feet wide and wrapped around multiple aspects. 
The crown height of this slab averaged one foot thick. The thickest section was just shy of three feet. 


Observed natural avalanche activity on east and south-facing slopes across the canyon. These avalanches appeared large (D2). 

Avalanches failed on a layer of near-surface facets that formed during our extended period of high pressure. 
photos video soon.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Light winds with moderate gusts today.

Observation Summary: 3” in Essex and 8-10” at my high point of 7000. New snow was a little upside down. Light snow with moderate wind in the morning gave way to sun breaks by mid day and on my exit solar aspects were getting wet. While skiing I stuck to north facing trees, had several audible collapses including one with shooting cracks where I turned around. Defensive skiing with ski cuts yielded lots of soft storm slabs, the biggest was about 100’ wide and ran 600-800’.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed  Collapsing Observed

Route: From Izaak Walton skinned up NE aspect, South of Essex Creek. Skied down within close proximity of our skin track.
Observation Summary: Initial observations in our snow pit were centered around the 30cm of new snow resting on a weak, sugary layer. ECT was performed on NE aspect around 7200ft which resulted in an ECTP13. Failure occurred at the new | old snow convergence roughly 30cm down.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Convective snow showers, broken clouds and partial sunshine back and forth all day.
Instability Comments: During our ascent we noticed frequent collapsing above 6000ft. We had several occurrences of cracking which ran about 20ft. No signs of recent avalanches were found.

Observation Summary: Afternoon tour to allow the storm to stack up . Found 4” of new at my high point of 6500’. Winds were gusting and swirling. No slab formation yet. Moderate speed sluff ran about 1200’ on my north facing descent.

Returned to Sixmile to recover my rutschblock cord out of last week's pit:

Skiff of snow at the trailhead increasing to an angry inch at upper elevations.
On E->S, new snow rested on a meltfreeze crust of varying thickness and supportability.  All were edgeable, fortunately.  Crust was most rebust and skiable on steep and sparsely treed southerly slopes. Elsewhere it was less consistent and require defensive skiing.
On N->NE, I found up to 6 inches of well preserved dry powder.
I avoided west-facing slopes as they were getting hammered by wind.
Wind transport was surprising considering the limited recent snow.
Isolated and thin pockets of soft slab were trending toward reactive on leeward terrain along upper elevation ridges.
Weather Summary: Some flurries around noon. Wind picked up around 1300 hrs.  Gusting moderate from the SW at mid and upper elevations.
Instability Comments: Isolated and thin soft slabs were stubborn, trending towards reactive.

Route: SW aspect to max elevation of ~6000'
Observation Summary: Quick, easy travel with cool temps and a firm MF crust.  Temps were stable right around freezing until our exit around 1:30.  Steady S-1 snowfall on an edge agreeable crust made for an enjoyable backcountry groomer experience.  We were happy it didn't warm up today.Low cloud ceiling obscured most of the surrounding higher elevation terrain.
Weather Summary: Snow in the trees above ~6300' from some previous snow event.  Winds calm to light from the S.  S-1 snowfall pretty much all morning.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed.

Observation Summary:
Checked out snow surfaces as this warming weather ends and more snowfall *may* arrive. And those surfaces were...variable. A wide variety of melt-freeze crusts and wind packed snow interspersed with soft, and sometimes moist snow. MF crusts softened today but remained supportable at low elevations. Breakable at upper elevations on westerly slopes. That said, we found reliable skiing on most other slopes. Today's light snow bonded readily with these surfaces. Snow on some shady mid and upper elevation slopes showed signs of faceting
In a steep, upper elevation, northerly bowl, we were surprised to find the mid-January crust only a foot below the snow surface. The snowpack in this area was < 150 cm in places. A good reminder that conditions are not uniform and persistent weak layers still exist close enough to the surface for a person to affect. We found no slab above the Jan. structure, and no shears in hand pits. To minimize the chances of stumbling into a trigger point, we used old debris piles for our ascent route in rocky terrain.
Corniuces continue to loom at ridgelines, and new glide cracks are opening on SE slopes. 
Ski crampons useful for low elevation trails.
Weather Summary: Overnight cloud cover meant for a milder morning. Overcast to obscured skies most of the day, with cloud bases about 7000 ft. A few brief breaks, several well timed for us, but little direct sunlight. Frequent flurries, with 1 cm new snow (large crystals). Winds calm to light.

Observation Summary:
Today was a short tour in the Flathead Range to get a handle on diurnal melt-freeze conditions.

We traveled on a supportable crust that varied in depth from 1 to 3 inches, with a dusting of new snow. 
More freeze than melt, as the crust did not soften up by our 3 pm exit. 
We did observe recent loose wet debris piles on steep, southerly aspects, which suggested that recent afternoon temperatures and solar radiation initiated these sunny slopes to slide.
Looming in the backdrop of our day, we could still see the fracture line of a very large persistent slab avalanche that failed on March 2nd.
Weather Summary: Winds were calm.  Sun and clouds scattered the skies. Air temperatures stayed below freezing in the shade.
Instability Comments: Recent wet loose avalanche activity on solar slopes, but we can only guess when they occurred.

Observation Summary:
Headed into the northern Swan to confirm Low Danger. Validated. 

Observed no signs of instability. No collapsing, no cracking, and no recent avalanches.
6 inches of settled snow rests on a variety of firm surfaces. 
Ski cuts on steep leeward slopes along the crest of the Swan produced no avalanches other than very minimal sluffing. 
We observed no evidence of recent wind-drifted snow in this drainage of the Swan. 
Intermittent periods of solar radiation warmed the surface enough to glop up under skis. 

With no signs of instability, we chose to ski steep terrain one at a time.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Observation Summary: 1-2 inches of new snow on the ground, with temps at 32 degrees at the valley floor at the start of our walk. Calm weather all day, with overcast skies, little to no wind, and very light snowfall. No signs of instability, but did notice old wet avalanche debris on south aspects. North facing aspect off the top of Furlong offered fun skiing with dry snow and no noticeable crust until about 5800ft.
Instability Comments: Old wet slides, but not from today.

Route: Snowmobiled to the summer trailhead and toured up the ridge, skied NW bowl
Observation Summary: New snow was easily sluffing on steep slopes that needed to be managed. We saw a lot of glide cracks and old crowns
Weather Summary: Perfectly calm, very light snow throughout the day
Instability Comments: Lots of glide cracks, old crowns and debris.

Route: North east of nyack via cascadilla creek
Observation Summary: Isolated shooting cracks on small slabs on northwest aspect

Route: Skiing north-facing aspects in the Marion Lake area.
Observation Summary: Dug a pit and performed ECT on a north-facing aspect at 6600' with no results (ECTX). Skied steep (>40*) north facing aspects with no signs of instability, had to manage sluff. South facing aspects held 1-2' of dust on a stout sun crust. Overcast with a high (>7500') cloud ceiling most of the day. Intermittent snow flurries in the morning. No signs of wind transport at upper elevations.
Pow n glide cracks
Instability Comments: Glide cracks galore

Observation Summary:
Took advantage of the uninspiring conditions to look for solitude in the Rescue Creek area. Also surveyed new snow amounts, and extent and distribution of wind drifting. Found a little of each - two other parties, 3-5" of fresh snow that was crossloaded into soft drifts less than 8" thick at mid elevations, from downslope westerly winds. These were unreactive. Less scouring and drifting at upper elevations. Elsewhere, new snow was mostly sitting on recent melt-freeze crusts or old wind board. The exceptions were in sun- and wind-sheltered areas.  
Surprisingly good skiing, especially above 5500 ft. Quietest turns on northerly slopes and low-angle slopes.
Ski crampons useful on the icy trail into the basin, and on steep, wind- and sun-hardened slopes at upper elevations. No wet snow, no glopping.
Weather Summary: Overcast, with cloud bases hovering about 7600 ft. and occasional bluish sucker holes. Mostly calm, except for some light, downslope winds late morning.  S-1 flurries off and on during the day, with little accumulation.
Instability Comments: Some short (< 1m) cracks around skis. New snow and recent Melt-freeze crust breaking on low density snow below.

Route: Up Cascadilla, down Crystal creek, over Nyack and down North face, up and down Penrose and back to Cascadilla.
Observation Summary: I found mostly stable conditions during the beginning of the storm.  By the end of the day, up to 3" of new snow had fallen at upper elevations.  Winds were strong along windlines and intermittently gusty below the ridges, which was enough to redistribute some of the snow into very shallow slabs.  I was able to ski cut small avalanches in the most sensitive east facing starting zones, and new snow was sluffing on all steep slopes, but generally there was not enough new snow to be of consequence.

Route: Flying over the Middlefork Drainages. Pilot: Mitch Fontenot
Observation Summary: Lots of wet loose activity on South Aspects, large debris piles

Today was a quiet avalanche activity day in Rescue Creek.

No avalanche activity was observed throughout the day. We didn't even see a roller ball. All previous wet loose avalanche activity was from previous days this week or earlier.
Surface crust made fast travel conditions and was supportable until we transitioned to a northerly aspect of about 5700 feet.
We dug at a pit on a north aspect around 6000 feet, and we got no propagating results.
Spring diurnal weather patterns were clear, as several cycles of wet avalanche activity covered steep slopes. Many of the slides originated from rocky outcroppings.
North aspects skied well above 5800 feet. Below that elevation, the north aspects were punchy wind-stiffen snow.  When we moved to a southerly aspect, the 1 to 2-inch surface crust was firm until around 5000 feet. The transition to wet snow was abrupt, and with the afternoon clouds, the descent was a race to stay ahead of the quickly refreezing snow.
Cheers to the snowshoers that summited Mount Penrose and descended avalanche debris. An impressive showing of snowshoe skills.
Additionally, we noted the low-flying plane over Rescue Basin, and we thank those flying for their observation and photos.
Weather Summary: Sun in the morning. Around 3 pm clouds rolled in.  Winds were light, with a few moderate gusts.
Instability Comments: All avalanche activity was from earlier in the week.

Route: Ascended and descended the ESE face of Elk Mountain
Observation Summary: Older avalanches (several days) were observed on surrounding slopes but no recent signs of wet loose from the recent warm weather we have had interestingly enough.Like stated earlier pockets of wind slab up to ~10cm thick were present.
Weather Summary: Splitter day
Instability Comments: Generally stable conditions for the day. Obviously quite warm in the later afternoon and was able to ski the face in before worry of wet loose was a problem. There was some obvious pockets of wind slab on the skin up but these were easily avoidable.

Route: Up Shed 7 path to East shoulder, dug pits and skied back along the same route.
Observation Summary: Generally stable conditions today and beautiful weather. Meltfreeze crust snow surface was very firm and supportable throughout the tour on solar aspects but began to moisten/soften by 2pm, especially below 5k'.
Weather Summary: Clear and calm. Temps at TH were 11*F at 915am and 40*F at 230pm.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today (avalanche observed was old).

Observation Summary:
We went to the central Whitefish Range to suss out buried weak layers at upper elevations. We did find ourselves an avalanche and also found ourselves a lot of stable conditions. Just depends on where you look. 

We intentionally trundled a cornice down a north-facing slope at upper elevations. The cornice triggered a Hard Slab that failed 2 feet thick. This failed into old snow roughly 1 foot above the ground. (This was thoughtfully executed and over a slope where we confirmed no other parties were.)
We observed no other signs of instability. We found generally safe avalanche conditions on solar aspects until our departure time of 2 pm. 
Previous sled tracks reached into many steep east-facing start zones with no triggered avalanches. 
We rode across old debris piles that came very close to reaching their historical trim lines. These avalanches failed sometime during late February or early March.

We steered away from complex upper elevation start zones on shaded aspects. We did ride in steep terrain on other aspects.
Weather Summary: Beauty of a day.

Route: Up and down the north facing tree route of snowshed
Observation Summary: Climbed up to the snowshed ridge. Was treated to clear skies, great views, and t-shirt weather. Snow above 6000ft was creamy and dreamy. Below that, not so much. Saw one surprisingly sized wet slide in the trees on a north facing slope. Plenty of signs of small point releases as well. Was 15 degrees when I started in the morning, and 44 when I stopped at lunchtime.

Out to check on todays thaw:

Low elevation snow in direct sunlight was softening by 1100 hrs.
Mid elevation southerlies softening by 1300 hrs but were cooled by easterly winds.
The surface at upper elevation S aspect consisted of a thin crust, above 3 inches moist snow on top of dry snow.
Found dry surface snow on N through NE at upper elevations.
Pit at 7000 feet NE aspect had several weak layers in the top 3 feet but did not propagate in large column test.
No new natural loose wet or roller ball activity.

Started heading down around 1530 hrs to avoid overcooked snow on Southern egress. Upper elevation was still a little stiff,  mid elevation had perfect corn.  Low elevations were sticky and punchy.
Weather Summary:
Not a cloud.  Steady light wind from the East
Gusting to Moderate kept things cool.
Instability Comments: Thin and small pockets of hard slab cracked easily along a ridge at 7000 feet.

Observation Summary: Went out to summit Red Crow in Glacier.  Observed the previous mentioned avalanche from the rude goats. Most interesting part of the day was the summit cornice that was starting to rip away and had skin tracks on it from previous groups.  Good reminder how far back cornices can peel away.  Otherwise snow felt in a freeze thaw spring cycle except north faces.
Weather Summary: Sunny, clear, warm
Instability Comments: Some wet slides from previous days and the avalanche from the red Crow crew over the weekend.

Observation Summary: Attempted another higher alpine face today but sadly found this north/north west face had previously slid and the bowl/apron had been hammered with wind leaving l the bowl and apron full wind board or scoured. Stark contrast to JFS canyon two days ago. Two other fun observations from the day: older skin and ski tracks covered by recent wet slides and shout out to the snowshoers from Alabama who were all over the basin with reckless abandon!

Route: Late start from 4000' turned out to not be problematic leaving trailhead at 10:30 - travelled to 7400 feet - returned to car by 13:30.
Observation Summary: -Snow was mostly supportable on solar aspects all the way to 7400 feet on ascent. -Dry snow still exists on northerly aspect above 5500 feet. -Great corn skiing in middle elevation band roughly (5000-7000'). -Snow below middle elevation band felt isothermic by 1pm on descent. -Snow approaching summit was in some places not fully edgeable. -Full value views from the top, cool wind kept the surface snow above 7000 feet locked up. -Saw tracks from large mammal could not identify due to freeze thaw cycle metamorphosis of surface snow (bring bear spray!)

Route: Up switchback, then north to top of Peak 6437. Descended through Checkerboard, passed by Jewel Basin Yurt and used Jewel Basin Road for out.
Observation Summary: Multiple wet loose slides, at least 1 glide crack beginning to open up.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Warm day, blustery winds at top of Switchback and 6437 but no signs of blowing snow, most transport at ridgelines or peaks and in very minimal amounts. Sun crust on affected aspects was 2-3in thick. Sun affect on the snow was minimal from slight overcast conditions, tour up switchback was great until it wasn't. Can definitely see freeze/thaw cycle above treeline.

Observation Summary:
We took the sleds to the Skyland area for a look at the snowpack close to the Continental Divide.

Good visibility provided a 360 degree view of the surrounding terrain and we were surprised that we did not see any wet avalanche activity.
Any snow that was recently touched by the sun was today’s refrozen surface crust that extended up to 7150 feet. Crust depths decreased as we climbed in elevation from 4 inches to a 1 inch crust at the ridgeline.
The cooler temperatures from overcast skies locked up the area, except for the true northern aspects that were sheltered and shaded, holding soft dry snow above 5800 feet. Soft snow at upper elevations was 6 inches above a crust.
Instability observed today was limited to old rollerballs that remain frozen hazards.
Recent northerly winds eroded and minimized cornices formed from prevailing SW winds. These Arctic winds also cross-loaded mid-elevation slopes.
Watch those scratchers, the hardened roads of Skyland's bumps can be tough on a sled's parts.
Weather Summary: Overcast skies all day, but the visibility was excellent for viewing surrounding terrain. Winds were calm, expect a few moderate gusts of northerly winds.
Instability Comments: Older roller balls observed.

Observation Summary: Pleasantly surprised to found solar aspects at elevation skiing quite well, very corn like. Polar aspect still finding powder. Noticed on open east aspects surface hoar forming. Active wet slides on steep and rocky faces in the afternoon

Took a leisurely walk up above Marion to dig around on south aspects and get some views of the surrounding alpine terrain.

Observed one crown that looked relatively newer in the Dickey drainage. This crown failed just below ridgeline. It looked more recent than other nearby slab avalanche activity. 
Wet loose activity appeared a couple of days old. All D1-D1.5 in size. 
Surface conditions on south aspects softened by the time I arrived at noon. Surface snow was still semi-supportable to ski at around 3 pm.  Just starting to get manky. 
North aspects at upper elevation still held dry surface snow. 
Meltwater reached the January crust/facet layer on southerly aspects at 6000 feet.  Facets below the January crust are just starting to become moist.
I only found a few inches of wet snow above a crust on a southerly aspect near 7000 feet. This was however on a slope with roughly a 30-degree angle. I suspect steeper slopes that receive a more direct sun angle have more wet snow. 

There was still plenty of time for the sun to do its work after I left.
Weather Summary: Clear and calm.

Route: Skinned and skied N facing terrain up to 7200’.
Observation Summary: We spied lots of wet loose coming down south aspects across the drainage, and I could just see the toe of debris of what looked to be a large wet slide down into the Marion side that looked to have slid today. We could also see an old large crown across the valley in rocky terrain. Polar aspects up high still held dry powder as of early afternoon. Below 5500’ we found a couple cm’s on a friable crust, except in the open sunny patches where the sun was warming that crust into a nice spring slush.
Weather Summary: Sunshine, sunshine, sunshiiiiine. Not a wisp of breeze.

Observation Summary:
I heard rumors of large avalanches impacting the Autumn Creek trail, so we went to take a closer look.

We viewed three large crowns of Persistent Slab avalanches that failed in late February. These were on east-to-southeast aspects between 6600 - 7000 feet.
All were in steep, rocky terrain with non-uniform snow depths due to the frequent wind. These characteristics are similar to the Persistent Slab we investigated on 3-17 immediately south of these three.
Two debris piles traveled a long distance and impacted the Autumn Creek trail.
The destructive size of the slides varied between D2.5 to D3.

First day out after a work stretch. Found several cm's of new snow on top of crust on more northerly/sheltered aspects. Any sort of direct sun resulted in near instant glopping. We observed a couple glide cracks opening on solar aspects in the park and a handful of point releases on Grant and surrounding terrain but no deep slab crowns like Mark saw. Counted seven cars parked at the rescue and cascadilla spots, so we look forward to their observations!

•	No recent avalanche activity observed.•	No snow profile or stability tests conducted.•	No audible failures or obvious signs of instability.•	No wind loading observed.  Previous wind loading was not recent.•	Above 5200’ elevation on solar aspects, the upper 6” was becoming moist.  •	Above 5200’ elevation on non-solar aspects, surface snow had recrystallized.•	Between 5200’ and 6000’ elevation on non-solar aspects, a stout crust remained intact below surface snow. •	By late afternoon, below 5200’ elevation on solar aspects snowpack exhibited signs of being isothermal and cohesionless to the ground. Did not conduct a snowpack temperature profile.•	On non-solar aspects below 5200’ elevation, surface snow had developed a melt-freeze crust with moist snow beneath it. (~1500 timestamp).  •	Clear skies with light winds from the southwest.  Air temps in the mid-30s °F.
Weather Summary: Clear skies with light winds from the southwest.  Air temps in the mid-30s °F.

Observation Summary: The flip flop conditions in the parking lot and all the avalanche activity suggest this is a bit of a proto-spring? Timing matters more than usual. Our tour up Rescue, over Penrose, and out Skiumah featured plenty of avalanche activity. Solar aspects in Rescue were generally crusty at lower elevations, with varying degrees of wind affected/slabby snow in polar aspects as we ascended to the Nyack/Penrose col. Lots of wet loose debris that seemed gouging fairly deep into deeper, dryer layers for how far it had traveled. Upper areas on the descent to Skiumah saw light wind loading in the bottom of the  chute, with firmer wind surfaces to both sides. Lower down, we skied consistent soft slab debris for over 1500ft. Multiple large slides (D2-D3) have run in the amphitheater above the lake. Skies were clear. Moderate winds on the summit, but light to none elsewhere. Temps cold in the valleys and summit but above freezing below 5k ft on our descent.

Outside of the advisory area but wanted to share as it caught us off guard. We felt fortunate for our timing.  Additionally we had considered that slope generally benign and discussed using some of it for our approach.
Weather Summary: Winds were pretty strong this morning with visible active loading, but generally tapered throughout the day.  Sun was strong but temps stayed cold with winds and snow surfaces remained cold throughout the day.

Observation Summary:
The BNSF snow safety team spotted a hard slab avalanche on Elk Mountain Thursday, March 16. We decided to take a closer look at this slide.

The avalanche occurred at 6600 feet on a wind-loaded east aspect. The failure layer was 10 cm thick, large-grained facets near the ground. These facets formed in November.
The crown varied between 1.5 - 6.5 feet thick and was 150 feet wide. We believe the avalanche initiated where the crown tapered into rocks.
The top part of the debris field consisted of large chunks of hard slab debris.
Surprisingly, we found 2-6" of dry snow in shaded aspects at low and mid-elevations. Areas exposed to the sun softened this afternoon and skied a little like corn.

Route: Up Rescue creek, down wahoo.
Observation Summary: Toured up Rescue Creek drainage this morning and down Wahoo. Breakable crust all the way up to 6000 feet and most of the way down on NW and NE slopes. Small wind slabs and wind affected snow. Lots of evidence of recent slides on the SE and E facing slopes of the Rescue drainage.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking on windslabs.

Observation Summary: Noticed this newer crown on the east face of Elk Mtn from the Hwy. today. There was some new powder snow on the debris, so it's not brand new , but pretty sure it wasn't there on Monday afternoon (13th). Maybe ran from recent wind loading or the Mar. 13/14th rain event?

location: Ridge Between Crystal Creek and Stanton Lake
Route: From the highway headed up the ridge, skied down the ridge to return to vehicle.
Observation Summary: Crust found at all E elevations traveled in (3800-6400), but the amount of new snow totals above crust increased with elevation. Thickness of crust varied from .5 to 2". Observed freshly transported snow on N to E facing slopes.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Mixture of sunny, light to moderate winds, broken clouds, S-1.

Observation Summary:
We poked around in the Swan Lake area to check on new snow and wind-loading:

We found touchy pockets of wind-drifted snow at upper elevations
These pockets were located on specific terrain feautres (along ridges, gullies and midslope convexities).
Slabs were  4-6 inches thick.
We intentionally triggered a D1.5 soft slab below a ridge line.
With light-moderate blowing snow, this problem will continue to develope.
Our pit at 6600' NE aspect had a propagating result with moderate force in the top 16".
No action from solar today but we found several wet-loose debris piles from warming on Monday. 

We rode one at a time on short slopes of  35+ degrees.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: 1" of new snow at the highway increasing to 8" at upper elevation. Sun broked out for only a couple minutes. Westerly winds light and gusting to moderate in the afternoon.  Short-lived squalls of S1-2 with rapidly diminished visibility.
Instability Comments: We initiated shooting cracks and intentionally triggered a small avalanche in wind-drifted snow.

Observation Summary:
In anticipation of warming and sun forecast for the end of the week, BNSF forecaster Adam Clark and I checked out low and mid elevation slopes above the railroad. We found a weak, unconsolidated snowpack below 5000 feet; steep slopes at these elevations may be susceptible to wet snow avalanches that gouge below the surface. At mid-elevations, this hazard would seem to be limited to sunny slopes, where the snowpack is weak, with one significant failure plane. On shady slopes at mmid elevations, the snowpack is well-consolidated, stronger, and more resistent to wet snow slides growing in size as they run. 
We found 3-6" of fresh snow and few signs of recent windloading below our high point at the Shed 7 Weather station (6300 ft).
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken skies w S-1 snow showers and light winds til mid-day/ early afternoon. Then clouds lowered and snowfall intensity increased to S1-2. Winds remained light.

Observation Summary:
We toured east of Whitefish Mountain Resort to take a look at how winds were affecting the surface snow and wind slab development.

Evidence of strong southwest winds was masked by a layer of fresh snow, which was a nice surprise during the first part of the day.
South-facing slopes had a supportable 1-inch sun crust, that was stiffened by recent winds. Frozen roller balls were observed on steep southernly slopes.
Chunky tree bombs (clumps of snow falling off trees or clumps of snow being blown off trees) made skiing a little more sporty.
The top 6 to 8 inches of cornices were sensitive to human weight, however, the underlying dense snow building the base of cornices was stubborn to even the most enthusiastic stomps.
No propagating results in our snow pit. Please see snowpack sections for details.
The sun made an appearance for approximately an hour, which was enough to stiffen the soft snow for a leg-burner last ski down to the groomers.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Light winds with a few gusts, followed by an hour of sun, a brief shower of snow, and full spring sunshine at the parking lot by 3 pm.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking in surface crusts. The top 6 to 8 inches of cornice failed under human weight. Evidence of prior surface warming was observed as frozen roller balls and tree bombs, likely to have occurred on Monday (3/13/23).

Route: Snowmobile up the Mt. Marston road.
Observation Summary:
Sled/ski of Mt. Marston.  

We found a modest amount of windloading at the ridgetops, and crossloading on SE slopes.  
It seemed like the winds must have stopped before the overnight snow as there was 4-5 inches of new on all aspects and all elevations above 5500'.
The warm up from sunday produced a MF crust on any slope that was able to see the sun.  Shaded aspects were saved and didn't have the crust.
We did not see any eveidence of natural avalanches from the weekend.
Our pit on a NE aspect still showed reactivity in a PST.  The weak layer we tested was the Mid Jan crust that was down 70 cm.  It was easily defined as a thin ice layer with large (2-3mm) facets below it.  
Overall I don't think it got as cooked by the sun over the weekend as Peters Ridge in the Swan Range had.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Nice sunny day, shaded aspects stayed cool, solar aspects didn't turn into mashed potatoes.
Instability Comments: Things seemed pretty stable.  No natural activity or instabilities in our pits.

Route: Jakes avalanche path above the rail
Observation Summary: •	The avalanche in Jakes ran sometime between 1630 and 1830 hours yesterday.o	It was a wet loose that initiated in the recent Mar 10th/11th storm snow at the top of the path around 5000’ (photo).o	A few hundred feet down, the debris gouged down into a layer of weak rounding facets (pit profile) which increased the destructive size to a D2.o	Associated debris was about 200’ wide and 1-4’ deep (photo) and terminated within 200 lineal feet of the rail, or about 80% distance to the grade.•	Observed avalanche activity today was limited to more D1-D1.5 wet loose on steep slopes such as Shed 12, the Shed 4D Cutbank, and other steep cutbanks above the highway. No debris affecting the rail.•	Freezing levels climbed to somewhere between 6400’ and 7000’ elevation today.•	High elevation winds were strong, and plumes of drifting snow were noted off the summit of Elk Mountain and other terrain features taller than 7000’.
Weather Summary: •	Partly sunny in the morning, turning mostly cloudy, then fully overcast by late afternoon.•	No precipitation until about 1600 hours when very light rain began, and it has been steady since then.•	Daytime high temperatures reached the high-40s °F on the Canyon floor, the mid-30s °F at 6400’, and 29 °F at 7000’.

Route: Toured up switchback trail to checkerboard summit.
Observation Summary: A wind slab still present but seemed to be in pockets, wet loose and pinwheels from previous day were all over. Hard to say the main player type for the slab observed but hesitate to say a PWL, not enough info

We suspected it was a hot pow/mash potato snow kind-of-day, so we took sleds to the Swan, in search of dry snow.

We parked the sled at 4800 feet and started skinning on wet snow that quickly transitioned into a sun crust that varied in thickness from 2.5 inches at 5200 feet to 0.5 inches at 6500 feet.
Overnight winds appeared to have redistributed the small amount of snow (snow available for transport) as a 0.5-inch to 1-inch soft snow layer over the sun crust.
Winds were surprisingly calm for most of the day. The good visibility allowed us to spot 7 different (old) glide cracks that had been partially filled in with the recent snow.
We observed blowing snow along the ridgeline of upper elevations.
We did not get propagating test results in our snowpits on a SE aspect and a W aspect at 6400 feet.
Yesterday and today’s warm temperatures were evidenced by a variety of small loose wet debris piles, trees shedding snow, and many roller balls.
We skied a westerly slope that held pockets of sheltered soft snow intermixed with a sun crust that turned to manky-mash-potato snow around 5600 feet.
Weather Summary: winds were calm, except along the ridge. Mostly cloudy skies and warm temperatures.
Instability Comments: We had roller balls with every turn, where there wasn't a sun crust. We estimated that the observed loose wet debris was a result of yesterday's warming.

Route: Hwy 2 Essex to Marias Pass
Observation Summary: 4-8" of new snow starting moving this afternoon with sunshine and warming temperatures. Multiple loose snow avalanches observed, but no recent slab avalanches.
Weather Summary: •	Partly cloudy to mostly sunny skies by late afternoon. No precipitation with calm winds on the Canyon floor and light SW winds above 6000’.•	Daytime high temperatures reached the low-40s °F on the Canyon floor and the high-20s above 6000’.

Route: Day 3 of Avalanche-1 course with the Patrol Fund in the Side Country off of WMR. Banana chutes into the canyon followed by ascent up bookshelf.
Observation Summary: Other notable observations from day: A glide crack was visible on the southwest aspect in WMR canyon. At 1400 rapid warming, resulted in a wet-loose slide intentionally triggered on an inconsequential test slope.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Stable overcast morning weather conditions with light snowfall and minimal wind that turned into rapid warming around 1400 as clouds cleared.

Observation Summary: The day started cold, but once the sun arrived, even north aspects at 6K elevation felt the heat, to say nothing of the sweat pouring off our brows. Solar surfaces were moist to the touch at ridgetop elevations in the afternoon, and the new snow picked up density from those high angle rays. Winds were light with moderate gusts.

Made a turn on a convex rollover, small scale, where the wind had drifted snow in recent days. As I made the turn, I released a small but surprisingly deep slab (about 12-14”) that propagated around 50ft to my right and only a few feet to my left. The rollover ran into a small flat shelf so the whole slab only moved about 15-20 feet laterally but moved cohesively as one slab.

We snowmobiled on numerous aspects from 3500 feet to 5700 feet and skied a NW aspect from approximately 6800 feet to 4800 feet. We observed a relatively shallow aspect on this NW aspect at 6800 feet (130-140 cm). Because of this shallow snowpack combined with the steep and rocky terrain, we decided to ski down the ridge to a less steep entrance to our intended line. We triggered several small dry sluffs that ran 100-200 vertical feet on slopes steeper than 35 degrees. The surface snow was very unconsolidated, and we did not observe any recent wind slab problems throughout the terrain we visited today. Surface snow became heavier at lower elevations due to increasing sunshine and warming as the day progressed. Overall, great riding and skiing.
Weather Summary: Overcast skies transitioned to clear skies by early afternoon.

Route: Up Rescue, down Wahoo N and NE
Observation Summary: Stunner of a day to be out with soft snow and no wind.  Sun kept trying to truly poke out and bring on a loose wet cycle, but patches of blue sky were as far as it got.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Calm winds all day with a slight clearing trend.
Instability Comments: No slab activity today but loose dries, natural and skier triggered, were running far.  No loose wet observed, even on SE aspects above Great Bear creek that stayed in a sunny hole for a few hours (although probably too far away to see if they were there).

location: Elk Man & MF Canyon window tour
Route: Typical circuit of Elk Mtn.
Observation Summary: Observed loose D1 wet activity throughout the MF corridor. No obvious signs of instability on Elk Mtn. "Dust on wind crust" > 6,800ft, warm snow below <6,800 ft.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: The clouds came, went, then came back again... A tricky day to choose the correct (low light vs dark) sunglass/ goggle lenses... Temps certainly warmed up, but not as warm as it could have been w/ more sun...

Observation Summary:
Today we went into the northern Whitefish Range to investigate a crown of a recently triggered avalanche.

This avalanche failed on an east-facing slope at 7600 feet. 
It failed on a layer of large, weak grains (depth hoar) near the ground. (See more in video or avalanche tab)
We found 6 inches of new/recent snow. We observed no signs of recent wind transport.
We experienced mostly cloudy skies until 4 pm, which kept surface snow cold.

Route: Up to Cascade Creek to enjoy a few north facing laps, exited on south facing terrain into Rescue Creek.
Observation Summary: Soft turns on north facing terrain, kicking off a bit of loose dry slough. Otherwise no signs of snowpack instability on north facing, shaded aspects. Late afternoon exit onto south facing terrain with small soft slabs breaking off at our skis on a few turns up top. A few tree bombs and point releases on steeper terrain noted with the warming temps, but the snow wasn’t quite catching the direct solar we were avoiding.
Weather Summary: Mostly cloudy throughout the tour, clouds breaking up into blue by 5p. Light snowfall before noon, minimal wind.

Observation Summary: 20-25 cm of new snow on top of all old skin track. Zero wind and light to moderate snowfall all day resulting in a couple cm of new. Natural and skier sluffs were common, only one small storm slab.

Route: From in bounds territory south of Flower Point - skinned south-southeast to Ghoulie Point and continued just south from the point to near "Baby's Butt" ski run. Observation was made on 30 degree slope, 6643' elevation, and on a SE aspect.
Observation Summary: Dug a pit and performed shovel shear and ECT tests. Shovel shear produced light fractures at 30 and 60 cm, but shear quality was low. ECT test resulted in ECTN27. Fracture depth approximately 30 cm but, as indicated, no propagation.
Weather Summary: Lightly snowing and very little to no wind.

Route: Nyack area.
Observation Summary: No wind/0 mph, no sun, about one in. of new between 8-3, below freezing all day. Weird, variable crusts under the new snow on all but the most north-facing aspects.
Instability Comments: Some natural sluffing off steep, rocky terrain, but not traveling far. No instability observed in skiable terrain.

Route: Skinned from WMR Flower Point lift to Ghoulie Point and then skied a short section east of "Baby's Butt" run before skinning back up to the ridge extending east from Ghoulie Point.  Observation was made on 29 degree slope, 6,500' elevation on a N aspect approximately 300 yards east of Ghoulie Point.
Observation Summary: Dug a pit and performed an ECT test as part of an Avi 1 class.  ECT test was ECT-N-27.  Small fracture occurred at 60 cm depth, but no propagation.  Small hand pits (southerly aspect) dug during skinning back to ridge showed 15 - 30 cm of fresh powder on top of a 2.5 cm crust.
Weather Summary: Calm, light snow
Instability Comments: None

location: West aspect near Chicken Bones off WMR
Route: Traversed ridge from Ghoulie point to Chicken Bones with the Patrol Fund Avalanche-1 course. Descended the west aspect of Chicken Bones.
Observation Summary: Right-side-up snowpack, no serious signs of instability observed. Dust on crust on southern aspect of Chicken Bones.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Instability Comments: Recent loose/dry accumulation with potential for sloughing on steeper slopes.

Observation Summary: Surprising amount and quality of new snow at mid and low elevations, w no signs of instability on consequential slopes up to 40*.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Snow showers (S-1 to S1) most of the day. No wind. Clouds in drainages.

Observation Summary:
Tunnel Ridge weather station recorded eight inches of new snow this morning. This was the most of all area stations. We chose a neighboring drainage to check on the new snow totals, wind loading, and the Persistent Slab problem.

We found 25cm (10 inches)  of new snow at 4000 feet and 35 cm (14 inches) above 5500 feet. This snow was extremely low-density and could be called cold smoke, champagne powder, or blower powder.
No evidence of recent or current wind effect along the ridgeline we walked.
A brittle 1 cm sun crust rests below the new snow on steep southerly aspects.
The new snow was cohesionless, and we did not find a slab in the areas we traveled.
Skier-triggered sluffs in the new snow frequently occurred on steep slopes. These would have been a concern on more consequential terrain features.
We found the mid-January crust approximately 1.5 meters below the surface on upper-elevation north aspects.
Weather Summary: Cool temperatures, no wind, and mostly overcast conditions.
Instability Comments: The only signs of instability observed were skier-triggered sluffing on steep slopes and small natural sluffs which appeared to run during the storm.

Observation Summary: Impressively dry, light new snow greeted us from the onset. We skied north aspects, and saw some sloughing, but it wasn't moving far or fast. Temps were below freezing, but progressively warming all day. No solar effect, and no wind. No crust interface between the dry new snow and the older soft snow underneath.

We went to the Swan Lake area to welcome in the storm:

No obvious signs of instability
~3 inches of new snow fell during our tour
We found only a couple pockets of wind-drifted snow that were wiling to produce cracks
These small slabs were still reluctant to slide downhill on a ~35* slope
New and wind-drifted snow sat atop Thursday's sun crust on aspects S through W
Our northwest descent held roughly 1 foot of well-preserved powder
Sluffs ran easily and traveled 200 vertical feet

We felt comfortable skiing extended steep slopes.
Weather Summary: Wind shifted from easterly to southwesterly just after 1200 hrs. We saw less blowing snow after this shift. Snowfall fluctuated with S1-S2 at 1400 hrs.
Instability Comments: Just short, thin cracks in two small pockets.

Route: Up SW ridge, descending N couloir
Observation Summary: Gorgeous day out. Small facets under thin (1-5cm) wind skin at mid elevations.  No surface hoar observed.
Weather Summary: Light winds low, moderate SW around 7000’.  No blowing snow.
Instability Comments: Widespread short cracking and small (5-10cm) windslab pockets above 6500’.  Isolated pockets same depth below 6500’.  Did not observe any windslab slides, just small loose wets from earlier this week on SW at 6200’.

Route: Up Shed 7 W shoulder and along the ridgeline to pit then back out the same way.
Observation Summary: Pleasant day of fieldwork getting Snowslip weather station back and communicating. Variable surface snow conditions kept us on our toes while skinning and skiing.
Weather Summary: Generally scattered clouds with light winds and a good bit of sunshine.
Instability Comments: Very localized cracking immediately around skis in recently wind-loaded locations. No cracking in deeper/thicker wind slabs from the last week or two. Observed a small old wind slab avalanche crown in steep wind-loaded terrain nearby.

Route: Northwest on Autumn creek west to east face of Elk proper.
Observation Summary: Small slab on top of faceted snow. Later was unstable to poles and skis.
Weather Summary: Clear sunny.
Instability Comments: Small wind slabs on top of faceted snow.

Observation Summary: Traveled to Tunnel Ridge Weather Station to swap out the wind sensor, which didn't appear to be giving good readings. That work precluded digging any holes in the snow. No signs of instability or recent avalanche activity, though we did see a at least one large, older fracture line on the high peaks of the Flathead Range.
Weather Summary: Increasing clouds through the day over the Flathead Range; skies over GNP stayed blue. Some clouds pouring over low spots on divide. Winds light at weather station, with no recent drifting. We did, however, see some occasional blowing snow on the high peaks of the Flathead Range.

Route: Up the Hay Cr road.
Observation Summary:
Today we ditched the skis and rode the sleds up to the Whitefish Divide via Hay Cr.

There seemed to be less wind affect than in the Swan range where I was on monday.  All day we only crossed one very small sliver of windboard.  The rest of the ride was in a fairly uniform 4-6 inches of new snow.
Pits were punched on NE and SE aspects.  No propogation in our ECT's.  We found the mid Jan. crust 70-100 cm down.  
Our SE pit had results of PST 30/100 (End) in the facets below the mid-Jan. crust.  This was the same aspect/elevation of the two older avalanches we crossed.  
We found widespread surface hoar above 5500', however, it was pretty well knocked down by the sun on any exposed slope by mid afternoon.
Observed modest rollerball, loose dry and loose wet activity considering the intense sun.  

We road one at a time on steep SE slopes, othewise the riding was great and we felt very comfortable going anywhere the sleds would allow.
Weather Summary: Bluebird!  Balmy and warm, but not above freezing. Light Southerly breeze.  Clouds building in the afternoon.
Instability Comments: We did not see any instabilities in our pits, around our machines or on slopes we could see.

Route: Highline from Wolverine Lakes
Observation Summary: Avalanche slide triggered by snowmobile mid mountain, deep snowpack slab on east facing slope.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Sunny and warm. Calm to light winds.Avalanche forecast was moderate to low.
Instability Comments: 18-24 inches deep slab

Weather Summary: Clear skies gave way to periods of scattered and overcast. Temps remained cold all day.

Observation Summary: -Sizable cornices development on E facing ridge lines - small wind slab 4-6 inches thick fractured on test slope running a short distance. -Structural lemons on north through east aspects kept us off and out from under consequential terrain.-We enjoyed skiing steeps on westerly aspect that hosted warm powder and soft turns.
Weather Summary: Afternoon convective showers with warm sunny skies were a pleasant treat from 12:00-14:00 S-1
Instability Comments: North through east aspects harbor a myriad of PWL of various depths and strengths.

Observation Summary: -Went up Cascadilla Creek for a close look at the previously reported very large (D3) avalanche on the east face of Nyack Mtn (photos). Continued up to the ridge crest dividing Cascadilla and Wahoo Creeks to a high point at 6700'.-Debris from this very large hard slab ran all the way down to at least 5000' elevation, possibly as low as 4600'. It was difficult to determine the exact terminus. This is a vertical fall of 2000-2500' and nearly a mile of lineal distance on the terrain.-Debris from this avalanche sympathetically triggered at least 5 additional slab avalanches, most D2 size.-Investigation of one of these smaller, debris-triggered crowns at 5100' on a NE aspect (photo) revealed the failure layer was facets right on top of the mid-Jan crust.-No obvious signs of instability on our route. No shooting cracks, collapses, or whumpfs.-Skied out on E-SE aspects and crossed underneath several old crowns from the Feb 20th/21st cycle. There was about a foot of softer snow on top of these old bed surfaces which was the mid-Jan crust.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Overcast skies with occasional S-1 snowfall in the AM. Broken skies, no precip, and some occasional sunshine in the PM. Winds were generally calm or light from the west. Daytime highs reached the mid to high-teens at elevations above 5000'.

Observation Summary:
The morning sun made our ascent a sweat-a-thon. We expected to see more solar-related instabilities on southern slopes, but we did not. The afternoon clouds continued to roll in and partly cloudy skies kept ambient air temperatures cool.
We traveled on a supportable crust that we estimated to be the result of a wind/sun-crust combination, that was topped with about an inch of soft snow.
Stubborn cornices previously developed from prevailing SW winds showed signs of erosion from the more recent N and E winds.
We observed reverse wind scour along ridgelines (also from recent N and E winds).
Skiing “stiff” snow on a NE aspect made for many guarded, defensive turns with intermittent pockets of soft snow.  Additionally conditions we sampled during our descent included wind-hammered powder, sun-crust, wind-board, and dust-on-crust conditions.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Periods of sun and clouds.  Winds were calm.
Instability Comments: Mini rollerballs.

Route: On ascent, stayed below (on W/SW aspect) ridgecrest because of winds.
Observation Summary: One several large natural avalanches reported 3/5-6, though date of occurrence not clear. Similar to other recent events: windloaded, north-east facing slope around 7000 feet and possibly triggered by natural cornice fall, sluff, or small slab.
Weather Summary: Howling, cold winds from the northeast

location: East side of the Swan Range -Alpha Beta
Observation Summary:
We took advantage of the plowed West Side Reservoir Road and took the sleds to the east side of the Swan Range.



Recent northerly winds hammered the exposed northerly slopes we traveled in, along with atypical drifts at mid-elevations that were responsible for mandatory sidehilling and exciting sled conditions.
Older cornices formed from southwest winds were eroded from the recent northern winds along the ridgelines.
We got some minor cracking in very small wind drifts along with small roller balls.
Temperatures remained cool despite periods of sun. Winds were calm.
We did not get propagating results in our snowpack tests on southern and northern aspects at approximately 6,500 feet.
We skied sheltered terrain that consisted of 8 to 12 inches of wind blown snow. The best we could describe it as “bouncy” or “springy” powder.
Weather Summary: Periods of sun with mix of high clouds. Winds were calm.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking in very small wind drifts along the ridgeline.

Observation Summary: Much to our amazement no wind today! Recent storm snow is settling a bit, with only a hint of soft storm slab development. As per usual polar aspects the best, everything else is variable. Did include a picture of what I believe was a wind slab induced failure, about 3 feet thick. Notable that it extended and included old growth timber and what I believe most of us would consider reasonably sheltered/safe terrain.

Good snow up high with substantial boosh still to be found up to 5k’ and a rain/MF crust up to ~4500’ below 15cm of new snow… making ingress and egress sub optimal. Wind affected (and actively loading) snow reserved to ridgelines and little loose dry concern with settled powder on slopes ~35*.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Cloud cover built throughout the day as easterly winds increased and temperatures gradually dropped.
Instability Comments: Only one small collapse noted today at ~5500’ at a thinner (and potentially faceted) spot along a ridgeline.

Route: Our party was able to find excellent snow above 7 grand the past 2 days. Today, because of Saturdays sunny colorado like day we made sure to be on North facing terrain and scored deep soft consistent snow all the way to the creek!
Observation Summary: Lots of older avalanches, big ones.Skied above 35 degrees on treed and open slopes.
Weather Summary: today was cold and high cloud decklight winds
Instability Comments: Saw massive old avalanches on Mount Adams steeper terrain lookers left of peak 500' below the ridgeline. It was difficult to see with the eye how thick the crusts were on S facing slopes, but saw small roller balls. Definitely effected by solar.

Observation Summary: Travelled to 7000 feet on south aspect-Observed no evidence of wind effected snow on this aspect-Variable sun crust existed too ridge top on exposed features (did not affect riding quality too much)North facing terrain-Small cornice growth was unreactive to cut-Small wind pillows unreactive to cuts as well-Scalloped and duned shaped textures were seen under 5500' (not cohesive or stiff)-Cold wind in our face on skate out of Dickey-Blower pow
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Observation Summary: Very large natural avalanche that likely failed during the late February natural avalanche cycle. Similar to other very large natural avalanches that occurred at this time. North through east facing, upper-elevation, leeward slope, possibly triggered by natural cornice fall, sluff, or small slab.

Ski toured the Whitefish Range near Olney and experienced overall good conditions on S-W facing slopes with some thicker wet snow in sun affected areas. Small rollerballs above 5000ft. Dug two pits:ECTP8 failed around 30cm on a S/SW face ~5400ftECTN 6 failed around 30cm on SW/W face ~5700ftManaged terrain by selecting areas with relatively clean runouts and avoided wind affected and N/E aspects.
Instability Comments: Rollerballs noticed on sun affected aspects later in the afternoon

Observation Summary:
We traveled to Cascadilla in the Middle Fork to look closely at a very large D3 hard slab avalanche on Nyack Mountain.

This slide occurred Thursday, March 2, during an intense wind storm with gale-force winds resulting in substantial wind loading.
The trigger was a wind slab avalanche that released 200 feet below the ridgeline. 
Crown depth was estimated at 5+ feet deep. The width of the slide is approximately 1500 feet, estimated using CalTopo.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Prolonged periods of the sun with light winds and cool ambient air temperatures.
Instability Comments: Numerous natural loose dry sluffs occurred during the recent stormy weather. These were harmless size D1. We observed triggered sluffs from skiers on steep terrain and from prolonged solar input. These were also D1 in size.

Observation Summary:
We searched the many ridges of Sixmile for wind-affected snow.
- We encountered one pocket of wind-drifted snow that was sensitive enough to crack and slide a few inches. 6800ft NE aspect.
- We found a couple small thin soft-slabs that would not crack around our feet.
-Cornice of of varying shapes dotted a ridge perpendicular to the previous SW winds. We could break off the most recent growth with some stomping.
-Recent snow sat atop hard drifts formed last week. On direct southerlies the snow was stripped down to the hard surface.
- We saw light blowing snow from Southeast winds at upper elevations.
-Solar aspects were moist by 1100 hrs.  Trees were shedding snow and causing rollerballs at 1400 hrs.  A new sun crust had formed by 1600hrs.
Weather Summary: Clouds building in the afternoon.  Wind mostly calm gusting to light from the South around noon and shifting to easterly.
Instability Comments: On isolated pocket with cracking

Observation Summary: Temperatures Saturday felt much warmer than predicted. The sun was warming whatever it touched but the north aspects skied great. Roller balls were gaining stem at the end of the day.
Weather Summary: Saturday - Warmer than forecasted and sunny all afternoon.Sunday - Ridge lines were very windy with active snow transport out of the NE. Once off the ridge and in the trees it was mostly calm.

Observation Summary: 6+” of new snow on top of yesterdays skin track. No wind. Sun was doing its thing on solar slopes. Heavy then refreezing. Polar aspects still glorious. Sluffing but nothing slabbing up

Route: Leaving Olney and heading to Red Meadow
Observation Summary: Clients reporting two feet of fresh powder near Red Meadow and previous slide before reaching Red Meadows that groom was unable to clear.

We climbed to roughly 6000ft where we found about 12 inches of new/recent snow. We skied a NE aspect that was sheltered from both the sun and trees, and snow was dry and unconsolidated on this sheltered slope. There was much rejoicing.
Weather Summary: Overcast skies in the morning became mostly sunny by late morning/early afternoon. It was a calm day in the mountains with no wind experienced at our location.
Instability Comments: Just some minor stuffing below rocks or trees in steep terrain.

Route: We skied north and south aspects north of Dickey creek between roughly 5300 and 6900 feet; we kept the slope angle to ~30* or less aside from the occasional steeper rollover.
Observation Summary: We found excellent skiing on northern aspects and conditions that deteriorated quickly from the solar blast on southern aspects. Some party members regretted their wax choices (or lack thereof) and everyone brought out the skin wax. After one south facing run we returned to the shady north where the snow remained cold and dry. In steeper terrain the heavy wet snow on S aspects may have been more of a problem, but with mild terrain choices we interacted with no obvious instabilities.
Weather Summary: Sunny, warm, and calm!
Instability Comments: The sun came out and cooked the snow on south aspects creating heavy, moist, gloppy conditions, especially at lower elevations. We did not observe any substantial roller ball action, however.

Route: Out and Back
Observation Summary: Multiple crowns and wind drifting noticed. Had to take an alternate, shorter, safer route.
Instability Comments: Multiple crowns and wind drifting noticed. Had to take an alternate, shorter, safer route.

Observation Summary:
Dug profiles at multiple elevations to assess sensitivity and distribution of mid-January ice/ facets structure. The variability in structure and results was notable. The ice and facets were buried deeper at mid elevation profile site than our upper elevation site. The structure was generally but not completely unreactive. At the upper profile site, the layer of most concern was large, rounding depth hoar near the ground.
