Recent and drifted snow is forming a soft slab 15-30 cm thick at the snow surface. It produced some short cracks at uptrack corners and we released one small and harmless slab at mid elevations on our descent.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Intermittant snow showers - S-1 to S1, increasing to S2 mid afternoon. Gusty winds veering from SW to NW. Periodic blowing snow. A few breaks in the clouds.
Instability Comments: A few short cracks on uptrack corners.

Route: Up through the bush from the Camas Road closure then up a whole lot more bush to the lookout for a snow science chemistry pit. Back down the way we ascended.
Observation Summary: Today we observed a very manageable and predictable dry-loose avalanche problem isolated to steep north facing slopes above 5000'. We also found prolific bush that made ingress and egress challenging. We observed light gusting moderate SW winds mostly at ridgetop and intermittent S1-S2 snowfall (mostly graupel). We dug a pit at 5200' on a SE 15* slope and found lingering weak snow around and between the mid-January crust and deeper Xmas crust - see attached pit profile for more details. We tested rounding facets buried ~75cm from the surface and just above the mid-January crust and observed unstable results (PST 37/100 END)... further evidence of the lingering persistent slab problem (picture attached).
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today on our quick tour.

Route: 3/3: Traveled SW from parking lot to north facing ridge at low elevations above the x-country trail.3/4: Traveled SW from parking lot to north facing ridge up to 4,800'.
Observation Summary: Yesterday, we found several layers of crusts mixed with faceted layers. These layers were reactive. Today, in sheltered terrain we saw pockets of very weak snow with wind deposited and new snow on top. We did not see any significant ECT results. As we traveled we performed hand pits that showed signs of this wind/new to be forming a slab.
Weather Summary: 3/3: We witnessed cloudy skies and wind, light snowfall and graupel surface.3/4: We witnessed significant warming from the sun to the snow surface. It was a beautiful day to be out in the mountains.
Instability Comments: Collapses were isolated under individual body weight as we moved through terrain.

Observation Summary: Strong winds transporting snow, scouring surface and making wind slabs. Sun crust up to 7400 on solar aspect, covered by 4-6” assuming the wind hadn’t blown it away. Sheltered polar trees were the ticket. Snowed most of the day but hard to measure any meaningful accumulation

Route: Skied S/SW aspects.
Observation Summary: Great skiing in low angle trees.  Snowing and wind transporting snow on shoulders/ridges all day.  Minor cracking and small slabs moving without much energy later in the day.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed
Weather Summary: Snowing all day.  Wind transporting snow on shoulders and ridges in the trees, not far below these zones the snow had little to no wind effect.
Instability Comments: Minor stuffing on steep pitches in the morning, no other signs of instability.  As new snow accumulated observed minor cracking, less than ski length.  By the afternoon on small steep slopes we were able to trigger small, very soft slabs within the storm snow.

Observation Summary:
We optimistically committed to our goal of reaching a mid-elevation crown despite the forecast for gusty winds:
-We were lured into the basin by decent visibility,  light westerly winds and light blowing snow.- Winds increased to moderate, visibility  rapidly deteriorated and we were hit by an extreme gust that stopped us in our tracks at 1100hrs.
-We dug a pit and found mixed results in our tests as we waited to see if the gust was an anomaly.
-It became clear that strong-extreme gusts and the resulting whiteouts were becoming the norm, so we abandoned our goal and made an early retreat.
-Wind-drifted snow was becoming sensitive during our exit: We were able to trigger table-sized slabs 3-4 inches deep.
Media to follow
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Light snow and increasing westerly winds throughout the morning with gusts strong to extreme.
Instability Comments: Minor cracking in wind-drifted snow during our early exit.

Observation Summary:
I chose the windy southern Apgar Range to monitor wind slab development and to take a closer look at the snowpack structure in an area with a skier-triggered slide ten days prior.

At 3300 feet, 6 inches of loose, dry snow rested on a rain crust. Above 5000 feet, up to 10 inches of loose, dry snow rests on older settled snow. The rain crust disappeared around 4200 feet.
Moderate to strong winds and blowing snow were observed from the valley floor to the ridgeline. 
On and directly below ridgelines were the only spots where slabs and cornices were cohesive enough to be reactive to my weight. They were very soft elsewhere.
Saddles on the ridgeline provided the most excitement. This is where winds funneled and cranked, resulting in cornice and slab development. 
Fresh drifts throughout the tour were soft but up to 30 inches thick.
A snowpit revealed the Christmas and mid-January crust, but snowpack tests resulted in ECTX.
Good riding conditions in areas not scoured by the winds.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Moderate to strong shifting winds on the ascent up an east-facing slope. The north-south ridgeline had sustained southwest winds. Light snowfall throughout the day resulted in minor accumulation.
Instability Comments: Wind slabs were cohesive enough to crack beneath my skis but only traveled beyond the length of my skis in the most wind-loaded areas on the ridge.

Observation Summary:
Weather station maintenance day for Aeneas Station in the Swan Range. Thanks to Ted and Adam with the BNSF Avalanche Safety Program for helping us maintain our weather stations.
- 6 to 10 inches of settled powder from Tuesday's storm.
- New snow sits on a pencil-hard wind slab that is roughly a foot thick.
- Mid-January crust is buried 185 cm at 6500 feet. This crust is in close proximity to buried surface hoar. This weak layer was the culprit of numerous avalanches during January and early February. 
Limited time for field data. Wind, temp, and RH are back in action.
Weather Summary: Light winds in morning. Sustained moderate/strong on ridgeline. Wind speeds felt that they picked up in the evening.

Observation Summary:
We traveled to the northern Whitefish Range to assess the impact of last week's storm:
-We were unable to reach our intended viewshed due to large drifts of snow blocking the road.
-We saw no recent signs of avalanche in our limited views of start zones. 
-We found a thin and weak snowpack at low and mid elevations on aspects NE to SE
-We got variable results in repeat tests in our snow pits.
-Westerly winds continued to rake trees clean, blow snow along ridges, and build cornices.
-Surface snow at low and middle elevations became moist by early afternoon.  Roller balls and very small wet loose ran from rocky, south-facing terrain.
-Ramping winds and building clouds cooled the snow surface enough to form a breakable crust on solar aspects by late afternoon.
We skied through the crust happily as we avoided the thick timber, brush, and exposed talus slopes we encountered elsewhere.  We felt confident riding a short slope steeper than 35 degrees as there was barely enough to cover surface roughness.
Weather Summary: Westerly winds gusted to moderate and clouds moved in  the afternoon.
Instability Comments: Cracking and collapsing on SE through NE aspects near 5000 feet.

Route: Lazy day late start bluebird pow tour in southern Glacier Park from 4400' to 7000'. Descended NE exit back to car.
Observation Summary: We thought we were in a different region with a bluebird pow day today! We had a lazy start and didn't start skinning until around 1215. Although the temperature stayed at or below freezing, the sun had warmed the roughly 4" of new snow at 4400' enough to cause skin glopping in solar areas. By the end of the day (1730) all areas with prolonged solar exposure below roughly 5500' had developed a sun crust as clouds rolled in and temperatures began to fall. I expect areas that developed this sun crust to have particularly active sluffing with the forecasted snowfall in the next couple days. --New snow totals were between 4" and 6" with higher totals at upper elevations. --We observed an intermittent and gusty, generally SW-W wind blowing and transporting snow at summits and ridgelines throughout the day. --Felt a few collapses, with one larger audible one while ascending a S ridge around 6300'. --Observed wind loading and wind affected snow throughout the tour, with loading generally left over from last week's strong NE wind event, and cornice formation in unusual areas. --New snow was fluffy and skied phenomenally.
Weather Summary: Mostly clear skies changed to broken, then overcast by sunset. Snow in areas with solar exposure developed a sun crust when clouds rolled in. Temperatures remained below freezing. Winds were intermittent and breezy at lower and middle elevations, with occasional gusts from unexpected directions. Winds were SW-W, moderate, and sustained at ridgeline throughout the day. We observed moderate blowing snow on ridges and summits.
Instability Comments: Felt two small collapses and one audible bigger collapse while ascending a south facing ridge between 6200-6500'.

Observation Summary:
Traveled to a drainage with some of the larger slide paths in the southern Whitefish Range to look for evidence of a persistent slab avalanche problem involving the mid-January crust/ ice. The steep, rocky paths in this terrain don't appear to have produced natural avalanches during the February cycle. We did find rounding facets under the mid-January ice layer in a mid-elevation profile on a southeast facing slope. Tests resulted in no propagation or sudden failures on this layer, or storm interfaces above. 
We triggered one wet loose sluff on a very steep convexity. No other signs of instability.
Weather Summary: Blue sky morning, with mid-day inversions, then late afternoon stratus. Winds light and variable, with hints of fresh wind transport on exposed ridgeline. Cold in the shadows, warm in the sun.

Observation Summary: Impressive walls left by snow slide - looked more like Spring time action, but it’s been very cold,  Recent heavy snow followed by -20 to -30’F with an 8 hr window of 50+ mph winds - Today 22’F
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Light snow showers all day

location: JFS Canyon Snowshed and Snowslip Mtns.
Route: Highway observations and a ski tour in the Shed 7, 6, 5 area
Observation Summary: •	No recent avalanche activity observed.•	A small (D1.5) slab avalanche ran in the Test Slopes on the Shed 6/7 ridge sometime during the Feb. 20th/21st storm.•	From the highway we saw the crown of the large hard slab avalanche that the FAC team investigated on Sunday, Feb. 26th. This is on the east face of Snowshed Mtn. and just outside of the Railway Program Area (photo).•	No obvious signs of instability observed. No collapses or whoomphs. •	No active windloading. Signs of previous windloading evident on all aspects.•	Substantial previous crossloading of mid and upper elevation starting zones in the Program Area. Particularly in the starting zones of Shed 5 and Path 1163.•	Excavated a test pit at ~6000’ elevation, SE aspect, 30° slope to investigate and test the persistent weak layers of concern that are deeply seated in the snowpack.o	Total snow depth was 170 cm (67”).o	The Feb 20th/21st pre-storm interface was at 105 cm (26” down).o	The mid-Jan. facets/crust interface was at 65 cm (40” down).o	The late-Dec. facets/crust interface was at 45 cm (49” down).o	The first Extended Column Test yielded no fractures (ECTX)o	Compression Test resulted in CT22 Q2 at the Feb 20th/21st storm interface.o	Deep Tap test performed over the mid-Jan facets/crust resulted in DT25 Q2 on facets just above the late-Dec. crust.o	Still curious, we removed 14” off the top of our 2nd ECT column and repeated the test. We had propagation at 30 taps on the facets atop the late-Dec. crust with the block then sliding into the pit.o	Finally, a Propagation Saw test on the facets above the late-Dec. crust resulted in 65/125 END.
Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with light snowfall accumulations throughout the day.•	Light SW winds above 5500’ elevation.•	Daytime highs reached into the upper 20s °F on the Canyon floor and into the upper-teens °F above 6000’ elevation.

Route: Beaver Ponds - Kona - Big Trees
Observation Summary: Skied some better than expected snow on the north side of Goulie then walked up to Kona. There were wind drifts in odd places and in probing the NW side of Kona(6300') found small hard wind slabs spread out along the treed ridgeline. In a hand pit test I had a 30cm fairly hard slab shear off with moderate force on a layer of graupel.Observed multiple insignificant loose dry slides in the Skook Chutes.
Weather Summary: Snowed lightly off and on until about 2:00 when it came down at 2"/hour for almost an hour. 3-4" of new snow during our tour on top of about 4" from the night before.
Instability Comments: Nothing of concern

Observation Summary:
Today we went avalanche hunting and mostly got skunked.

Surprisingly, the drainage we toured in today did not have widespread natural avalanche debris that the neighboring drainages experienced.
We observed 6 soft slab avalanches (D1 to D2) on northern aspects at mid-elevations. Across the drainage, the steep southerly avalanche paths did not appear to run.
There was 3 inches of new snow, over 3 inches of recent snow, all on top of a crust. This crust disappears at around 5000 feet.
There were many very small point releases of loose, dry sluff that occurred today.
We did not have propagating results in our ECT. Please see snowpack tab.
Skiing powder was worth the sporty skinning over downfall and dodging alders.
Weather Summary: Winds were calm, with intermittent squalls of snowfall that lasted about 10 minutes each. The low cloud ceiling made for poor visibility.

Observation Summary: Spent a couple days touring in the South Fork Two Medicine. Conditions were great as recent snowfall fell on supportable wind-board that was present among most terrain in the Two Med valley. Wind was calm to nil. Tuesday evening a good band of snow deposited a few quick inches before skies cleared and the mercury dropped to zero. Wednesday we found great skiing on low-angle, E facing slopes (6500-5000) under sunny skies before the onslaught of Westerly wind began to wreak havoc in the region.
Weather Summary: As advertised, winds began to pick up Wednesday afternoon bringing overcast skies and large wind transport.

Route: Pin is just the area. Essex. Snowshed.
Observation Summary: Some cool natural activity from the last few days. We had good vis and were able to see many crowns on the surrounding peaks. Didn’t want to push it in these conditions, kept it pretty low angle. Lots of facets under the wind crust.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: The wind has been blowing in the middle fork in all directions and the snowpack reflects that. Easy to see wind loaded cornices and cross loaded ridges. The new snow didn’t move around too much yet.
Instability Comments: No audible collapsing or cracking, saw some big natural crowns and old debris that was covered with the fresh snow from the last few days

Observation Summary:
Today we partnered up with the BNSF Avalanche Safety program to look for recent avalanche activity and assess the snowpack structure on a path above the rail.
- We experience 4 audible collapses. These occurred at mid-elevations on an east-facing ridgeline. 
- We had propagating results in an extended column test on an east aspect at roughly 5400 feet. The slab above this weak layer is roughly 2 feet thick. 
- Surface snow is faceting.
- We observed numerous crowns at mid and upper elevations (no surprise), some of which are already documented. 
We primarily stuck to ridgelines and spaced out on the few very short steeper pitches.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Overcast skies in the morning. Broken skies in the afternoon.
Instability Comments: 4 audible collapses on an east-running ridgeline between 5100-5400 feet in elevation.

Observation Summary:
Today's priority was to practice our rescue skills and thank Whitefish Mountain Resort's Ski Patrollers.

As Forest Service employees, we are required to practice our beacon skills once a month. Grey Wolf Beacon Park (at WMR) is an excellent place to accomplish this requirement.
We are incredibly grateful to work with the ski patrol professionals at WMR. They share their daily weather data, which improves the value of our forecasts.
East of WMR, we traveled to Skook peak to take a look at the various wind-affected surfaces.
Multiple layers of building cornices (photo) were telling of the winds over the past several days. Cornices were stubborn except for the top thin layer. Those layers looked like diving boards hanging over the main cornices. Rideline surface snow was firm, unsurprisingly. 
We did not have propagating results in our ECT test in our northwesterly snow pit at 6700 feet.
We skied a sheltered, northwesterly slope. We made some nice turns in the top soft snow with a little harmless stuffing.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Most of the day was calm winds. Light snowfall around midday, but it didn't seem to accumulate.  The afternoon had periods of sun, but temperatures remained below freezing.
Instability Comments: Harmless sluffing in recent 2 inches of soft snow.

Observation Summary: Traveled to Link Mountain to de-rime the weather station. Compared to earlier this winter, it was almost nekkid. Recent natural avalanches crossed the groomed trail below Red Meadow lake. Recent bouts of winds have deposited a chaotic sea of drifts at near ridges above about 6500 feet. Even away from ridges, many slopes harbor a layer of drifted snow thats mostly supportable. It made for some harder-than-it-should-be skinning and variable skiing.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: S-1 to S1 snow showers through the day, with alternating obscured and broken skies. Accumulation of 1" on sleds mid afternoon. Appears to have been 2-5" in since Sunday AM, with lots of debris scattered on trail at low elevations and drifts along groomed trail. Sunday's snow not as rimed/ graupel as in Flathead Range. Light but gusty SW winds today, with light drifting at ridgeline.
Instability Comments: Some short cracks 10-15 cm deep and < 100 cm long in drifted snow, mostly where it sat on older, very hard snow.

Route: East and South Facing Slopes Between 3,800-6,400'
Observation Summary: We found nice riding and good stability on South and East facing slopes, lower and middle elevations. Our south facing lap was through fairly mellow glades and our east facing lap took us into steeper terrain( 45+ degree avalanche path). 15cm of nice powder on supportable windboard 6400-5200. Faceted surface snow and minor sloughing observed in steeps 5200-4400. Punchy unsupportable windboard/crust and variable/poor skiing below 4400.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Light snowfall, broken skies, and calm winds throughout most of the morning. New snow throughout the day estimated 2cm middle elevations. Sun began to peak through around 2pm, leaving a thin superficial crust on southernlies.
Instability Comments: None Observed

Route: Leaving Olney on the way to Red Meadows we observed two areas next to each other that had recently slid.  Approximately 3-4 miles past upper whitefish lake these slides obstructed the trail.
Observation Summary: You can see above the overhang where the avalanche broke from.  The slide was across the groom trail obstructing the trail.  People have cut across this on snowmobiles so there is a single track trail through the slide.

Route: Headed up toward Apgar Lookout, got to about 4800' and turned around.
Observation Summary: We experienced some localized cracking around our skis. Most of this SW aspect we somewhat wind affected.We observed obvious recent crowns on the NW face across from the canyon near Apgar lookout.We dug a pit which showed 18" of densified new snow on drying rounded snow. There was a slightly faceted layer 6 inches down in the new snow. The interface between the new snow and the drying rounded snow was poor. We recorded an ECTN15 but after the ECT we pulled lightly behind the block that was left and it easily popped off. While we felt the newer densified snow was not consolidated enough to propagate we decided to turn around based on this information.It was quite warm at our location with only light winds.

Today we visited a crown to identify what the recent carnage in the Flathead Range is failing on. We suspect this slide occurred during the Feb 20/21 storm. 
- We observed a hard slab that failed on a persistent weak layer (facets below the mid-January crust). This avalanche failed on an E aspect at 7000 feet.  
- We estimated this avalanche was a D3 (very large). We found snapped trees in the debris on the smallest part of this avalanche. Visibility was obscured where this avalanche ran the furthest.
- A large portion of this avalanche wrapped onto a 30-degree slope. That slope angle is the low end of slope steepness for "avalanche terrain." The majority of this start zone measured 35 degrees. 
- Strong winds were transporting snow into small firm slabs. The only sign of instability in the new snow was isolated cracking.
We traveled on bed surface and avoided steep terrain that had not slid. Unfortunately, this will likely be my approach for some time to come in the Flathead Range. 
 
Sorry for the bad photos. Poor lighting.
Weather Summary: Gale force gusts at upper elevation ridgelines. Increasing winds throughout the day.

Observation Summary: Lots of blowing snow and plenty of thin, soft slabs of drifted snow near crossloaded, mid-elevation ridgelines.  Unreactive, except for one soft slab released on a ski cut. Cornice drops did not produce slides. We did not linger in the maelstrom of winds at the main ridgeline to investigate more exposed terrain. Stuck to sheltered, north through east slopes with short pitches of 30-35*. Very good riding on soft, settled, boot-top drifted and new snow. Snow profile and tests on a northeasterly slope at 6700 ft did not produce propagation or sudden shears under the wind slab, on a layer of graupel, or near the mid-January crust (~130 cm below the surface).
Weather Summary: Westerly and southwesterly winds most of the day, reaching into valley bottoms at times. Gale-force gusts at ridgeline, with an hourish break mid-afternoon. Snow much of the day, S-1 to S2, pushing S5 briefly. Mostly heavily rimed precip particles that were not cohesive. Hard to tell how much fell because of moderate-intense blowing snow.

Route: Standard skin route to the top of Sub Shields (6996) and down the "Big Run"
Observation Summary: High winds along the ridge that leads to the summit.  The cornice on the approach to the top of 6996 was very sensitive and broke off while standing five feet from the edge.  The southerly facing bowl near the top of Sub Shields was very wind affected. It appears that the wind had blown in several directions over the past few days as wind loading was occurring in areas where it normally doesn't.  The ski down the big run was "slabby".
Weather Summary: windy
Instability Comments: I witnessed one collapse where wind loading was occurring on a ridge.  I experienced snowpack cracking in a similar location.  These were both isolated incidents.

Observation Summary: Variable snow up to 6,200 ft. Trees were dripping by 4 at about 4,000 ft. even on non-solar aspects.
Weather Summary: Calm, mostly sunny.

Route: Lower skin track (westerly) for approach to ridge on top of Skookoleel, back down low angle trees near skin track.
Observation Summary: Snow was soft and mostly unaffected by wind up until about ~6400ft. Above that was the expected wind dunes, drifts, and harder pack surface. Firm wind affected surface on ridge.
Instability Comments: Some cracking around skis on test slopes, with no obvious signs of propagation or sliding. Cracking was more prevalent as we reached higher elevation on more wind affected snow, still no propagation.

Observation Summary: Visibility improved throughout the afternoon and many recent, large to very large avalanches, were visible all along the Flathead Crest on typical leeward aspects. Name any peak along the crest in the Essex region and there were crowns present; variable elevations, but certainly in steep, alpine terrain above 6500'. The largest avalanche crown observed was in the unnamed "Sheep Lakes" area of Sheep Creek (East of Felix)...Estimated D3 or D3.5. The width of the slide was impressive.
Dug a pit at 6100' on a 30 degree SE facing slope that wasn't obviously wind affected. ECTN, a little lateral force caused the column to fail on sugary snow at 115 cm.
Temperature (F): 15 in the morning and 35 in the afternoon at hwy 2
Weather Summary: Calm and overcast most of the day though clouds were moving fast along the ridge. Sun came out around 4 as the clouds blew off.

Observation Summary:
I visited the Cascadilla drainage to catalog recent avalanche activity associated with the Atmospheric River event from February 20 and 21. 

Most every avalanche path in this drainage slid during the storm. Three paths ran as hard slabs and produced D3 debris at the mid and upper elevations.
Twelve mid-elevation paths produced D2 soft slab debris which terminated in the valley floor. 
10-15 smaller soft slab slides were noted in cross-loaded convex terrain features between 5000 and 5500 feet. 
Avalanche crowns ranged in elevation from around 5000 to 6600 feet and were on NW-NE-SE aspects.
The surface snow, which was cold-smoke quality yesterday, had warmed and densified but was still in good shape.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Observation Summary: We found nice supportable powder on sheltered, mid-elevation S-SE terrain. No cracking or other sign of instabilities encountered. Sky was broken until 1330 when clouds cleared and the sun really shone through. Calm to moderate mostly SW winds kept the snow surface cool. We observed wind effect in open burned areas at middle and lower elevations. The snow was punchy, sugary, and much less supportable at lower E elevations.

Route: From Delrey to Upper Whitefish Campground and Lower Antice.
Observation Summary: My guide reported a lot of downed trees and drifts from Delrey to Upper Whitefish Campground. We did not go up striker but he would imagine that with the wind blown snow and trees there will be some slides.

Route: Skin up to Goulies ridge, dig pits, ski down trees above and right of Half Moon
Observation Summary: Considering the strong, Northeasterly winds the Whitefish Range experienced over the last 48hours, the SE aspects including our test site seemed relatively unaffected by the wind. Extreme wind affect resulting in pillows, wind slabs, and sastrugi was observed during our skin along Goulie Ridge. The SW aspect of which we descended back into the resort we encountered few pockets of light snow, though mostly wind affected with small reactive wind slabs.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Light winds coming from the SW, cold temperatures, very short breaks of sunshine masked by a thin layer of clouds, with predominately overcast skies, no precipitation

Route: Skinned just South of Ghoulie Pt to assess conditions, then skiied NW facing slope to the Bigfoot T-bar.
Observation Summary: We found clear evidence of the recent NE wind event on the landscape, in the form of tree debris and wind slabs. SW facing slopes seemed to remain relatively unaffected. The ridgetops had wind slabs that varied in depth and hardness, and were limited in size because of the tree-cover along the ridge.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Winds were light from the SW, cloud ceiling was around 7000ft then became clear after 4:00pm, no snowfall.

Observation Summary:
Quick explore while Mr. Cold Miser was distracted. Winds early this week played havoc with the snow near ridgelines - drifts, dunes, scouring, crusts, slabs. Surface patterns indicated these were upslope winds, mostly from the N and E. Also found evidence of upslope southerly winds at basin bottom. All hope is not lost, however. Mid slope snow remained soft on E, S, and NW facing slopes. 
Did not find much evidence of a wind slab problem on these mid elevation slopes. A ~10 cm thick, 4F+ hard wind slab failed easily in tests, but did not propagate and weak layer did not appear to be a persistent weak layer. No propagation or alarming results in the top ~75 cm of the snowpack in tests on a mid elevation, ESE-facing slope.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Decreasing clouds. Light SSW winds.
Instability Comments: Surprisingly, no cracking in wind-drifted snow longer than a foot or so.

Route: Navigated some pronounced whoopties and a wind drifted roadway in search of powder in the swan.
Observation Summary: Wind F-ed snow on SW, NE, N, and W aspects, including in treed areas that seemed as if they should be protected. Snow remained on trees but somehow not on the ground. In some places the surface snow was knife hard wind board. We did find some good turns in some tight trees and noted that anywhere it opened up, even briefly, was hammered by the wind. Where the snow was good, it was deep.
Weather Summary: 2 degrees in the AM, 14 in the PM at the car. Cloudy in the AM and clearing in the PM.
Instability Comments: Despite visual cues of the wind’s effects (scouring, cornices, debris everywhere, sastrugi), we saw little to no cracking, heard no whoomphs, and saw no recent avalanches.

Observation Summary: Natural avalanche observed by public, submitted by forecaster.

Crept back out into the cold.  Left the skis behind to simplify our plan:
-The first 15 minutes of riding was pretty brutally cold.  After the handwarmers heated up and the screaming barfies went away, it turned into a pleasant day.
-This area received roughly a foot of snow earlier this week.
-We found several small natural loose-dry and slab avalanches that failed during the the snowfall.  
-This area seemed to have been spared from the recent NE winds.  We did find evidence of SW winds at all elevations. 
- We slope cut small test slopes without any signs of instability. 
-Our route was choked off by a long train of hard and stubborn drifts at 7000 feetRiding through or around the drifts seemed like more cost than benefit, so we bailed on our summit bid and made an early exit.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Temps started just below 0 and ended just above.  Some low cloud giving way to decent visibility under broken skies. Intermittent light SW wind.
Instability Comments: Minimal cracking around machines.  No perceived collapses in limited ofd trail travel.

location: Flathead Range - Snowshed Mtn and Skiumah Lake
Route: Driving Hwy 2 and ski to Skiumah Lake
Observation Summary: Saw portions of two large crowns on Snowshed Mtn, D2s at least. One on the SE face above Sheep Creek at about ~7000' and another on the E face above McDonald Ck at ~6800'. Estimated these ran back on Feb. 20th/21st during the storm. This last one may have been previously reported? Crown thickness looked to be 2-4'. Could not view the rounout or debris.Skied into Skiumah Lake in the afternoon. Widespread avalanching in here during the Feb. 20th/21st storm. Multiple D2 and D3s with a few events putting debris down to the lake. Multiple crowns observed on the surrounding terrain, 2-4+ feet thick and some running several hundred feet. Two large ones up on the NNE face of Penrose and Peak 7510' (photos). Also, notable were a couple large crowns (estimated 3-4') thick on the steep slopes only a few hundred feet above the lake, around 5200' (photos).Several crowns look like they may have failed on older persistent weak layers but did not inspect any crowns close up to confirm.

I enjoyed time with the Patrol Fund Level 2 class in the morning. In the afternoon, I took a short walk up Rescue Creek to catalog recent avalanche activity and assess the near-surface conditions.

Many large avalanche paths ran earlier this week during the Atmospheric River event. I spotted 4 D3-sized slides that had hard slab characteristics. Debris is estimated to have run 2000 - 2500'+. Other paths produced D1.5 - 2 of mostly soft slab debris. The lighting was poor, and I could have missed a few slides.
A handful of thin, soft slab crowns was observed in cross-loaded convexity features.
The number and size of avalanches were about average for an avalanche cycle in this toilet bowl.
This afternoon, the southwest wind kicked into gear, moving plenty of low-density surface snow and snow off tree branches. 
8 cm of champagne powder rests on a supportable 10 cm thick crust at 4000'. At 5000', 15 cm of cold smoke rests on a 5 cm breakable crust. 
Large swaths of this drainage escaped the effects of the northeast winds.
Good riding conditions today. Southwest winds may affect the snow quality in this open drainage by tomorrow.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Cold temperatures were bearable until I traveled further up this open drainage. Increasing winds and ambient air temperature resulted in chilly wind chill readings and exfoliation of any exposed skin.
Instability Comments: Cracking was observed in soft wind slabs at low and mid-elevations. These did not propagate beyond the length of my skis.

Route: Hwy 2
Observation Summary:
Out for a drive on a cold day. Visibility was good enough to spot some crowns in the Flathead Range.
- Noted one large (D2) natural failing on a wind-loaded SE slope at upper elevations.
-Saw several of the recently reported crowns on Nyack.
-Wind effects were notable at all elevations particularly near the continental divide.
Weather Summary: Wind and drifting increased in JFS canyon up to the summit.  Blowing snow obscured the sky near Marias Pass

Route: View from Hwy 2
Observation Summary: At least 4 crowns in Cascadilla Creek from very early yesterday or the night of the 20th/21st. All were partially filled back in with recent snow. One was down on the low bench around 5300' and the others were up high (6500-7000') on the NE face Nyack Mountain. The upper ones may be the same events reported yesterday?

Route: Up WMR East route, then out towards Goolie's.
Observation Summary: Some very minor movement associated with small windslabs, otherwise new snow seems to have bonded pretty well on the southerly aspect we skied.  Minor differences in aspect had dramatically different snow though.  Surprisingly, lower elevation snow was more wind affected than the snow at upper elevations in halfmoon.

Route: Driving backroads saw lots of wind drifts and consistent, strong winds.. obviously.  good day to be a tow truck driver..
Observation Summary: Icy roads and mostly drivable drifts.
Weather Summary: Normal February weather

Observation Summary:
Today we went for a ridgewalk in the Flathead Range to escape the chaos of the windy valley.
- Only a couple of inches of snow on top of a wet snowpack at low elevations. Snow heights increased rapidly with elevation. Roughly a meter (3 feet) of new and recent snow. 
- We experienced many audible collapses at middle elevations (5000-6500). One of the collapses shook snow off of trees 30 feet away. 
- We kicked and stomped small test slopes and were able to easily trigger small slides in new snow. 
- Minimal wind-drifting at middle elevations. There is a lot of snow available to transport. 
- We observed roughly 5 large avalanches at middle elevations. All but one of the crowns were mid-slope and crowns wrapped around terrain features. The bed surface had a sheen to it that looked crust-like. I anticipate these all failed on one of the faceted crusts (Christmas or Mid January). 
Trail breaking was arduous. Snow is slow. We stuck to ridgelines.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Weather Summary: Dropping temps throughout the day. Sun was trying to poke through but never did.
Instability Comments: Loud audible collapses between 5000 and 6500 feet. We experienced 6 throughout the day.

Observation Summary: Clients reporting 18-20 inches of fresh snow over the last 3 days

We heeded the avalanche warning in the Flathead Range today and chose terrain that offered low-angle slopes and shelter from the wind.

Most of the day we traveled in cohesionless powder, except at wind-exposed ridgelines and saddles.
The new snow contained a lot of graupel and was moist up to 7,000 feet.
We tried to kick off some cornices, but they were surprisingly soft and would not propagate.
We skied powder until about 5800 feet, at which point, the snow got progressively heavier and eventually turned to mashed potatoes and roller balls at about 4500 feet.
We spotted a 3-foot crown of a recent avalanche on a wind-loaded slope. It was 150 feet across and ran 300 feet on a northeast-facing slope of about 6800 feet. 
The anticipated atmospheric river started at about 4 pm, with big clumps of snowflakes (larger than a quarter).
Weather Summary: The morning was calm with periods of sun.  At ridgelines, there were moderate gusts that transported snow. The atmospheric river did not arrive until 4pm in the Essex area.
Instability Comments: Today we were in a conservative mindset, so testing for instability was limited.

Observation Summary: Enjoyable skiing on sheltered southerlies. No signs of instability.
Weather Summary: S1 most of day. S5+ began around 3pm. Modrrate west winds above about 6500.
Instability Comments: None observed

Route: Skied n and nne aspects.
Observation Summary: Copious amounts of low density snow. You could describe it as “blower”, “hero”, or  “bring you snorkel” snow… We did!We avoided steeper leeward terrain with roll overs, and features that were likely wind loaded to avoid risk of exposure to wind slabs.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Snow accumulated throughout the day, often and strong “pulses”” of intensity.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed

Route: Ascended the ridge following the long slog through the flats of Fern Creek. Descended one of the open lines, one at a time, and then slogged back to the car.
Observation Summary: We observed light, unconsolidated storm snow in areas that were not wind-affected. Wind-affected areas had limited evidence of instability (cracking) other than the convexity one of our group members was unfortunate to hit.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Warm and wet down low. Cool and breezy up high.
Instability Comments: We found cornices to be moderately sensitive and observed cracking and collapse with some stomping. Other than the avalanche we triggered, there were no major signs of instability.

Observation Summary:
Today we went to the favored Swan Range to get a taste of some deep snow and see what problems were developing. 
Two takeaways:
1. Don't trust wind-drifted snow. We unintentionally triggered a slab that failed 1 to 1.5 feet thick that took me off my feet. I only went a short distance due to the relatively benign terrain we were in. The slide was triggered just below a ridgeline with light wind drifting. 
2. Excellent riding in wind-protected terrain. We managed sluff by selecting slopes with slope angles less than 38 degrees. Sluffs only ran short distances in this terrain. More aggressive tactics would be needed in steeper terrain because sluff did entrain snow as it moved downslope. 
 - NOTE: This slab failed on a persistent weak layer (Surface Hoar) that likely formed Wednesday or Thursday. This layer was beneath a harder windboard under the new snow. See video for more information.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Steady moderate to heavy snowfall. Precip rates appeared to be higher below 6000 feet. There were roughly 3 inches on the sled during the 4-hour tour.
Instability Comments: Evidence of drifting snow. A loose-dry problem that felt on the verge of being a storm slab problem in wind-protected locations. Few loose drys did begin to fracture into small soft slabs.

Route: Standard trail up to Marion Lake, then skinned up to the ridge between Marion and Dickey. Down the middle drainage to Dickey Rd and back to IWI
Observation Summary: Wind slab
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Wind appeared to come from all directions. Lots of gusts. Snow was was definitely being transported
Instability Comments: On the ridge line we observed a clearly wind loaded slope. We intentionally ski cut the wind loaded slope to observe wind slap pop off. Slab broke away cleanly and traveled 20ft before settling in mellow terrain.

Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed.•	Since Friday, Feb. 17th through midday today, the Canyon floor picked up 3-4” of snow with 0.30” SWE. Upper elevations accumulated 5-7”.•	Snowfall rates increased around noon today and the Canyon floor has picked up another 3-5” with 0.25-0.30” SWE since then. Another 4-6” has accumulated at elevations above 6000’.•	Moderate to strong westerly winds produced heavy wind-loading at times above 6000’.  •	Ski cuts on fresh wind slabs resulted in only minor cracking underfoot or no cracking at all.•	Dug a hasty pit near the Burnout path at 5200’ on an east aspect, snow depth was 39”. ECTP25 and ECTP20 on a layer of facets between the mid-Jan. and late-Dec. crusts about 14” down.•	Dug a second hasty pit at 5800’ on an east aspect, snow depth was 69”. Just some ECTNs in the upper 20” with no fractures on the facets (~30” down) between the above-mentioned crusts.•	The mid-Jan. crust was buried 30” down below the surface in Shed 7 W at about 6600” and the late-December crust was buried about 40” down.•	Cornices are growing large in Shed 7 West (photo).
Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with light to moderate snowfall (S1 to S2).•	Daytime high temperatures reached the low-30s °F on the Canyon floor and the low-20s °F above 6000’.

Route: Headed up to the moderately treed area just before Marion Lake.
Observation Summary: Performed ECT at 6400'  on a SE aspect with a result of ECTN2 failing on the interface between the variable crust and the fresh snow. There was only ~5" of fresh snow and it was loose and dry so we were not concerned. Sluffs weren't running at 35 degrees. Two crusts were clearly visible in the pit but everything below the upper crust was well consolidated. Crust was generally supportive but there were some areas where the crust had some lightly faceted snow underneath. Overall riding was awesome, we did 2 laps. Shoulda been there Mack.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: It was surprisingly warm, hovering right around freezing all day, and straight up hot with the sun out. Snow tapered off around 11 AM and sun was in and out all afternoon.

Observation Summary:
Hard to beat a storm day in the Middle Fork.  Our risk management began early as the forecasted daily snow totals caused us to relocate to a zone with less exposure to avalanche terrain.  The quantity of new snow didn't meet expectations, but the quality or riding certainly did.
-We found roughly 6" of new snow.  Combined with this week's prior snowfall it amounted to ~12" above the most recent sun crust at mid and upper elevations.
-The recent snow seemed well adhered to crust below. We could not produce sluffs or cracking.
-The snow surface showed minimal wind effect,  however, we were wary of old windslabs hidden beneath the new snow
-We walked atop small cornices. These broke easily and tumbled downhill without revealing any other instabilities.
-Pit tests indicated stable results on weak layers buried in the snowpack.
We steered around areas where we exected previous wind-loading. We group skied terrain less than 30 degrees and allowed some distance between skiers on steeper sections.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Winds were light and variable starting at 6000 feet.   At ridgetop (7200 feet) winds were NNW.  Snow tapered by late morning and the anticipated afternoon snowfall never arrived. We even saw the sun and some blue sky at moments.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability

Route: Leaving olney trail head going over Stryker Loop there is some moving snow activity in a couple of the slide areas, mostly just snow balls rolling down the hill. Fitzsimmons trail and snow conditions are looking great there was a little bit of drifting over the trail in a couple areas but are cleared now.
Observation Summary: I seen a couple areas on Fitzsimmons that were drifted over but are now cleared and also a small amount of snow movement in one area on fitzsimmons

Route: Trail to lookout
Observation Summary: SE facing avalanche near 4-5k. Wind-loading on S,E, and W aspects. Warming temps were conducive to light roller-ball action.
Weather Summary: Sunny skies with 2-3” of new lightweight snow with a surface hoar. Observed recent avalanche on distant slope SE Facing at mid/high elevation
Instability Comments: Isolated audible collapsing near windswept ridgelines above 5k

Route: Road to trail to ridge up to 6600 feet and back
Observation Summary:
Check-up in the Swan Lake area:
-No recent avalanches or obvious signs of instability
-Found intermittent and breakable sun crust at middle elevations
-Saw wind-affected snow that produced minimal cracking
-Located at least two facet/surface hoar layers in the top 3 feet of the snow pack. The buried surface hoar is well-weathered
-Extended Column Tests did not propagate in pits on NE and SE at 6400 feet
-Found a new crop of surface hoar that had already toppled over on slopes exposed to the wind.
- Cloud cover kept the snow surface dry above 5000'
Despite the above findings, I limited myself to slopes less than 30 degrees as I was without partner today
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Skies started mostly clear with clouds building late morning.   Sun broke through again around 1600 hrs.  Wind gusted light from SW.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability.

Observation Summary:
I chose a known windy drainage in the Middle Fork to evaluate current and recent wind loading along with surveying the surface before the weather changes to a more snowy regime.

Several inches of loose, dry snow capped the surface above 5000. This snow generally rested on a wind crust or older hard wind slab in locations open to the wind, nearly everything above 4500' in this drainage. In sheltered terrain below 5000', a thin breakable temperature crust from last weekend was beneath the new snow.
There was toppled-over surface hoar on the surface in many locations. 
Southwest winds transported the recent snow at upper-elevation ridgelines and on the valley floor throughout the day.
Wind-affected snow was found on most slopes above Skiumah Lake. Slabs only cracked under skis, with no shooting cracks observed.
Snowpits on shady mid-elevation slopes revealed a stronger structure than most recently observed pits. This is due to the large amount of wind the snowpack in this drainage is subject to.
Snowpit tests had no propagation, and no recent avalanches were observed.
Weather Summary: Mostly clear morning skies gave way to overcast conditions in the afternoon. The temperature remained cool throughout the day, even when the sun was out. Moderate southwest winds affected higher ridgelines and the Skiumah valley floor.
Instability Comments: Cracking in freshly formed wind slabs. Sluffing of dry, loose surface snow on steep pitches.

Route: Up the main drainage and up the south face to pit profile location. Down similar back to the railroad and the car.
Observation Summary: We observed a surprisingly soft surface snow atop a generally supportable crust. We did see signs of recent wind loading throughout the Shed 7 W bowl and kept our descent route to lower angle planar slopes that lacked the pockets of windslab observed near the ridgeline.
Weather Summary: Pleasant weather with few clouds, light gusting moderate SW winds (only transporting snow along ridgelines) and warming temps throughout the tour from 930am to 1pm
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today. There were a couple of day+ old piles of cornice debris along the upper ridge of Shed 7 W but nothing of concern.

Observation Summary: Scrappy trail access down low, obviously. Lots of wind up high mid afternoon, varying from West to North, blasting the upper reaches of the ridge I climbed. 5 mm surface hoar was fairly prevalent in wind protected areas. Generally low snowpack in this particular zone. Hasty pits revealed Xmas crust down 50-80 cm’s. NW descent route from 7000 to 5200 harbored varied conditions. Cross loaded wind features up high were locked in place, despite lots of stomping. Once below 6400 ft skiing conditions were OK, with 1-3” fresh snow on a variety of near surface breakable crusts. Surface snow sluffed fast on steeper pitches. Most consistent skiing was below 6000 with a more northerly aspect. General faceting of surface snow was occurring in the lower fully shaded elevations.

location: Swan Range, north and east of Peters Ridge (Brown Ridge Area)
Observation Summary: See photos.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Overcast to clear and cold.
Instability Comments: Photo Swan1 was first observed Feb. 9, and is current as of this date.  Swan2 is a brown frown about a quarter mile north of Swan1, and was first observed Feb. 14.  Swan3 is a brown frown about 3/4 mile south of Swan1 and was first observed this date.  Swan4 is a brown frown about 2-3 miles south of Swan3.  All are observed and photographed through a telescope from about 5 miles away and with a cell phone.

Observation Summary:
Took a break from the machines and used human powered devices to ascend and descend some of the fine mountains in the Flathead Range.
-There is a nasty crust from the warm up last friday that exists up to 5000'.  Beyond that elevation the crust seemed to be confined to the southern aspects.  On the solar aspects it was prevelant to the ridgetops at 7K.
-We found old windslabs at the ridgelines.  From a distance we could see several E facing slopes that were well cross-loaded.  
-There was 4-6 inches  of dry snow from overnight.  This snow had not been moved at all by the wind by the time we left.
-We did not find any surface hoar.
-Skiing was great.  We felt plenty comfortable skiing bigger terrain.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken clouds with some very small sucker holes.  The ceiling was high enough all day to give grande views of the terrain we were in.
Instability Comments: No instabilities observed in our pits or while skinning.  We did notice one 6 inch wind-slab that had released, and steep slopes were certainly prone to some minor loose-dry while skiing.

Route: We gained a S ridge and followed that north to the high point and skied a S/SW aspect down to the summer trail.
Observation Summary: The crust was present and firm on our S facing approach up to 5k or so. Higher elevations in protected areas actually held several inches of soft snow on top of the crust. Once topping out at the summit there was obvious signs of wind transport but the southerly aspect still held snow even in less treed areas. The crust was untouchable until about 5200’ and minor sloughing was noted. We probed around 5200’ and found 175cm depth. Obviously the lower we got the crustier the snow got.
Weather Summary: It was hard to tell how much recent snow had fallen due to the wind. We stayed off the N side so I’m not sure how loaded that was. There were still 3-4” of fresh snow on the S facing bowl.
Instability Comments: Other than minor sloughing, no signs of instability in our tour. There were at least 2, maybe 3, glide cracks towards Peters Ridge. Unfortunately with an iPhone and overcast skies I wasn’t able to get a worthwhile photo.

Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed.•	About 1” of new snow on the Canyon floor and 4-6” above 6000’.•	No signs of instability observed. Ski cut a couple fresh wind slabs with only minor cracking underfoot or no cracking at all.•	Light to moderate SW winds above 6000’ elevation were occasionally loading leeward slopes just below ridgelines. But minimal wind loading over the course of the day.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: •	Broken skies with occasional very light snowfall. Minimal accumulation throughout the day.•	Daytime high temperatures reached the high-20s to low-30s °F on the Canyon floor and the mid-teens above 6000’.

Route: Skied NW and W aspects, we felt comfortable skiing slopes up to ~ 35 degrees
Observation Summary: We observed some Isolated cracking in the top 1-2" of new snow. There was significant previous wind loading along ridges at upper elevations with moderate to large cornices hanging over east facing aspects. Minimal sluffing of new snow on steeper pitches.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Route: Running Rabbit Traverse
Observation Summary: Bluebird skies and general light winds. Minimal snow transport noted. Skied a mix of powder and 2-3" of dust on crust on mostly south and west facing aspects.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)

Route: Wet slide in southeast face of true Elk summit.
Observation Summary: Long slide. Approximately 500 meters long.

Today was a comical test of contingency plans. Good thing we had a plan B and C. 

Rain at the trailhead was not ideal, with sloppy, wet conditions. We decided to reevaluate and drove further East to the Essex area in the hopes that the summer trail would be supportable despite the rain at lower elevations.
At 5600 feet, the dusting of new snow transitioned to about an 1 inch of snow. The snow was moist until about 5800 feet. The day’s total accumulations at 6000 feet was 2 inches.
We visited the recent avalanche on Essex Mountain, estimated to have run on 2/11/23. We wanted to take a look at the bed surface layer of the avalanche, as a possible failure of a persistent weak layer or a wind slab avalanche.
Turns out, this avalanche was a great example of a cross-loaded slope sliding on a 10 cm pencil-hard crust. We estimated that the crust that was buried 85cm from the surface was formed around Dec. 25 (as we have referred to it as the Christmas crust).
This was a hard slab that differed in slab depth, commonly seen in cross-loaded slopes.
Unsurprisingly the ski out was fast, as the wind-buffed surface was Mother Nature’s groomer.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Rain in the morning at the trailhead, followed by brief periods of graupel and snow. Broken clouds with pockets of blue sky in the afternoon.

Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed.•	Poor snowpack structure persists between 5000’ and my high point at 5500’ elevation with weak facets below the mid-Jan. crust and a building slab on top. This slab is now 1-2’ thick.•	No signs of instability observed. Ski cut a couple fresh wind slabs with only minor cracking underfoot or no cracking at all.•	Moderate to strong W-SW winds, predominantly above 5000’ elevation, were actively loading leeward slopes (photo) until late afternoon when the winds began to calm down.•	Dug a snow pit and conducted a full profile at 5300’ elevation on a SE aspect, 35° slope (see pit profile). Found a poor snowpack structure with a layer of facets below the mid-Jan crust about 16” below the surface. Unstable result with the first Extended Column Test, ECTP24 on said facet layer, but then ECTN25 with the second test on the same layer.
Weather Summary: •	Broken or overcast skies with light rain or rain/snow mix on the Canyon floor and light snowfall above 5000’ elevation.•	2” of new snow today above 5000’ feet and a 0.25” water on the Canyon floor by the time precipitation ended around 1600 hours.•	Moderate westerly winds with strong gusts at mid/upper elevations with winds subsiding around 1600 hours.•	Canyon floor temperatures were in the low-40s °F in the morning and cooled as the day progressed, finally dropping below freezing around 1700.

Observation Summary: We skied a north facing aspect in a sheltered zone with sparse trees starting near the Nyack area in the Flathead Range. We noticed no instability, some old windloading near the ridge, but otherwise soft snow. The zone was sheltered from the sun and did not seem terribly impacted by wind from recent weather events. Calm day, no wind and mostly overcast skies aside from brief sun in the morning.

Route: Traveled through and observed slopes across the compass from 3,900-8,000 ft
Observation Summary: 2x natural avalanches, variable surface snow conditions, no other obvious signs of instability. Cloud deck was above neighboring peaks. No other natural avalanches observed.
Weather Summary: We observed wind gusts moving snow along ridge lines.
Instability Comments:
Observed 2 natural avalanches. Both appeared to be recent, possibly 2/11/23?

Both appeared to be wind slabs. #1 lower elevation @ Marion lake = cross loaded?. #2 @ higher elevation= top loading?

Both were in rocky, steep (40'+) terrain.

No other obvious signs of instability observed.

Route: Day off wander at mid-elevations in the northern Flathead Range. Did not travel above 6700 ft, .
Observation Summary:
Widespread wind effects above 4500 ft. Some scouring, but most slopes < 6500 ft had 15-30 cm of dense, wind-packed snow (aka wind slabs) at the surface. Ranged from 4F hard to 1F hard. These were unreactive; we experienced no cracking or collapsing while skinning or skiing slopes up to about 38*.  Snow/ rime melted or blown out of most trees below about 6500 ft; trees above still white. Spotty melt-freeze crusts onSW and S slopes below about 6500 ft, possibly higher. 
The wind-effected snow skied better than expected, though still grabby at times. We found soft, low-density snow on gladed NW-N facing slopes, or improbably given the NW winds, in a NW facing basin.
Weather Summary: Overcast with hints of blue sky. Some wind gusts midday at mid-elevations, from both west and east. Otherwise light winds in this elevation band. We could hear strong winds near uppe elevation ridgelines, though we saw no blowing snow. Warm below about 4000 ft.
Instability Comments: A glide crack is opening on a steep, upper-elevation, east facing slope on Mt. Penrose. The slope is bedrock in the summer.

Observation Summary:
Today we rode around in the N. Whitefish Range to see what's going on up there. We found generally safe avalanche conditions at middle elevation. We didn't get above 6400 feet.
- We observed no recent natural avalanches, no shooting cracks, and no collapses.
- We found wind-drifted snow at middle elevations. Slabs were hard, thin, and unreactive to sled cuts. We observed evidence of drifting snow at upper elevations. 
- We got propagating results in extended column tests. These failed on a layer of stellar dendrites that fell last week. These were decomposing and we had no other signs of instability on this layer.
- A poor basal snowpack structure exists in the N. Whitefish. 
We rode in steeper terrain and did short high marks in wind-protected areas given the lack of instability.
Weather Summary: Overcast and calm wind. We never reached upper elevations or ridgetops.

Route: Skied E, NE, N, and S aspects near WMR.
Observation Summary: Light to moderate wind gusts, though pretty calm between gusts. We had cloud cover all day. No cracking was noted, no avalanches observed.
Instability Comments: Light sluffing of the surface powder or crust.

Route: Skinned up W and N aspects from 3500-6200’. Skied N facing terrain.
Observation Summary: Visible snow being transported on ridge tops. Consistent high winds were noted on ridgetops all day and at all elevations at various points through the day.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: It seemed to stay below freezing except on the valley floor all day, but the solar aspects definitely were catching some rays much of the day.
Instability Comments: We experienced a large collapse while skinning up at 5200’, and several shooting cracks in the same zone. The collapse was an isolated wind slab in sheltered terrain, occurring about 6” deep in the snowpack, failing on facets sitting on a firm crust.

Route: round-the-compass, to sample a wide range of aspects and elevations prior to this week's storm. Only missed NW.
Observation Summary:
Hand pits, slope cuts, and snow profiles (W, 5100'; NE, 6100') showed no troubling failure planes in the near-surface snow.  Weakest near-surface snow in between ice/ crusts on SE. The surface snow was dry on the shady half of the compass in the morning, spongy on high SE and SW. Below about 6000 ft, that shrank to the shadiest third by mid afternoon. Expect some robust crusts tomorrow. On steep, northerly slopes, the surface snow sluffed easily but did not entrain additional snow. 
Images soon
Weather Summary: Few-scattered clouds, calm to light winds, warming temps and some strong solar radiation input

Observation Summary: From our vantage on a shaded northerly aspect, we noted a small number of roller balls on a sunny southern aspect on the opposite side of the drainage. Winds out of the NW with minimal transport in our sheltered trees, but noticeable snow transport on the higher peaks of the Flathead Range.
Weather Summary: Wind blowing from NW throughout the day. Mostly sunny, with southern aspects receiving moderate sunlight. Minimal wind transport at middle elevation bands where we were traveling, but noted active wind loading on higher peaks of Flathead Range.
Instability Comments: No notable instabilities save some isolated cracking around our skies.

Observation Summary:
We traveled to an upper-elevation location to observe the effects of recent windy weather and the current state of our Persistent Slab problem.

Recent winds scoured windward aspects and left a thin breakable slab on cross-loaded slopes above 6000 feet. Top-loaded locations held soft and hard slabs. Slabs cracked beneath a skier, and occasional shooting cracks were observed. Cornices were stiff and difficult to trigger. 
There are up to 12 inches of loose, dry snow in sheltered locations.
Snowpits at mid and upper elevations reveal the mid-January crust down 35 - 65 cm. No propagation was noted in stability tests.
The snow structure at mid-elevations is much weaker than at upper elevations.
The riding quality remained good on easterly aspects down to 4300 feet.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Moderate winds with strong gusts at mid and upper elevations. Partly cloudy conditions limited full warming potential.
Instability Comments: Cracking in recently formed wind slabs.

Route: North & East facing lines off Essex Mountain
Observation Summary: Way better pow skiing was had than we expected. Couldn't stop. We were glad to be on a shady aspect despite the clouds rolling in about 11- the sun this morning was stroooong.
Weather Summary: We witnessed wind loading and snow being transported at higher elevations, like 8,000' +. We climbed to just under 7k and did not experience anything more than a couple moderate gusts all day.
Instability Comments: Minor cracking, but almost entirely between kickturns so to be expected. No shooting cracks or collapses.

Observation Summary: Re-build of the Tunnel Ridge weather station (wind site).

Route: Skinned up SE ridge of sub-Shields and skied the S bowl.
Observation Summary: One of our group members heard a small whumpf while breaking trailing in the low angle S bowl at around 6500 feet. We dug a quick hand pit and found a layer of facets above a crust buried roughly a foot and a half deep. There was a 4 finger plus slab on top of the facets and we were noticing light to moderate NE winds with moderate gusts. We also felt wind coming from the SW and witnessed lots of swirling snow.
Weather Summary: Beautiful sunny day with light to moderate wind from NE and SW. Lenticular clouds hanging out around peaks.
Instability Comments: Stomped on small cornices and windslabs facing NE. Some were more reactive than others, one broke just by skinning next to it. Lot's of shooting cracks while walking and stomping on them.

Observation Summary:
This is the Brown’s Creek basin just north of Peters Ridge; the usual spot for these glide cracks that can be seen clear from Bigfork.
 
The left one had been visible for about two weeks, but I’ve never had a camera handy to send you a photo. 
 
The right one caught me totally by surprise as I had seen no crack there as of yesterday 5pm.
 
I was out and about enough today to scan from this area all the way down to Broken Leg Mountain, and I see no other glide cracks in the usual places - yet.

"Brown frown" that started out small and grew over time.  This morning I observed that a large avalanche had occurred immediately south of the brown frown.  My observation point is approximately 5 miles west of location of avalanche area, and photo is taken with a phone through a telescope.
Weather Summary: Today is CAVU (clear and visibility unrestricted).  The past week has been warmish.
Instability Comments: See photo

Route: Toured up to ghoulie point and traveled east of there
Observation Summary: SE Facing @ 6512 (Upper)Ghoulies side country Total snowpack: 210cmECTN11 @ 195cmECTN29 @ 177cm

Weather Summary: Light winds out of SWMostly cloudy with breaks of sun

Route: Left Whitefish Mountain Resort into side country, skinned up Ghoulie Point then traversed to the saddle
Observation Summary: Ghoulie Point. Elv.6400ft. Aspect: Eastern facing wind-affected slope. Slope Angle: 20-25 Degrees. Performed 4 ECT tests. No propagation on tests 1-3. Propagation on test 4 with hard force. Multiple weak layers in new storm layers from last night, not quite forming a slab yet.

Route: Ascended and descended a N/NE aspect from 3800' to 7000'. Also skied an E facing run from 7000' to 6300'.
Observation Summary: We went out hunting sheltered pow turns, and were rewarded. It was a "look-but-don't-touch" kind of day, as the 30-35 degree entries into the zones we hoped to ski were very wind loaded, with wind slabs on test slopes proving easily reactive. We skied a mellow (15-30 degree) east facing run in a sheltered area and enjoyed soft turns. We skied down the N/NE ridge we ascended. We descended steeper (35-45 degree) terrain below 5000', as we had not observed wind loading or instability at that elevation. The new snow sluffed fairly fast on the recent crust, but was easily manageable. We estimate a widespread 8" of new snow above 5000' fell in this zone. Below 5000' and in the parking area below 4000' there was about 3" of new snow. Graupel was common on the surface and widespread throughout the new snow.We observed what looked to be an avalanche failing on a steep SE facing chute around 6000' off of Essex Mountain, likely from loading within the last 24hrs. Tough to see much from a distance, but it looked to have failed on the old/new snow interface. D1/R2.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: We were thrilled that the winds relatively light and much calmer with gusts more sporadic than forecasted. Winds were generally from the west, with intermittent gusts to moderate and periods of calm/light wind. When the wind was blowing and during gusts, we did observe plenty of blowing snow on the surface. During our ascent in the morning the upper level clouds were moving west to east quite quickly, and there was some observable blowing snow at ridgeline. The sun poked out intermittently throughout the day, and light (S1 or less) snow/graupel occasionally fell. Temperature was 33 degrees at the car at 9am.
Instability Comments: New wind slabs of varying size proved pretty reactive on test slopes. Cracking on these slabs was widespread. Some cracking around ski tracks as well. We observed wind slabs on slopes N, NE, E, and SE.

location: Swan Range South of Swan Lake
Route: Road to north-facing bowl. Skin and ride NE aspect.
Observation Summary:
Into the Swan! Let's see what this storm left for us:
-We found exceptional riding with 4-10 inches of recent snow.  
-We noticed a handful of small, natural soft slab avalanches that failed in wind drifted snow during the last week. 
-We stomped though wind drifted snow with minimal cracking. 
-Cornice were growing and we were able to chunk off the top 30cm with some jumping. 
-We found buried surface hoar 55-90cm deep in our testpits.
-An extended column test at 7200 feet, NE aspect produced a propagating result with moderate force.
We backed away from riding a steep, treed bowl.
Weather Summary: Bursts of graupel
Instability Comments: Cracking was confined to the top 10 cm.  Was able to break some chunks off of cornice ~30 cm thick.

Observation Summary:
Today we were Flathead bound to observe snow totals and see how sensitive freshly formed wind slabs are. We also wanted to assess buried weak layers in the upper snowpack.
- We initiated many shooting cracks and triggered a few very small slabs. We skied a windward aspect and found isolated pockets throughout our descent. 
- Freshly formed drifts were thicker, but stubborn on more wind-loaded aspects. 
- We found weak faceted snow roughly 40 cm and 50 cm down from the surface. This similar structure was found in a number of neighboring drainages. We had nonpropagating results in extended column tests. See snow profile for more info.
We chose to ski steep terrain on windward aspects below 6500 feet. This did require attention to surface texture since winds drifted snow further down the slope than anticipated. 
profile and photos soon.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)
Weather Summary: We could hear the winds overhead most the day, but not as windy as expected. Full trees in motion at low elevations, light winds at mid and upper elevations.
Instability Comments: We triggered small and inconsequential wind slabs. Lots of shootin cracks in pockety wind slabs at middle elevations.

Route: Upper whitefish lake zone, Up and down an east facing aspect from 4500’-7000’
Observation Summary: Some patchy sun through the day. Growing  cornices on the divide. Fun skiing in the sheltered terrain.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Route: Goolies point
Observation Summary: Snow pit tests were conducted around 2pm in Whitefish Mountain Resort side country on a 29° southeast facing slope of Old Man’s bowl at approximately 6700ft of elevation.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Observation Summary:
We bumped our way up the west side road and overheated the machines every 10 minutes. Once we got there, we toured up to assess a month-old layer of buried surface hoar and look for signs of recent avalanche activity.
- We observed recent natural avalanches on steep north-facing slopes. These were likely wind slabs. Crown depths appeared to average 1 to 2 feet thick.
- We dug a pit on a north aspect at 6900 feet and found a buried surface hoar. We did not get this layer to propagate in extended column tests. A propagation saw resulted in an unstable result (PST40/100End). Although considered unstable, this weak layer has gained strength. 
- We found hard slabs of drifted snow above 7000 feet. These were hollow feeling and roughly a foot thick. 
With recent avalanche activity, we chose to ski on slopes sheltered from the wind and less than 35 degrees.
Weather Summary: Generally light winds. Precipitation started to pick up during our exit at around 1500.
Instability Comments: Observed 5 crowns and/or debris piles from avalanches from the past few days.

Route: Skinned up from shooting range on J-Basin Rd up to summit of Aeneas, skied NW Face to picnic lake, skinned up Noisy Notch to top of Crown Bowl, skied bowl, skinned up to Checkerboard Summit, skied Checkerboard and back to truck.
Observation Summary: Obvious signs of recent and active wind loading with an evident  windslab on pockets of our tour.
Weather Summary: Once on the ridge leading to Aeneas summit there was fairly consistent 25-35 mph winds with active windloading observed. Temps were warm down low (high 30s) and cooled off significantly around 5500ft, unknown temp.
Instability Comments: Cracking was initiated on purpose with a test slope above the skin track. Was a pocket of windslab roughly 10cm thick.

Observation Summary: •	No recent avalanche activity observed.•	Poor snowpack structure predominantly above 5000’ with weak facets above/below the mid-Jan. crust and a building slab on top. This slab is now 1-2’ thick.•	Some facets noted on top the late-Dec. crust as well, but these layers were harder and rounding.•	Despite the poor structure, no signs of instability observed, save for some minor cracking underfoot. We could not release any slabs on small, steep test slopes, nor get any shooting cracks.•	Moderate to strong S-SW winds, predominantly above 5000’ elevation were actively loading slopes just below ridgelines on northerly and easterly aspects.•	We stayed down in the middle of the basin and limited our terrain choices to slopes most sheltered from the wind and lower than 35°. Avoided consequential slopes that were steeper than this and had rock outcrops and/or unsupported slopes above cliff bands.
Weather Summary: •	The morning had broken skies with the occasional sunshine. By noon skies were overcast and by 1400 S-1 to S1 snowfall with elevations above 5500’ favored.•	1” of new snowfall above 5000’ on our exit and a trace on the Canyon floor.•	Daytime highs reached the mid-30s °F on the Canyon floor and the low to mid-20s °F above 6000’ elevation.

Observation Summary:
We toured east of WMR to check on the surface snow conditions before the incoming storm.

The biggest takeaway from today was that the recent (8 to 13 inches) of soft snow rests upon a crust. The density change between the new and crust is a clear sliding surface. New snowfall from Tuesday night's storm may be consolidated enough to act as a slab upon this crust.
Below the crust, the snowpack consisted of facets layers with varying types of crust.  We dug into the snow on various sheltered northern slopes, but we did not find the buried surface hoar found on 2/5. 
We did not get propagation in our ECT. 
The impact of many riders on WMR's sidecountry slopes proved to be a unique "micro-climate" of compaction layers observed within the snowpack. Sking on stiffened drifts with variable soft snow layers had us retreating back to the resort. Yes, skiing within the resort was better than the sidecountry.
Returning to the parking lot, the temp was 33 degrees. During the drive down, the snow turned to rain around 4000 feet.
Weather Summary: Moderate winds at the minimal time spent in upper elevations. For most of the day, winds were light at mid-elevations.  Visibility was poor, with obscured skies. Snowfall was light before noon and transitioned to S-1, with bursts of S1.
Instability Comments: On steep slopes, there was some harmless sluffing of the top 3 to 4 inches.

Observation Summary: Still quite windy at elevation here but we managed to find good snow on east and northeast aspects. Two types of snow pack: 4-6” inch on unreactive wind board or 12-14” on the old rain crust. Mild surface sluffing otherwise no instability.

Today we traveled to the site of the 2/5/2023 avalanche above Skiumah Lake in the Flathead Range.  
We are grateful that no one was seriously hurt and that the reporting party shared information to help the greater snow community.

The Skiumah Basin had evidence of many generations of avalanche debris that were present on all observed slopes.
Winds were moderate above 5,000 feet. The 1" to 2" of new snow was redistributed into small drifts over a supportable crust.
The D1 avalanche occurred in an isolated, cross-loaded chute that was actively being reloaded by swirling, localized winds within the chute.
The avalanche was a 1-finger-hard wind slab, varying in slab depth from 20 to 35cms. It failed on the interface of new snow over a recent knife-hard crust.
This avalanche was an example of a relatively small slide that had amplified consequences due to the cliff band below.
The slope angle of the crown was observed to be 38 degrees. 
As my partner and I traveled on the bed surface, we noted that the underlying crust was intermittently blue ice. 
As we cut a small column test, the slab failed upon isolation. Due to the active re-loading and a shooting crack on a small adjacent concave slope, we heeded the snowpack's warning and stayed in an area of safety.
We did not observe any other signs of instability outside of the isolated cross-loaded chute.
Skiing was punchy with a wide variety of wind-affected snow surfaces.
Weather Summary: Overcast skies
Instability Comments: Besides the D1 avalanche, we did not observe other signs of instability.

Route: Trail into Skiumah and climbed towards the Western side of the main bowl to the saddle skier right
Observation Summary: Lots of small cliffs and trees in the area. Steeper area than was thought from down below. Lots of released avalanches in area. Lots of steep areas surrounding Skiumah lower lake.Lower clouds and could not see the top of the mountains and ridges.

Temps in the low 30S.
Where the avalanche was trigger was on a hump and stomped on a few times as skier tried to make his way over to the saddle.

Route: Ghoulies/half moon gully
Observation Summary: Light s1 and fog. What else is new?
Instability Comments: Saw D1 sized debris in half moon as I was cutting across towards slick slot.. doubt it was natural..

Observation Summary:
