Today we went to the northern Whitefish Range to assess the snowpack structure and visit the area around the Ninko Cabin. We hoped to get some info on wind-slab sensitivity, but this appeared to be a relatively protected area. I found this surprising since Hornett (a weather station nearby) was pumping averages of 25 mph for two consecutive days. 
- Recent and overnight snow totals increased in depth above 6500 feet. We found 3 to 4 inches of graupel above 4 to 6 inches of stellar dendrites. This made for springy pow skiing on our descent.
- Upper snowpack structure above roughly 6200 feet is poor. We found a layer of near-surface facets about 12 to 16 inches below the surface. In some areas, this layer was capped with a thin layer of surface hoar.
- We observed no avalanche activity, no red flags, and had non-propagating scores in snowpit tests. 
- The snowpack is shallow and weak. We probed a few areas and found average depths of 130 to 150 cm between 7000 and 7500 feet. See snowpit for more info on snowpack.
With the lack of red flags and no evidence of wind-drifted snow, we felt comfortable skiing a planar slope at 35 degrees.
Weather Summary: Cloud cover rolled in around noon along with light precipitation. Precipitation rates started to pick up (S1) during our exit around 1530.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed.

Route: Dug on W facing slope
Observation Summary: Great skiing on sheltered slopes. Structure is a bit disconcerting but could get nothing to move on various test slopes. Many clean shears in hand pits on all aspects. All popping on the MLK crust. We chose to stick to slopes below 35°. Had a break in the fog and saw older WL in the Skook Chutes, unsure how old they were, seemed to have ran in the last few days warm up.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Rime growing on rime.
Instability Comments: Localized cracking around skis

Observation Summary: We observed 15-30 cm of nice powder sitting on top of a hard crust. Great riding. Zero wind affected snow observed.

Route: Toured up the Tunnel ridge west of Pinnacle creek east of Tunnel looking for higher elevation terrain where it didn't warm up so much.
Observation Summary: Looked like there was debris from a small slide at the top of the Tunnel Ridge high point.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Warm and windy gloppy up to about 4500-5000 and a little better but quickly warming higher than that. Started colder and light but was heavy by the time we got down.
Instability Comments: Cracking on cross-loaded aspects of the ridge

Observation Summary: SS-ASc-R1/D.5-I cornice kicking/ski cuts breaking 6-12 inches and running too to mid path.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed

Observation Summary: Traveled to a northerly, mid-elevation basin and stayed well below ridgetops to try and avoid wind-affected slopes. However, we were surprised with swirling winds that seemed to find their way into every opening between trees. The wind was blustery and strong enough to see swaying trees.Around 5000 ft there was a gradual transition from moist/glommy to dry snow. Observed 2-3mm graupel on the surface from the trailhead up to highest point of travel at 6000 ft. In areas untouched by the wind, there was approx 3-5 inches of graupel. There were a few nice soft turns to be had!Steep convexities that seemed to have soft wind slabs were not reactive.Sky was broken and light graupel fell consistently throughout the day
We observed all the wind things: wind slabs, wind boards, wind scour, wind gusts, wind transport, and wind chimes. Even in fairly protected areas, most trees had lost their snow.
Weather Summary: Mostly overcast. Some groppel, some snowfall, and something that resembled freezing rain. Some winds strong enough to move trees and lead them to drop their bombs. Sticky snow downlow. 39 degrees at the truck.
Instability Comments: We did observe recent cornice fall on Western aspects that had left debris but not much more in its wake.

Observation Summary:
We traveled to the east end of John F. Stevens Canyon to observe all things related to wind.

In the morning, we noted wind transport at all elevations, which diminished throughout the day as the winds tapered.
Large sections of terrain open to the wind had been affected by recent wind. The snow surface was capped by a 5 cm thick slab that was 1F hard. This layer cracked frequently, but no shooting cracks were observed.
New cornice growth was soft and deep and failed under the weight of a skier. Sections of cornice that failed did not exceed the length of my skis. Cornices seem to have gone through a recent growth spurt.
There was 5 cm of graupel which fell overnight at 4500 feet. This increased to 8 cm above 6000 feet.
We located the mid-January faceted crust responsible for our current Persistent Slab problem. This layer was 45 cm down at 6740 feet but did not propagate in large column tests.
Weather Summary: Intermittent graupel squalls and rain showers on the valley floor. Pure graupel showers above 5000'. Moderate winds in the morning diminished throughout the day.
Instability Comments: Wind-affected snow had a 5 cm thick surface layer that was 1F hard. This layer cracked under the weight of a skier. Fresh cornices broke easily from the weight of a skier.

Observation Summary: Intermittent snow and graupel squalls and gusting winds out is the west. Found somewhat reactive wind slabs on due north slope and unreactive wind slab on NW aspect. Best skiing  on NE sheltered aspect. Roller balls started around 5k on the exit ski

Route: Up n down
Observation Summary: Very variable snow conditions that made for a funtastic day of skiing.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Sun in the morning was fortunately covered by clouds around noon and then the winds that eat the snow launched a full scale assault on the western front of the mountain range named after the white birds..if you know what I mean
Instability Comments: Lots of glide cracks and an old loose dry from maybe …Wednesday in spied her bowl

Route: Skinned up the west slope of the Swan. Skied the skiers left of Crown. Traversed up through picnic notch and skied down to picnic lake.  Skied back down picnic notch and cross courted over to checkerboard.
Observation Summary: Wind. Lots of wind and the effects of wind. Also glide cracks on a south facing slope, kind of the opposite side of Spider. And another glide crack in the bowl north of Peters Ridge. Sensitive cornices.
Weather Summary: Some blue sky in the morning. Wind, all day.
Instability Comments: Wind slab, dry snow sluffs, glide cracks

Route: Skinned up WMR and over to Ghoulies. Skied a south facing run, a north facing run, and then a west facing run in to the SW facing drainage east of ghoulie ridge. Tour went between 4400' and 6700'.
Observation Summary: -I toured up in sunshine on a ski trail up to 5800', then left the ski boundary. Between 5800' and 6700' I observed surface hoar growth on the surface of the snowpack, isolated to sheltered areas on W, NW, N, NE slopes.-Snow quality remained excellent above 5500' before my decent around 2pm, still fairly cohesionless considering the sun and warmth. There was some minor wind loading on N through SE aspects.-I saw two very small, recent slides on a S face around 5800' and 5500'. One (5800') was a tiny wind slab that looked to be about 3 inches deep from my vantage point, and ran about 30 feet, not entraining much snow. It looks to have failed on a skin track (see photo), so not sure if intentional or unintentional. By itself it's innocuous, but suggests that larger wind slabs on that aspect may also be easily reactive. The second (5500') was an easily predictable small wet loose slide alongside a cliff band that had been baking in the sun/warmth today. It ran about 80 feet and entrained some snow (see photo).
Weather Summary: High clouds that were few at first gradually covered more of the sky as the afternoon went on, although it was generally sunny. Temp at car at 4400' at 11:00 was 26F. Back at the car at 4:30 it was 36F. Wind was calm to light and variable.

Observation Summary: Significant warming and Crust forming on solar aspects up to 6200'.
Weather Summary: Very strong westerly winds, blowing snow, warming temps. Light snow began 4pm, light rain below 4200' continued all the way down highway 2 to West Glacier
Instability Comments: Serious wind slabs forming along the lake. Isolated cracking on wind slabs

Route: Accessed via WMR resort. Then skinned to Big Treed and made three laps.
Observation Summary: Only observed wind formations on the ridge from WMR to Big Trees.
Instability Comments: No instability observed.

Observation Summary:
We traveled to the southern Whitefish Range to observe surface conditions and wind transport before an onslaught of users this weekend.

 Moderate southwest winds affected large areas open to the wind above 6000'. Wind transport formed fresh cornices and wind slabs that were reactive to the weight of a skier.
Thursday's warming left a 2 cm thick sun crust on steep southerly slopes at mid-elevations and minor fields of rollerballs.
Friday's warming was most prevalent at low and mid-elevations. Cool air temperatures and wind kept the snow surface cool above 6000'. 
Recent low-density snow varied between 20 cm deep on steep southerly slopes to 30 cm in sheltered locations.
Riding conditions remain good, with no instabilities noted in terrain sheltered from the wind and sun.
Weather Summary: Mostly clear skies in the morning gave way to partly cloudy conditions in the afternoon. Blustery southwest winds transported snow throughout the day.
Instability Comments: We observed cracking in freshly formed wind drifts and cornices. New cornice formation failed from the weight of a skier.

Observation Summary: Riding was good on all aspects down to 5400.  Snow surface on south and west aspects became moist from about 6500 down from solar radiation, developing a crust once shaded. The wind and cool temps helped preserve snow quality at higher elevations.
Weather Summary: 18 in the am at the car, 26 upon return.  Clear skies in the am with increasing high clouds from about 1:30 on.  Variable winds in the am, increasing to moderate with strong gust in the afternoon from the west. The Great Bear Wilderness Divide looked to have strong winds with moderate amounts of snow transport.
Instability Comments:
Isolated developing soft slabs as a result of the increasing winds through out the day.

Surface cracking in the top 2-4", location dependent.

Route: We skinned up the south-facing slope to the west ridge of Snowslip (thanks to whoever set the tracks!), dropped into the southeast basin below the peak, then skied down the southwest slope below the summit.
Observation Summary: - Wind-affected snow on all slope aspects- Crossed soft wind slab pockets on the way up and harder wind slabs near and on ridgelines- Skied great snow on south and southeast aspects from higher to mid-elevations- Encountered a sun-affected zipper crust on a south aspect at lower elevations in the afternoon - Observed active wind transport of snow, most intense on the ridgelines- Saw Roller balls (various sizes ranging from the size of a baseball to larger than a basketball) starting from trees and the base of rock features on south aspects in the afternoon
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking around our skis on wind slab pockets

Route: Skied East and N/NW aspects.
Observation Summary: The snow surfaces we traveled on were dry upon our exit around 3pm.
Instability Comments: Small Loose Dry on convexity, East aspect at 7k. Rider not caught

Route: Camas Rd. to Schwack City to "Rubideau."
Observation Summary: - Strongggg solar input on south-facing slopes from 10 a.m. until I bailed at 1, dripping trees, roller balls, etc. ... another fun layer to look forward to!- Westerly 15 mph winds at the ridge top transporting some snow onto east and south faces- Not nearly as much snow as I expected ... it's lowww tide up to 5,400 ft. in the Apgars ... like, even lower than usual for this time of year :/
Instability Comments: None.

Route: Ascend/descend south-facing slopes.
Observation Summary:
What a day to be out in the mountains. We enjoyed the sunshine, though it did make for a stick/crusty descent. 
-Large column tests produced propagating results with moderate force in our test pit.
-Hand pits during our ascent revealed recent and wind-drifted snow on top of a layer of facets above the mid-January crust. 
-Winds increased to moderate from the West above 6800'. 
-We saw light to moderate wind transport in our vicinity, but noticed impressive plumes of snow extending from peaks in the Great Bear Wilderness
-Found one small natural soft slab avalanche that likely failed yesterday. 
-Noted debris from two small loose wet avalanches that slid during this afternoon's heat. 
-Observed isolated cracking in recently wind-drifted snow. 
We avoided convex roll-overs on our descent.  Variable surface conditions made for defensive skiing.
Weather Summary: Beautiful day with sun and warm temps.  High cloud began moving in mid-afternoon.
Instability Comments: Cracking in windrifted snow 4-6 inches deep.

Observation Summary: Wind sheltered terrain held plenty of soft dry snow (20-30cm) but it’s beginning to settle. Open bowls and higher terrain adjacent to ridgelines were heavily wind drifted and winds mid day were blowing 20-30 mph on higher (6800 ft) ridge tops. Interestingly, in some of the higher south facing terrain the recent dry snow had been blown into cross loaded formations, revealing harder wind slabs, presumably from the last NE wind event. Lots of deep drifts on ridgelines. We observed no cracking or movement of snow (besides dry sluffs) where we rode but did not feel confident riding wind affected open terrain. One of the NE facing bowls had experienced several fresh cornice fall avalanches but the slides appeared to have been limited to cornice debris and recent storm snow. (See photos) Super fun day to be out with all the sun and powder.

We went into the Flathead Range to track storm snow totals and the sensitivity of wind slabs and loose dry avalanches.
- Storm totals were roughly 6 to 9 inches of snow. There were 3 inches of snow on yesterday's skin track (Tuesday). 
- We triggered small loose dry and soft wind slab avalanches. These were all harmless in size due to the terrain we were in. The wind slabs we triggered were 6 to 8 inches thick and propagated roughly 30 feet. These were on slopes with small amounts of wind loading. 
- Observed active wind transport along the crest of the flathead. Winds were out of the south and west. 
- There is 16 to 20 inches of low-density snow sitting on a slick crust on solar aspects. 
We selected south-facing aspects to avoid freshly formed wind slabs. We managed sluff by riding slopes less than 40 degrees. Overall the skiing exceeded expectations. 
photos/video soon
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Observed active transport along the crest of the Flathead Range. Winds gusting into the moderate category.
Instability Comments: Cracking anywhere winds stiffened the snow surface. Triggered small wind slabs along a lightly wind-loaded ridgeline. Observed a large (D2) crown on Nyack from the road.

Route: Up from the Skiumah Trail and back down through the woods (and bush!)
Observation Summary: Challenging skinning conditions but pretty good skiing conditions up high. Still quite bushy down low.
Weather Summary: Calm winds and S-1 to S1 precip during our brief outing.
Instability Comments: Instability was confined to the recent surface snow (Loose Dry) sluffing on old crusts and was easily skier (or treebomb) triggered. More sluffing and longer running higher up and on steep slopes.

Route: Ascend southern ridge to subpeak and traverse to summit.
Observation Summary:
We traveled into the Swan Range to verify recent snow totals and see what effect the wind has had.
**We found buried surface hoar 1 meter below the surface on a S-SE aspect at 6800 feet.  This particular spot was not an area I expected surface hoar to survive.  It isn't particularly well-preserved at this point, but its stil there. 
-Small and large column tests produced fractures (no propagation) with moderate and hard force at this location.  
- We found roughly 10 inches of new snow at upper eleveations. This snow fell on old wind board, wind slab, and-in sheltered areas-dry, unconsolidated snow. 
-Old wind slabs were masked by the new snow.  We were able to identify their locations by digging down in areas where our ski penetration decreased.
-Old wind slabs were generally stubborn to non-reactive.  They were hard (1F) and 2-8 inches thick in this location.
-Fresh drifts of snow were up to 2 feet deep.  These drifts were soft and produced minimal cracking.  They did, however, slow our skinning/skiing.  
-In seltered terrain we saw minimal cracking in the top 3-4 inches of the new snow. 
With this information we chose to ski Southeast aspect with slopes generally 30-35 degrees (but some spots exceding 35 degrees). We did travel down the ridge a ways before dropping in to hopefully avoid recently drifted snow.  We still found pockets that cracked 1 foot deep and extended up to 10 feet but did not slide.  We rode one at a time and kept eyes on eachother.
Weather Summary: Wind began calm, increasing to light above 6500' and gusting to moderate above 7000' with light blowing snow.
Instability Comments: Collapsing occured when traveling over the edge of old windslabs buried beneath the new snow.  Cracking was minimal where the new snow has been stiffened by wind.

Route: Toured from 3300' to 6600' in the Spider area for a couple of powder laps on a W aspect.
Observation Summary: Enjoyed a great powder day with a plan to avoid the newer thin wind slabs on NW-NE-SE aspects, as well as the surface hoar weak layer potentially lurking on those same aspects. There was about 2-4" of new, cold, dry snow at the trailhead at 3300' at 9am, and snow fell constantly throughout the day. At ridgeline, and below on a SW-W face we encountered old wind slabs from last week's storm with NE winds. Despite some huge whalebacks, all wind slabs we tested proved to be very stiff and very unreactive. In areas sheltered from the wind we enjoyed 18-24" of new snow from the last couple of storms. All the superlatives for fun powder skiing apply here. Snow did stuff quite fast and far (mostly at the surface) in steeper terrain. SE-SW winds were generally light throughout the day, although gusting moderate in the afternoon -- we did note snow had blown off of the windward side of some trees. There is plenty of snow available for transport if winds do pick up.
Weather Summary: Temperature at trailhead at 9:00am was 14 degrees with little to no wind. The snow fell constantly throughout the day, mostly S1-S2, with brief periods of heavier (estimate up to S4-5, quickly accumulating on hat brims and backpacks) snow. Winds were generally light, from the SE in the morning switching to SW in the afternoon, gusting moderate as the afternoon went on. We noted some new snow had blown off the trees on the windward side on our final descent. Upon returning to the trailhead, about 2" of snow had accumulated on the car. We estimate between 6-10" fell at upper elevations since last night.
Instability Comments: Older wind slabs caused by last week's stormy NE winds were very stiff and very stubborn. 18+" of new cohesionless snow sluffed fairly fast and far on steeper (35-45 degree) terrain, but only superficially.

Route: Took sleds to the end of the road, skinned up a southeast aspect to the ridge, and skied powder down.
Observation Summary:
We were in the Swan Range today, on the east side of the Swan Crest, in the Lost Johnny drainage.

Today we skied the best powder I've skied all season. The southern-facing slopes we descended were protected from the recent winds of this past weekend's storm.
Our first snow profile at 6100 feet, was on a southeast aspect and had the multiple crusts we've been tracking (Christmas,  MLK, and 1/18 rime-crust) with 8 inches of new soft snow resting above. We did not get slab propagation in our ECT (see the snowpack section for details).
Along the ridge, we observed older, stubborn cornices from typical southwest winds, with pockets of fresh wind drifts from recent northerly winds.
Our second snow profile at 6800 feet was northwest facing and also had the three identified crusts. We got propagation with hard force (ECTP30) above a crust 90cm below the surface. We found scattered pieces of surface hoar crystals mixed with facets within this weak layer.
The thin wind slabs we did observe in upper elevations were at most 1 foot thick.  Besides some isolated cracking around our boards, we did not observe any significant signs of instability on the slopes we traveled.
The bumpy sled ride into and out of Lost Johnny was worth the 8 to 11 inches of sheltered powder.
Weather Summary: A little sunshine in the morning and then overcast most of the day. Cold temperatures to start, with warmer temperatures (10 degrees) by 1400. Winds were calm, except for a light breeze around 1530.
Instability Comments: Minimal, isolated cracking was observed around our boards at upper elevations.

Route: We started on Jewel Basin Road, skinned up the FS5392 to then hike up the Switchback trailhead.  Got up to 5,400 ft. Were we saw signs of severe wind loading and instability. We aborted and climbed up to checkered board via camp misery on the second attempt. All aspects we tested were mid/low elevations on SE/SW aspects.
Observation Summary: Cracks on the snow. Unpredictable snow pack. Large snow pillows.
Weather Summary: Very cold, clear day. No wind.
Instability Comments: We arrived to an area exposed by Friday/Saturday were the snow started to crack with load.

Observation Summary: What’s harder: skiing inbounds and losing your toes for hours at a time or touring in the cold and trying to sort out the right blend of layers?We opted for the latter. Clear skies and cold. Ridgetops showed signs of wind, but S aspects held the goods below where the wind did its work. Sloughing of new snow was evident in shallow natural releases in the Skook shoots, and we kicked some slough down steeper angles on Dorothy.

Route: Found warmth and good skiing in the sun today. S facing slopes were the ticket with this weekends storm.
Observation Summary: The storm was definitely a dud at lower elevations,  but surprisingly impressive up high. The winds did not effect S facing slopes where we were. Up to 10 inches of new snow from Friday's storm.
Weather Summary: No windBlue skies on the continental divide
Instability Comments:
40 cms of loose snow sitting on top of MLK crust at upper elevations. 1 meter of snow on top of Christmas crust.
Dug pit at 6600' SW facing slope
Ectn16 40 cms down
No other results
Heard 1 whumpf entering drainage above creek.
No cracking, good bonding of storm snow on S facing slope.
Unintentionally Triggered one avalanche at 5800' on descent at the bottom of the run. It was storm snow, loose, dry avalanche. 60 ' wide and ran 300'. Not big enough to bury a person.

Route: Skinned the road up to checkerboard and summited peak 6437.
Observation Summary: At 5500' near the NW ridge of checkerboard we experienced shooting cracks and thick wind slabs on the west side of the ridge, indicating the snow transport likely occured recently when the wind was coming from the NE. Evidence of wind transport was widespread with slabs, scoured nodules, and cornices visible throughout the day at all elevations and aspects, even deep in the trees. While at lower elevations, slabs we're observed primarily on NW-S aspects, at higher elevations large cornices and slabs were observed on primarily NE aspects, indicating the wind transport at these elevations was older, from a few days ago when winds were from the SSW. Perhaps the upper elevations had less snow to transport by the time yesterday's gusts came through.The slabs within the forest were very sporadic and varied a lot locally. We dug two pits 15 feet from one another. The top 4 inches of wind slab popped off as soon as we cut it with the cord on one ECT, where the other pit 15' away was ECTX with no wind slab at all.Overall the ski was cold and a bit chunky coming down with thin slabs everywhere, very little powdery snow on top without wind alteration. Really nice day overall.
Instability Comments: Experienced localized shooting cracks in wind drifted snow. Cracks never extended beyond ~10 feet.

Observation Summary:
Sunshine! Today we took a trip into the Jewel to assess wind slabs and quickly dig around for buried weak layers. 
- We observed very little recent avalanche activity. We suspect that most natural activity from the storm quickly got covered with drifting snow. 
- It is hard to pinpoint the distribution of wind-drifted snow. We found slabs on all aspects above 5000 feet. The softest snow we found was on a south aspect. 
- A weak layer of buried surface hoar standing upright on north-facing aspects. It is buried between 70 to 90 cm at upper elevations. 
- We triggered small slabs of drifted snow and one loose dry avalanche. These were all small due to the small (putt putt golf) kind of terrain we were on. 
The skiing was honestly mediocre due to the stiff, grabby wind slabs everywhere. I say mediocre, but also hilarious due to the numerous wipeouts it caused. We were extra mindful of the terrain we traveled in once we switched to northerly aspects. A persistent weak layer at 70 to 90 cm isn't something we cared to tango with.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)
Weather Summary: Cold, clear day with calm winds.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking around skis. Drummy sounding pillows. Hollow feeling slabs. Human triggered wind slabs (small). All signs of drifting are present. Winds drifted snow on almost every aspect and all elevations.

Route: Up Rescue Crk to 6600'
Observation Summary: Warmer, windier, and less accumulated snow than expected. 15F at the hwy with just a dusting of new snow on top of ice and hardpack. Higher up the valley most of the fresh snow appeared to be in the form of wind slab.
Instability Comments: Soft 2-6" thick windslab was widespread on North and East facing aspects. Observed some soft D1 slide debris on steep slopes, possibly from 1/27.

Route: Summer trail
Observation Summary: Dense wind drifts on ridge lines.. stayed away from anything over 30 deg..could have been trouble if you ended up on a west/lee aspect unexpectedly.
Weather Summary: Polar air plunging down from the north
Instability Comments: Skinning on mellow ridge line thru dense, hard wind drifts let out a couple good woomphs

Route: Road observations from Hwy 2.
Observation Summary: Two new avalanches that likely ran this morning observed from Hwy 2.The Jan. 26-28 storm was a dud here in JFS Canyon. Canyon floor received 2-3” snowfall with 0.30” water. HST totals at Shed 11 were 10” (suspect some wind-loading) and 4” and Shed 7.Arctic air pushed around 0100 last night with increasing easterly winds and moderate amounts of blowing/drifting snow. Some serious near-surface faceting going on now in the upper 5” of the snowpack.
Weather Summary: •	Afternoon had partly sunny skies with no precipitation and light to moderate E winds on the Canyon floor and moderate N-NE winds above 6000’.•	Daytime highs only reaching single-digits °F on the Canyon floor and negative single-digits °F above 6000’ elevation.

Route: Up Essex Creek and into the bush!
Observation Summary: We observed no natural avalanches, no collapsing, and no shooting cracks. We did trigger a variety of inconsequential small avalanches on steep test slopes while descending.We did not find any buried surface hoar despite poking in the snow pretty regularly, but were confined generally to N/NE aspects during our brief tour.
Weather Summary: Temps started at 15*F @ 9am and cooled to 10*F by 2pm in Essex. Winds were generally light and from the NW shifting to NE by our exit - but we were well protected in the woods. Skies cleared up by noon and made for a lovely albeit brisk day!
Instability Comments: See avalanches description - we triggered a variety of very small inconsequential storm slab/loose dry avalanches.

Observation Summary: Due to life instabilities including a sick toddler and a sick dog, two members of the Dickey Cr Powder Party Posse found themselves still at home yesterday. The remaining two managed to arrive sans either of the two snowmobiles they planned to bring. It started chilly, and just got colder. We traveled on N and NW aspects. The more open the terrain, the more we saw evidence of wind affect, but this mostly manifested as thick (1” or less) wind skins on otherwise dry snow. Wind was moving snow into our skinner between laps. We saw sloughing on steep, sheltered slopes. Winds were light to moderate, higher on the ridgelines. Sky overcast to clear as the day went on.

Route: S facing runs outta Essex town.
Observation Summary: At trailhead 0900 it was snowing hard with big wet flakes. We found about 2 inches of overnight snow to start our tour. The storm came and went through the day. Mostly lighty snowing, light winds except for Ridgeline @ 7200' was gusting to moderate. 7 new inches of pow up high by the end of the day. Good day to be skiing S facing runs, with surprisingly little wind affect top to bottom (until tonight....).
Weather Summary: Not a huge storm.Good enough to be skiing fun pow without signs of instabilities.
Instability Comments:
Did not notice any cracking or whumpfing.
1" thick storm slab forming on Ridgeline at 7200'.
Below 5500 the loose snow was sluffing on crust.

Observation Summary: Storm day skiing in sheltered, mid elevation terrain, in large part to avoid exposure to developing new snow hazards. No signs of wind drifting in this terrain. Wet day to start, with outer layers soaked first climb. Snowfall intensity diminished early afternoon and dried out. New snow loose with few signs of storm slab development in the terrain we chose. Loose snow sluffs a hazard on very steep slopes.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Silver-dollar sized flakes early AM, then most of the day. High intensity (S5 mid morning) with snow piling up on packs and jackets in a few minutes. Some breaks late morning-early afternoon, then steady snow (S1-2). Light winds at ridgeline (6400 ft) but no apparent drifting.
Instability Comments: Some short cracking at switchback corners and in turns. Seemed more common mid afternoon, after snow settled some.

Route: Went to get a fun times tour in pre-storm. Went from the trailhead at 3800' up Marion Creek to the ridge at 6800' and skied a south facing shot, then went back up and skied a northern aspect out Dickey Creek.
Observation Summary: Went out to see what the snow was like before tonight's storm. Small snowfall events from the last couple of days left us with a (wet) dust on (stout) crust down low. This new snow was fairly wet and sticky below 5800' on south aspects and 5300' on north, leading to some glopping on skins and less-than-ideal skating conditions on the exit. Above those elevations however we enjoyed great soft turns with 3-6" of new dry snow. We observed some blowing snow from gusty variable winds on ridge line, potentially creating some new small slabs about to be buried by tonight/tomorrow's snow event.
Weather Summary: From 9:00-11:00 there was occasional snowfall ranging from S-1 - S2 intensity. There were occasional breaks of sun, but mostly overcast. The wind was gusty and variable, with periods of calm followed by airborne rime chunks to the face. The wind became a more consistent westerly flow as the afternoon wore on, mostly moderate winds. Around 14:30 the cloud ceiling lowered to around 7000'.
Instability Comments:
Our main concern with instability was wind loading. We did find wind slabs, particularly near the top of our north facing line with a fair amount of cross loading below the ridge line. I jumped on small wind slabs on SE, N, and NE slopes between 6500-6800' -- most were stubborn and did not fail. Two of the slabs on test slopes on N and NE aspects showed minor cracking, and one released a small slab that ran thirty feet or so below my skis. (see photo)

We also discussed the day's potential for small wet loose sluffs and avalanches down low. Our observation of this hazard was limited to skier-triggered roller balls on our exit.

We observed one large old avalanche in a south facing gully across the Dickey drainage from repeater ridge. We were not close enough for good photos or great detail, but it appeared to be a wet slab avalanche that ran over a thousand feet, likely from the Xmas week rain event.

Route: Up Disbrow creek
Observation Summary: Gusting winds transporting snow and light snow throughout the day. Warming below 5,000’ glopping snow.
Weather Summary: Gusting winds transporting snow and light snow throughout the day. Warming below 5,000’ glopping snow.

Observation Summary:
Today we went into upper elevations to look for buried weak layers on wind-sheltered slopes. We also wanted to look at what surfaces new snow will fall on with incoming precipitation.
- Could not find buried surface hoar at any elevation on north, east, or west aspects above 7k in this area. We found a 2-inch hard wind board with a crust capping it. 
- We had non-propagating results on extended column tests above the MLK crust. 
- We initially descended onto a south-facing slope at 7600 feet and found an incredibly rotten snowpack structure. Large depth hoar grains with a stiff slab above them. We tucked in below a rock and decided to boot out. 
We found the snowpack structure generally stable on north, east, and west-facing aspects. We felt comfortable skiing steep west-facing terrain after observing no signs of buried weak layers in the upper snowpack. A hard and supportable crust in the upper snowpack also added confidence to our decisions.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Cloud deck was at roughly 7000 feet. Obscured above that.

Route: Up the groomed trail until grooming stops, then skin up to the lookout.
Weather Summary: Gorgeous day.  Sunny and mild temps.  Solar aspects were slightly tacky feeling in the afternoon
Instability Comments: No instabilities that we noticed today.

Observation Summary: Minimal evidence of precious days wind transport on west aspect. Very small amount of surface hoar present. Overall found about 15-25 cm of snow sitting on top of MLK crust. No sluffing during skiing. Benign weather. One pit at 6500’. Depth of 180 cm, ectx

A couple of fresh inches (5cm Canadian) of very low density snow had fallen overnight, adding to the pretty decent snowpack near Red Meadow pass. Wind slabs seemed to be the most obvious avalanche problem of the day, which was managed easily by avoiding wind-loaded slopes. The other primary issue of the day was the well-known low viz combo pack of fog-cloud obscura and flat light. We managed this by skiing short pitches and keeping one another in view, especially near tree well hazards and other forms of uncertainty (such as, where is the edge of this ridge, bro?).
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Cloudy.Temps were colder than they’ve been (20F at the TH) and the air had a bit of a bite to it. Guy’s Marias Pass winds were easily found in the Whitefish Range today: winds were moving trees and definitely strong enough to transport snow. At least 1cm of rime had returned to the weather station overnight. That place got more rimes than Phyllis Diller.
Instability Comments: Cornice.

Observation Summary:
I took a quick trip to the southern Whitefish Range to familiarize myself with middle elevations.
- Observed no signs of instability below 6500 feet. 
- Found a layer of faceted snow in hand pits roughly 6 to 8 inches below the surface. This layer is roughly 4 inches below the MLK crust.
Being that the terrain was nearly bumped out and no signs of instability, we felt very comfortable skiing in steep terrain spaced out.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: In and out of obscured and overcast. Light precip throughout the day with short periods of snow showers.

Route: Took the logging road behind Challenge cabin.  Then skinned up the ridge to the West.
Observation Summary:
We took the highway and had a look at the Skyland country today.
-We had exceptional sking, and felt comfortable stepping into bigger terrain.
-No cracking, whoomphing or sluffing was observed.
-Besides the MLK and Dec crusts the snowpack is very upright.
-The MLK crust was capped by 5 inches of light snow that is very much available for wind transport.  Below this crust is an inch of small facets and below that is a layer of well preserved buried surface hoar.  
-Suprise of the day was no wind!  A nice treat for being in the Marias Pass country.
Weather Summary: No wind!  Overcast and snowing lightly all day.
Instability Comments: No instabilities in our pits or that we noticed.

Route: Summer trail into the basin, up to the saddle to our high point of 6800 feet.
Observation Summary:
Glacier's 2023 winter park intern joined me today in the Flathead Range to sample the varied surface conditions 

Low elevations consisted of stout crust that made for some challenging skinning.
Moderate winds from 1000 until 1400. S-1 for 2 hours. We observed snow being actively transported.
0.5 to 1 inch of new snow at 5k.
Snowprofile at 6k, HS: 240, ECTX. We observed many crusts, including a layer of broken surface hoar, buried 45cm from the surface.
Surface snow was highly diverse. We traveled on a slick, supportable, and stout crust that transitioned to semi-supportable, with thin drifts of wind-distributed snow. 
Skiing above 5600 feet was good. Winds have been redistributing 3-4 inches of low-density snow.
By the end of the day, winds were calm.
Weather Summary: Overcast most of the day. Moderate winds until 2pm, when winds became calm.

Observation Summary: Did ECT on NE facing slope - ECTN 18 - 160cm deep in a 190cm pit.
Weather Summary: Calm winds, snow was available for transport but didn’t see any transported. Decent visibility until afternoon.

Route: Old mans bowl
Observation Summary: Another 1” of snow, calm conditions, no wind, and fair visibility.
Weather Summary: Great day
Instability Comments: All good

Route: North and east aspects to mid elevations
Observation Summary: Several crusts adding strength to the upper snowpack. 2 to 4 cm of new snow with thin drifts up to 15 cm over the most recent rime crust. All very small and harmless.
Large cornices on north aspect, multiple rain crusts, small volume of lower density snow on top of snow pack
Weather Summary: Overcast with occasional light flurry
Instability Comments: ECT n16 @100 cm from bottom

Route: Ascended up south facing terrain to ridge and up to peak.
Observation Summary:
Today our primary goal was to de-rime Link Mountain weather station. That station took first place for the most rimed station I have ever seen. 
- 1 to 2 inches of new snow overnight. We found roughly 4 to 6 inches of new and recent snow sitting on the MLK crust. This crust is thin and not noticeable while skiing here. We found it up to 7200 feet. 
- Two different layers of near surface facets were observed. Both had non propagating results in snowpit tests. 
- Surface snow was almost unneffected by wind in this location. 
- We observed some impressive debris piles that we suspect occurred during the Christmas rain event. 
Link Mountain is back in action.
Weather Summary: Relatively calm winds and very light precipitation.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking in small wind drifts near ridgeline.

Route: Essex to Marion Lake, Repeater Ridge, Dickey Creek and back.
Observation Summary: We found generally stable conditions, with very stubborn pockets of wind-deposited snow at upper elevations.
Weather Summary: Very light snowfall all day. No significant breaks in the clouds, though you could see them thinning. Ridgeline winds were light from the W.

Route: Crustville to Powderton
Observation Summary: Traveled into the Central Flathead Range to continue the hunt for buried surface hoar. We dug one pit on sheltered north facing below a saddle that we deemed a reasonable location for fragile feathers to survive the wind and sun. No luck finding the culprit.We did find high quality riding at upper elevations. 26cm of soft snow above the 1/13 crust. No signs of the 1/19 freezing rain/rime crust here. Winds appear to have been minimal in this drainage as even upper elevation westerlies harbored nice unconsolidated powder up to ridgetop and there was undistrurbed powder in the trees on "windward" slopes as well. Riding conditions were great above 6000', good down to 5200', and good enough below that.
Weather Summary: Cool temps and cloudy skies. Light snowfall most of the day with a few burst of S2. Approximately 6cm of new snow fallen at upper elevations by 3pm. Very light/calm winds all day to 6900' on ridgetop.  Climbed to this elevation for 3 laps between noon and 3pm and winds were calm on each climb.
Instability Comments: Generally good stability. Some sloughing on steep westerlies. Not at much as expected and inconsequential. Hardly worth noting.

Route: Skiied the first obvious Avi chute north of the trail as you approach Marion lake.
Observation Summary: We observed some short dry loose sluffs that were harmless at a slope angle of 40 degrees on a SSE aspect beginning at 5500 feet. This was very manageable at our elevation with only 10-15 cm of dry, fresh snow. Cloud level was hanging out around 7k or so all day.

The top 5-10 cm of snow not well bonded to the most recent crust, skiing on steeper slopes caused sluffing to run for a couple hundred feet. Dug one pit at 6400, north facing. Depth of 200 cm. Similar to Marion last weekend, got ectn11 at the layer 20 cm from surface. Slides right off with a light shovel shear. Everything below 4500 is locked up!

Route: Skied S, SE, and SW aspects. Spent a little time playing around on flat terrain at 5500’, where the snow was soft but too dense to be the deep fluffy powder we were looking for.
Observation Summary: Generally consistent snowpack where we traveled, no cracking. We felt comfortable skiing steeper pitches one at a time with eyes on our partners to manage the fast-moving sluff.
Weather Summary: The clouds hovered at about 6800’ all day. Breezy but no gusting on the ridge, and felt calm all day below ridge line.
Instability Comments: Sluff in the top several inches ran pretty fast and for several hundred feet in steeper zones and gullies

Observation Summary: Lots of snow moving.. which was expected but not much room for error.  Risk tolerance is too high :/
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Normal wx
Instability Comments: Medium loud whumpf then immediate shooting crack from my skiis out into the fog.  Slope was steep enough to slide so I’m not sure why it didn’t… tree anchors?!?  Humbling and unnerving.  Was third in line on the up track;other two in party did not observe/were too far ahead.  NW aspect. 6500 ft.  Northern swan range.

Observation Summary: No avalanche problems observed today.  Rode sustained 35 degree slopes with no concerns.
Weather Summary: Mild temps and light WSW winds on ridge line .  Skies were overcast throughout our tour, but upon returning to the valley floor around 3pm noticed the clouds breaking/sunny drive home through the Middle Fork Corridor.
Instability Comments: No major instability noted.  Some very mild effected snow near ridge lines but nothing of concern.

Observation Summary: Rode up towards the Whitefish Divide. Super crusty low snowpack around Polebridge made for difficult temperature management with the sleds. Snow got much better around 5800 ft and downright fun above 6200 ft to our high point at 7000. Intense fog kept us in the trees and ponds below the main east faces. Sidehill tested  some short 40+ degree slopes and saw no signs of instability. New snow on thin 1/19 rime crust was 10-15cm. Another 15cm soft below that. Great traction with the stiff but edgeable base snow.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)

Observation Summary: Triggered long shooting cracks when on landing snowbike at top of short, steep slope. Cracks extended about 100 feet. Small pocket in one corner released about 2 feet deep. Did not see anything else like this during the day.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Nice day. Calm.
Instability Comments: Cracks occurred on a steep, mid elevation slope that was south-southeast.

Observation Summary: 2-10 cm new/ recent snow on MLK crust. Crust supportable at low elevations, making for slick slick skinning and sometimes teeth-chattering exit. At mid elevations, crust occasionally unsupportable, with some hooky turns. Covered enough at upper elevations to make for good, consistent riding. Some soft, drifted snow on leeward slopes at upper elevations, with light blowing snow after 14:30-15:00.
Weather Summary: Two - Two days in one. Foggy and calm until ~1430, with cloud bases below 7000 ft. 1430-1500 Clouds abruptly broke up and skies cleared, with light winds at ridge lines. Gusts just strong enough to move some snow.

Route: Toured up from Stahl Peak to the lookout.
Observation Summary: Foggy and cold below about 7100’ after which we emerged into a world which led us to ask “is that sky particularly blue or have we just not seen it for that long?” Snow skied fast and fun.
Instability Comments: Our group noted a fairly well-consolidated snow pack as we conducted moving tests along our route. Dug about 200’ below the summit (in the Sun!) for the curious minds in our group and found a thin crust about 16cm down and a firmer one about 40cm down. Moderate force revealed failure on the layer 40cm down.

Observation Summary: Toured from WMR and skied multiple trees and open lines facing between east, south and west, 30-40+ degrees. The 1/19 rime crust was covered with 6-10 cm mid density snow (some grauple mixed in) and 10 cm below that there was another breakable rime crust. On a couple steeper convexities we experienced soft sluffing of the upper 10 cm’s. No cracking or or others signs of instability during skinning or skiing. Super fun turns with nice poppy rebound dynamics. Not deep but fun!

Route: Ascend/descend NW ridge.
Observation Summary:
We chased the surprise storm in the Northern Swan Range to check on the new snow and buried surface hoar:
***We found two layers of buried surface hoar in the top 50 cm of the snowpack in hand/test pits on W, S, and E aspects at middle and upper elevations.  These layers were adjacent to  thin crusts. 
-Small and large column tests failed with medium and heavy force including a propagating result in our South-facing test pit. 
-Found 4-6" of recent snow atop a rime/freezing rain crust.  The storm snow seemed well-adhered to the crust below and we noticed no signs of instability.
-Observed little sign of wind effect in an area that typically funnels SW winds.  Current winds were light with no blowing snow.
Weather Summary: Light wind from SW, S-1 at 1200hrs
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability

Route: Up skiumah to 5200'
Observation Summary: Little haggard down low on trail, but I'll take a supportable crust over an unsupportable one.  Guess the Swan took all the precip this week, unless it stacked up at higher elevations in the Flathead.
Weather Summary: S-1 to NO during day.  L-SW winds.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.  Felt like locked in frozen Low danger in terrain observed.

Route: Toured up to 5900' on north, northeast aspects of Herrig Mountain.
Observation Summary: We dug a pit at 5200', north aspect. 110cm hs, CT23 failed on recent crust layer approximately 12cm down. ECTN26 failed on the same layer. This crust layer was quite supportive, we were able to walk on it without skis/boards in many places. Observed a couple small rollerballs in steeper sections > 30deg
Weather Summary: Wind was calm to 5mph from the West, temperatures were low 20s.

Route: Skied three bowls along Mt Baldhead ridge.  Rode sleds up the 25mi rd and skinned up the standard route from the switchback above the cabin one day.  The road was easy sledding so kept going out toward the pass and skinned up the next drainage to the south the other two days.  Skied the north-east facing bowls along the ridge, never summited the peak.
Observation Summary: We felt good about skiing the steeper glades, so got some sluffing on pitches and roller overs.  There was hour long wind event thrusday night, but no signs of windslab formation.
Instability Comments: Experienced some sluffing on steep slopes

Route: Up ridgeline from Shed 7 pullout, past the weather station and down the Shed 7 W shoulder.
Observation Summary: Variable recent snow depths observed throughout tour, especially along the wind-affected ridgeline ascent, with slippery crusts exposed or just under the surface making ascending less than lovely. Recent Jan 13 crust is mostly supportable to ~6300' and thins above that, making for hooky skiing. We found buried decomposing surface hoar on sheltered N and NE aspects at 6800' all the way down to 5500' ~20cm below the surface. We began to trigger small roller balls with our turns below 5000'.
Weather Summary: S-1 snowfall throughout tour and light winds with little snow transporting.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today. Stomped small very fresh and touchy cornices near the top of Shed 7 W with no meaningful results.

Route: more tracks than the WHitefish railyard.
Observation Summary: We went hunting for recently buried surface hoar or near-surface facets. Did not find them. There are some weak interfaces in the upper ~75 cm of the snowpack but none propagated in tests.  We were surprised to trigger several sluffs on very steep, north-facing, mid elevation slopes that involved the 4 cm of new snow. These ran surprisingly far and could be a hazard in areas with more snow above the 1/19 rime crust.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Foggy, slightly less foggy, then more foggy. Fog base about 6000 feet in canyon creek at times. S-1 at times. Calm.

Route: Headed up Lost Johnny and over to Wounded Buck and back.
Observation Summary: Today our only goal was to assess the sensitivity and distribution of persistent weak layers in the upper part of the snowpack. We rode into the Swan Range and tried to cover as much ground as possible on snowmobiles. The snowmobile riding was excellent, but the visibility from icing on goggles was atrocious.  - We dug 4 pits on northerly and easterly aspects between 6000-6550. All snowpit tests propagated. These all failed on a weak layer of buried surface hoar and near-surface facets. - We were both surprised to see 2ish feet of snow resting on this weak layer in areas protected by the wind. We quickly wrote off all steep terrain. This included terrain sheltered from the wind, which we didn't suspect to be a problem. Surprise!- Our hopes of looking for any recent avalanche activity was squashed by the thick cloud we were stuck in.- A thin freezing rain crust now blankets the surface.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: We were in a wet cloud with freezing rain almost the entire day. Visibility was less than a 100 yards. Winds calm until the end of the day.
Instability Comments: We were on snowmobiles all day so signs of instability were not as obvious as traveling on skis. We had no views of larger avalanche terrain above 6000 feet.

Route: Multi aspect tour in the Northern Whitefish Range from 3600' to 7000'. We skied mainly NW aspects and dug a pit on that same aspect to test the near surface weak layers we found yesterday.
Observation Summary: The temperatures dropped below freezing at the trailhead, which improved the quality of the snow at lower elevations (hardpack is a huge upgrade over breakable crust). The rime we had observed on the surface of the snow yesterday above 5500' had turned into a 1-2 mm thick rime crust today, increasing in thickness with elevation gain.We dug a pit at 6464' on a NW aspect, and did not get propagating results in the weak layers we found yesterday. CT23 RP failed on a weak layer of facets 26 cm under the snow -- the same layer on which we were able to trigger three slides yesterday on a loaded test slope.At around 3:00 pm a freezing rain began to fall. The air temp and snow surface temp at our test pit less than an hour prior was -5C. It quickly froze on the existing rime crust on the surface of the snow, making the surface crust a little more stout. We experienced freezing rain between 5700' and 6800' and between 3:00 and 5:00 when we descended.The snow quality was soft in sheltered areas without the rime/freezing rain crust, and still fairly enjoyable where the crust was present.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Temperatures dropped below freezing at all elevations down to at least 3600' at the trailhead. Temp at 3600' at 9am was 33F, Temp at 2:00 at our pit side at 6464' was 23F. Winds were mostly calm throughout the day, occasionally light and variable. The sky was obscured (fog above 5200'). Occasional snow flurries in the morning. A light freezing rain began at 3pm that quickly froze on goggles, gear, and the 1-2mm thick rime crust that is widespread in this zone at mid to upper elevations.

Observation Summary:
Took a tour into the Flathead Range to verify danger rating and look for any buried persistent weak layers.
- Wind speeds were stronger than expected. Ridge walking above 6500 feet was comical and painful at times due to gust speeds and blowing snow. Observed very small and isolated slabs of drifted snow. These were unreactive to stomping. 
- Found surface hoar buried just beneath the surface in all hand pits on northerly aspects an up to 7500 ft. There is currently only 3 to 5 inches of snow resting on top of it.  
- Recent melt-freeze crust extends to 6800 feet. Below this elevation, the crust is supportable and slick. Crampons are helpful.
We chose to ski a steep northwest-facing run given no signs of instability. The skiing was much better than expected, even despite the freezing rain crust that formed throughout the day.
Weather Summary: Periods of strong winds above 6500 feet. Freezing rain up to 6800 feet. Light snowfall on and off throughout the day (S-1).
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.

Route: Toured into Ten Lakes to ski a northern aspect and get eyes on the northern Whitefish range for the first time since the fall. Started at 3600' and toured up to 6500'. Great snow above 5200'. A mixture of grabby, icy, and punch crust below that elevation.
Observation Summary: We went out hunting in a favorite zone for surface hoar and wind loading, hoping to ski some steep lines. We did not observe any surface hoar during our tour. We did find moderate wind loading. There was no recent snow below 4800', about an inch of the heavy wet stuff between 4800' and 5200', and substantially more above that elevation line -- roughly the 5-8" reported in the rest of the forecast area.Due to cross loading and wind slabs from a westerly wind in our north facing lines, we chose to ski an adjacent slope that was sheltered from the wind, and enjoyed soft snow free of wind slabs. However, snow nerds are curious, so we traversed out onto a loaded test slope below 35 degrees, and were able to intentionally trigger three small, but propagating slides (see photo).Several quick hand pits between 5700' and 6500' reacted with some energy on two weak layers, both thin layers of facets, after exerting moderate force. The first layer we found about 20 cm down under a M-F crust at 5700' on a W aspect. This weak layer was still present at 6500' on a N aspect, while the M-F crust was not present. The second layer was roughly 35-40 cm down.Given our findings, we're hoping to dig tomorrow and assess the propagation propensity of these near-surface weak layers.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Valley fog lifted in the early afternoon. We were mostly obscured in fog up above 5600', with occasional breaks. Unsurprisingly (it is the Whitefish range) we noted heavy rime on trees and cliffs, which had blown off or accumulated onto the surface of the snow in some locations. Winds were calm with the exception of ridgeline on the top of the pass where they were light and variable. Last night's rain line we estimated at 4800'.

Route: •	Climbed the Shed 6/7 ridge to the Shed 7 weather station, then continued up to the Snowslip weather station and descended the Shed 9 path.
Observation Summary: •	A new crust formed after the recent cool down and extends from the Canyon floor (~1/2” thick) up to about 7000’ (high point of the day) where it’s about eggshell thickness.•	New snow depths on top of this latest crust were about 1” on the Canyon floor and 2-4” above 6000’ in non-wind loaded locations.•	No wind loading until above 6000’ elevation and then this was minimal. Found some new wind slabs (6-10” thick) near ridge crests on easterly and northerly aspects, but these were unreactive with no shooting cracks from ski cuts.•	Below 5500’ the snow is moist between the mid-January crust and the late-Dec/Christmas crust below. By ~6000’ the snow between the two crusts was dry with some notable facet layers but could not find any buried surface hoar. •	Dug a hasty pit in the upper reaches of Shed 9 at 6400’ on a SE aspect. Snow depth was about 47”. Extended Column test yielded some ECTNs right below the mid-Jan crust and then ECTP28 on 1-2 mm facets right above the Christmas crust (~22” below the surface, mostly 1F hard snow save for the newest snow on the surface) with the column sliding into the pit. Did a 2nd test and the same layer failed again only with less force, ECTP13. •	No recent avalanche activity observed.
Weather Summary: •	Overcast or broken skies with very light snowfall and a trace of accumulation throughout the day.•	Light SW winds above 5500’ elevation.•	Daytime highs reached the low-30s °F on the Canyon floor and the mid-20s °F above 6000’ elevation.

Route: Elk/Shields trailhead to Elk Mountain traveling up SW ridge and yo yo on SW/W face.
Observation Summary: Temperatures at trailhead were right around freezing.  Area toured had temperatures below freezing all day.  The 1/13 crust was very supportable to 6,300 feet.  Above this elevation it was breaking but still evident to 7,350 feet; where it transitioned near the top of Elk Mountain to old wind crust and large rime.   New snow was 1/2 inch below 5,500 feet and about 1 inch up to around 6,200 feet.  Above this it varied from 1 to 6 inches depending on aspect and wind deposition.  Snow was actively being transported above 6,000 feet.  Refer to snow profile but ECT and CT tests were not very reactive and no notable sluffing or cracking was experienced during skiing.

Wind transported snow deposited into soft pillows on NW-SE aspects. These pockets were not reactive on test slopes. Rollerballs from the warming event earlier in the week were observed on all aspects and were nearly covered up by new snow. Loose snow generated impressive amounts of sluff in steep terrain.
Weather Summary: The sky was mostly obscured for the day with periods of moderate S-1 snowfall for 2 hours mid-afternoon. Winds were generally calm out of the SW, gusts produced ridge top transport onto leeward slopes.

Route: Up Marion Creek trail, north-facing laps down to Marion Creek, then egress off Essex Mtn. back to Essex Road.
Observation Summary: Snowpack is fairly solid from 50 cm on down.  Looks like top 50 cm could possibly slide but not in our test and we observed no dry or wet avalanches.  Did see quite a few rollerballs from several days ago on several aspects.
Weather Summary: No precip today and no wind.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability

Route: Up rescue creek to 5640 ft, N aspect.
Observation Summary: ECTN5 down 5cm below 2cm P hard melt/freeze crust on facets.ECTN15 down 20cm on 1cm K hard melt/freeze crust on small facets.
Weather Summary: Calm SW winds, s-1 precip, overcast to sparse moments of broken sky
Instability Comments: Few wet loose D1 avalanches observed at low to mid elevations. Most activity consisted of rollerballs and pinwheels triggered from falling snow from trees and shedding rocks/steep aspects above 60 degrees.

Observation Summary: Holiday explore with a trip plan primed with potential excuses - overheating sleds on frozen roads, creek crossing, mushy snow, doghair wildfire regen. Never took one of the excuses, and found decent conditions up high. However, these deteriorated to knee-wrenching mush at low elevations.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Valley fog below about 4800-5000 ft. High stratus above. Calm winds, except for a few brief breezes. S-1 snow shower mid afternoon. Temps above freezing except upper elevations.

Route: WMR- Skook Peak and beyond
Observation Summary: A generally stable snowpack with kinda poor structure.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Only light wind observed throughout the tour. WX conditions generally matched the forecast.
Instability Comments: We did see some (15cm thick) slabs breaking on the new crust on steeper test terrain features, and on steep kick turns. The new 15cm slabs that we observed were fist - 4f hardness. No other obvious signs of instability observed

Route: Central Flathead Range via Highway 2
Observation Summary: Traveled into the Central Flathead Range to follow up on FAC's report of buried surface hoar and touchy windslabs in the Swan Range. We were curious to see if similar conditions and structure could be found here. Not quite. We targeted a 6800' peak with a moderate angled NE facing slope as a prime candidate for safely assessing these problems. We were underwhelmed by the amount of recent snow in the area and we also found very little evidence of wind affect and wind slab formation. Winds were dead calm on ridgetop today and appear to have been insignificant in the last 24 hours as well. We were also unsuccessful at locating buried surface hoar. In hindsight, we felt that we should have selected a pit site further below the ridgetop to search for SH. So we dug several quick handpits at middle elevations to continue the hunt and they were not revealing any signs of the weak layer FAC found in the Swan. We skied one lap on NE facing and a second lap on West Facing. Both aspects harbored a thin crust below the recent snow, however the windward westerly slope had a slightly thicker crust that was noticeably more firm and icy. We attribute this difference to the NE slope forming its crust due to warm temps, whereas the westerly slope was subject to both warm temps and sun Jan 12-13. Loose dry snow sloughed easily on this west facing slope. Riding conditions were pretty good at upper elevations and crappy/dangerously challenging below 5500'.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Dead calm winds. Broken skies, Inversion sandwich, thick valley fog, and mostly cloudy. No new snow during the day. Temps remained cool all day.
Instability Comments: Not a lot of new snow since the Jan 12-13 warm up. Only 10cm or so available for transport at ridgetop (~6800'), but with no wind present to move anything around. No signs of instability observed. Pit test on NE facing upper elevation slope resulted in ECTX.

Route: Sleds from Whitefish to 6000’, skied a SW face up to 7000’
Observation Summary: Flat light made it challenging to observe much on the ridge while stumbling over windlips, but the rime on the ridgetop made the first couple of inches of snow there seem to crack and collapse under our skis, but this disappeared quickly below the ridge. The skiing exceeded our expectations and no stability concerns were noted. The upper cloud deck moved around all day, while we stayed above the valley inversion layer. Descending back into it at the end of the day, it was certainly cooler than the morning, with some flakes swirling in the air, whereas the morning was more like sledding through a cool steam room.
Instability Comments: lots of leftover rollerballs from the trees shedding the day before, which were covered with a couple cm’s of fresh that seemed to keep them from turning into frozen death cookies. No current signs of instability were observed.

Route: Rode sleds to Camp Misery. Skinned to Noisy Basin SNOTEL. Ascending to Aeneas Ridge weather station.
Observation Summary:
We went hunting for buried surface hoar in the Swan Range today, but despite digging all day, we did not find that pesky gray line. 

As we rode up the Jewel Basin Road on sleds, the slush gradually turned to snow about 5500 feet.
We dug pits at all three elevation bands, on different aspects, but we did not find a reactive weak layer of buried surface hoar.
We did find a knife-hard, 1 cm, melt-freeze crust buried under the recent snow (10 to 15 cm). This crust extends up to 6700 feet.
Many of the northeast-facing slopes that we could observe in the fog, had evidence of older roller balls and tree bombs.
There was evidence of wind-loading and drifting along the Aeneas Ridge, despite having calm conditions all day.
Cornices along the ridge were sagging. We estimated that some chucks of cornices had failed during the warm temperatures on 1/13/23.
We suspected that the buried weak layer found 1/15/23 was destroyed by wind in the area we traveled, as we could only find broken pieces of surface hoar, buried 20 to 35 cm below the surface.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Unusually calm winds. Visibility was limited most of the day. At our exit, we rode in S-1 conditions for 20 minutes.
Instability Comments: Minimal cracking in wind stiffened drifts. We observed that when we stomped on 8-inch cornices, they failed on the recent melt-freeze crust.

Route: Standard route up, lap on SW aspect, out NE aspect (skiers right trees to gut)
Observation Summary: No negative feedback, snowing on top raining at the bottom. Old pinwheels under fresh snow. Breakable crust starting at 6200’, supportable above to 6900’. Manky. No concerns about stability unless it gets even warmer.
Weather Summary: Overcast, broken clouds, fog, no wind. Some snow/rain.
Instability Comments: No negative feedback, snowing on top raining at the bottom. Old pinwheels under fresh snow. Breakable crust starting at 6200’, supportable above to 6900’. Manky. No concerns about stability unless it gets even warmer.

Observation Summary:
Today we went hunting for buried surface hoar and wind slabs in the Swan Range. We found slabs of drifted snow to be "touchy" where they rested on buried surface hoar. I was quite surprised at how sensitive these slabs were. 
- Triggered two slab avalanches on a leeward-facing ridgeline above 6500 feet. The first slab we triggered would likely be large enough to bury a person. Both slabs were 1 to 2 feet thick. More info in avalanche section. 
- We descended down the ridge to search for buried surface hoar beneath 6500 feet. We found faceted snow resting on a crust, but no surface hoar. Although we never triggered anything, the snowpack structure and neighboring avalanches were enough avoid steep slopes on north through east aspects. 
- There were roughly two inches of new snow on a superficial rain crust at middle elevations. 
The obvious red flags we observed made many decisions for us. We turned to windward and solar aspects where no buried surface hoar was observed. Here we felt comfortable skiing steep terrain spaced out. At roughly 5700 feet, the crust became unsupportable and we triggered very small wet loose avalanches in the surface snow. The ski quality went in the dumps at around 5500'. Above that, quality exceeded expectations.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Rain up to roughly 5000 feet. Snow showers (periods of S1) above 5500 feet.
Instability Comments: Remotely triggered one slab avalanche on a northeast facing slope at roughly 6700'. Triggered a second slab avalanche with a cornice fall at roughly 6800'. Both avalanche suspect failed on buried surface hoar, which was found on an adjacet slope. We did not access crowns due to overhead cornice hazards.

Route: Visited various locations in and around WMR.
Observation Summary: Observed fresh soft wind slab formation on lee side of non-forested ridge lines. It appears that all steep terrain in the "Skook Chutes" may have produced wet slides in the last 48 hours. Debris is deposited to and across the lower portion of the summer trail (typical skin track from saddle), and the Canyon Crk road (as was previously reported in another observation).
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Temps remained at or below freezing above 6,000 ft.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability noted.

Route: Cascadilla drainage up to 4000'
Observation Summary: New wet slide activity from the last 48 hours observed at all aspects in cascadilla up to 5500'. Slides entrained snow entirely above the Xmas crust. Many slides occured directly on top of the large holiday slide paths, but some slides we're observed on faces that did not look to have slid previously. Some slides ran for 1000 or more vertical feet and had large debris run-outs.We experienced about 8 inches of concrete density wet snow on top of mostly crust but some facets were present underneath as well. This thick concrete was present up to 4500' where we turned around and likely extended much higher, it was really warm. We observed a mix of snow and rain from 4000'-4500' and rain below that. It was a light drizzle for most of the day.
Instability Comments: New small to medium sized wet slides observed at all aspects.

Route: Out Ghoulies Ridge from Flower Point to do snow assessments then returned to WMR
Observation Summary: Calm socked in day out in Ghoulies with quite a few folks out for a rip off the ridge!
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Calm winds today and light snowfall between 2 and 4pm.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today, but looking for avalanches wasn't easy with consistent obscured skies and thick fog above 5500' today.

Observation Summary: News of wet loose slides definitely reduced use. High elevation in canyon was decent, got heavy and wet near lower elevation.

Route: Took Wounded Buck Road up to "Y" that splits off to the trailhead. Continues left to an opening. Got a report from the public of avalanche debris above our turnaround.
Observation Summary: Did a column test northerly slope. Ct of 16 that split across the column at approx 120 cm. Sheer test broke off clean at 100 cm with moderate pulling.

Rain gave way to snow around 5500” and started accumulating above this. About 5 cm of new snow sitting on top of the newest crust at 6900’. Dug one pit on south aspect at 6700’. Depth of 185 cm. On this aspect the crust layer and below is quite uniform and stout! No propagation, but with shovel sheer there was a notable failed 20 cm from surface. Some mild wind, but nevertheless cornices rebuilding and cracking. Saw recent wet slides and plenty of partially re frozen roller balls. Certainly felt winter like above 6k!

Route: Skook face to Eastern border of Skook chutes.
Observation Summary: Lots of pinwheels. Loose wet avalanche in Skook chutes that ran to the canyon. Deposited four feet of debris on the upper road.
Weather Summary: Party sunny, no wind below the ridges, and above freezing temps.
Instability Comments: Hot pow with lots of pinwheels.

Observation Summary: Above freezing temps all morning combined with mostly cloudy skies turned the atmosphere in a greenhouse.  Easy to make large truck tire sized pinwheels with the toss of a snowball.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Looked like March, skiied like April.
Instability Comments: Easily could have triggered wet loose slides on open exposed terrain. Rolling baseball sized snowballs off the side of the trail told me all I needed to know..

Observation Summary:
I visited a windy location in the southern Whitefish Range to get a handle on recent wind slab distribution and reactivity. I was also interested in seeing how the former cool, dry snow surface was being affected by overnight warming.

Southerly winds had drifted recent snow into slabs up to 1 foot thick on northerly slopes. The slabs were generally small, cohesionless, and unreactive to a skier's weight.
New cornice growth cracked and failed from the weight of a skier. The new lip was soft, and cracks would travel no further than the length of my skis.
Overnight warming initiated tree bombs, rollerballs, and two thin Wet, Loose slides. Moist snow extended to ridgeline elevation (up to 6700') on all aspects.
It was easy to trigger rollerballs on steep slopes, but none of these initiated a Wet Loose slide. 
A snowpit at 6650' was dug down to the Christmas Atmospheric River rain crust. See the snowpack section for more details.
Weather Summary: Warm and humid conditions this morning continued through the afternoon. Fortunately, a thick layer of high clouds limited solar radiation.
Instability Comments: The recent warming triggered two thin wet loose slides in the south-facing Skook Chutes and extensive rollerball activity on all aspects at mid-elevations.

Route: Summer trail to Marion Lake to Repeater Ridge.
Observation Summary:
We went to slopes above Marion Lake today in search of surface snow conditions and to look for evidence of wind-affected snow.

It felt like a day in April and not mid-January. Snow was wet and transitioned to moist around 5000 feet.
The winds were calm most of the day with only a few wind gusts.
The sunshine brought fresh roller balls on both northerly and southerly aspects.
We skied slopes that had already been warmed with above-freezing temperatures, experiencing heavy, mash-potato quality turns.
In the area that we traveled, the trees were free of snow.
Weather Summary: Periods of sunshine with scattered clouds for most of the day. Dark clouds loomed South of our location.
Instability Comments: Roller balls observed on all aspects. Trees were clear of snow, with evidence of widespread tree bombs.

Route: Ascend southerly aspect to ridge.  Traverse ridge to the west. Descend easterly slide path.
Observation Summary:
Teamed up with USGS on a visit to Snowslip:
-Found a variety of surfaces on the ascent including: breakable sun crust, supportable rain crust, dry faceted snow and wind abused surface hoar.
-Winds were variable, gusty, and sufficient enough to drift what little loose snow was available.
-We found isolated pockets of wind drifted snow on Easterly aspects. Generally these were not cohesive enough to produce any cracking. 
Because we anticipated potential wind loading at the top of our descent, we chose to ski one at a time as we navigated the entrance to the line.  After regrouping, we skied the remainder of the line, spacing ourselves out at an interval of roughly 10 seconds.. The skiing on shelterred terrain was much better than anticipated.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: S-1 started at 1100
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability

Observation Summary: Overall fun lower angle turns to be had above 6000 ft on west to east slopes esp. on more sheltered terrain with an icy luge exit. Very thin sun crust on more open south slopes observed on the way up. Moderate winds with high gusts from the west on the ridge transporting the 8+ new inches from Sunday. Ridge line scoured and rimey.
Weather Summary: Flurries to light snow for a couple hours starting about 1030 with clearing skies and much better visibility by 1600.

Route: Windy multi-aspect tour up to 7600'. We chose to ski a western face to try to avoid our wind slab problem. Great surface snow above 6000' -- snow changed to rain below this elevation in the late afternoon. Swirling winds, blowing snow, and (below 6000') a snowpack that was saturated with small amounts of rain caused instability to rise throughout the day.
Observation Summary: Went out in the wind and surprisingly stormy weather for a multi-aspect tour up to 7600'. We had multiple ascent/descent options we had discussed, and ended up choosing to ascend and descend a western face after observing moderate to strong W and SW winds transporting snow at ridgeline, as to avoid being on wind loaded slopes in low visibility. Minus the couple hundred feet of wind-scoured-death-ridges down from the summit we were rewarded in our decision making with a long run of great soft surface snow mostly free of wind loading. As the rain line rose to 6000' by 1700 hrs, we observed widespread rollerballs and rising instability as water has been added to our unconsolidated top layer.We observed blowing snow in addition to a small amount of new snowfall throughout the day. While the blowing snow occurring at ridgeline was SW-W, we observed snow being transported by the S to E wind funneling through our drainage. We estimate yesterday's snow available to transport at up to a foot.Due to the wind and time we did not dig a pit today. My partner did note widespread surface hoar on all aspects in the same drainage yesterday. By this afternoon, however, surface hoar only existed on the snow's surface in isolated sheltered areas -- either knocked over by the swirling winds or buried under new/blowing snow. We found an isolated, thin (1mm or less) sun crust around 7000' on a SSW aspect. During our exit from the drainage around 1630 hrs, we noticed glopping on our skis (around 6000'). We were initially befuddled until we looked up and realized that the wind-driven snow had changed to wind-driven rain. We made haste and traveled one at a time through steeper terrain to limit the hazard as the risk for wet loose avalanches rapidly increased. It was a little spooky to be skiing down while rollerballs ran naturally past you. A great ski day, but with a lot going on!
Weather Summary: Our weather forecast of "30% chance of everything" delivered and then some. At 1100 hrs at the trailhead at 4200' it was 27 degrees F with S-1 precip.
