By 1700 hrs at the same location it was 34 degrees and raining (or "heavily misting"). The cloud cover ranged from broken to overcast to obscured. The snow was on and off throughout our tour and briefly increased to a maximum of around S2 in the early afternoon. By 1530-1600 hrs, the snowfall at our elevation on ridgeline at about 7600' mixed with wind-driven frozen pellets.The wind and blowing snow was the main story of the day (until we discovered it had rained below 6000'). It seemed to change slightly from the SW to W at ridgeline throughout the day, consistently moderate gusting to strong as large trees were swaying. However, ascending an east aspect on a southern drainage we noticed that the wind was funneling from the S through the drainage, and swirling at us from the E. We imagine this terrain-enhanced wind swirling occurred in drainages and canyons throughout the area, and would expect to find wind loading in unusual spots, and crossroading near the tops of gullies outside of the normal places to find wind loading with a SW-W wind.
Instability Comments:
The wind was swirling and being funneled through drainages and terrain features, therefore we expect canyons, creeks, and gullies below ridgeline to have wind slabs and crossloading in unusual areas. We avoided wind loaded slopes on our tour and did not have the opportunity to test the reactivity of any newly formed slabs. My partner had toured in the same drainage the day prior (before today's wind event) and noted up to a foot of available snow for transport up high, most of it being faceted "recycled pow." We did not experience cracking, collapsing, or whumpfing.

By 1700 hrs rain had fallen at up to 6000'. Rollerballs were happening naturally and were widespread on steeper slopes below 6000'. I would expect wet loose avalanches ran naturally in areas collecting the most rainfall. We did not dally long enough to take photos of the widespread rollerballs.

Route: Toured to 7200' on east aspect
Observation Summary: North and east facing snow was faceted and cohesionless with surface hoar on top. Multiple small loose dry avalanches in steep terrain that ran fast. Making sure riders were in safe zones and not stopping in on top of one another was critical.

Route: Toured up the south west slop to top of snowslip
Observation Summary: Snow seemed solid.  Skiing was better than expected as skinning was a struggle guessing due to so much hoar frost.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Light winds. Not much of any snow moving around.
Instability Comments: No signs

Route: On Wednesday we reached approx 7200 on a steep protected N-NE aspect. Thursday we reached 6000 on a similar aspect.
Observation Summary: Went up Skiumah 2 days in a row. Wednesday the riding was great with small pockets of wind slabs on unsheltered NE aspects. Found the supportable Christmas crust underneath the powder up to the top of our line at 7200 feet. Toured up again on Thursday with the plan to drop into the Great Bear creek drainage, but vis was bad and the wind was blowing every direction, so we stuck to sheltered terrain in Skiumah.
Weather Summary: 1/11: no notable weather. Slightly foggy, no sun after the morning1/12: a dusting of new snow. The wind appeared to be swirling around the drainage, had a hard time pin pointing a specific wind direction. NE aspects were actively loading.
Instability Comments: Windslabs varying from 2-6” deep with varying levels of reactivity

Observation Summary: Rollerballs, large ones, and wet loose avalanches
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Instability Comments: Witnessed warming snow beginning to produce large numbers of big rollerballs, and small shooting cracks and slabs forming while creating a skin track on a S/SE facing aspect, saw appx 5 (D2) slides on a high elevation E/NE facing aspect

Route: Toured to 7200'
Observation Summary: Toured to 7200' hoping to ski a steep alpine SE face higher up. We found it wind-loaded and baking in the sun so we decided to ski a Windward NE aspect. ECTX at 7000' on NE aspect. There was a thin crust above the stout rain crust. Significant sluffing on steep NE aspects made sluff management critical.

Today we investigated a crown of a reported avalanche in the Southern Whitefish Range. See the avalanche section for more information.
- We found 3 to 5 inches of new snow overnight. This refreshed surfaces and skied quite well.
- Observed one very small loose dry sluff from steep rocky terrain. 
- Snow above the December Rain Crust is faceted and weak in sheltered terrain at middle elevations. No signs of cracking or collapsing.
- Our travels only took us into terrain sheltered from the wind.
Weather Summary: In an out of the cloud throughout the day. Felt rain on us at roughly 5800 feet for a brief moment.

Route: Essex Area
Observation Summary: Another good day out in the mountains. Evidence of strong winds last night at all elevations. This unfortunately negatively impacted riding conditions in areas open to the wind. But, on the plus side, there was not significant new snowfall in our drainage last night so fresh windslabs were thin/non-existent at upper elevations. We didn't encounter any windslabs worth noting during our ski off ridgetop onto a ENE slope.
Weather Summary: Winds were calm to light during most of our tour. However, winds were light to moderate out of the SSW on the last 200 feet to our days highpoint (7900'). We were up top at noon. Between 7000' and 7900', west facing slopes were significantly rimed, inhibiting windloading. We enjoyed another day of sunny skies until mid afternoon. By about 2pm skies transitioned to overcast and light flurries began around 530 during the drive home. Temps were cool, but ample sunshine was impacting solar aspects at mid elevations.
Instability Comments: No recent instability observed. One glide crack found on an east facing slope around 6000'. Most of the major SE facing paths in lower essex creek had cycled during the Santa Soaker. At least two went big and reached the creek bottom. The northwest facing paths on showshed seem to have been quieter during the cycle.

Route: Rode sleds up to and around Werner Lookout.
Observation Summary:
Today we went into the Whitefish Range to hunt persistent weak layers and explore some less frequented ground for us. 
- Overall observed a shallower snowpack (120-130 cm) than seen throughout the rest of the forecast area. This average was from a couple different probes between 6500 to 6700. 
- Found pockets of recently formed winds labs. Slabs were 4 to 6 inches thick and relatively firm. These were stubborn to trigger. 
- Basal snowpack structure is poor. 3 to 5 mm depth hoar with obvious evidence of rounding. 
- We got propagating pit results on large column tests failing on the Christmas crust. 
This is the poorest snowpack structure we've found throughout the forecast area. And shallowest. I would currently be making a lot different decisions if the impenetrable rain crust didn't exist.
Weather Summary: Clear skies became broken after noon. Partly cloudy for the remainder of day. Late morning sun was warm.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking in snow drifted by wind.

location: Cliffs east of half moon outside WMR boundary
Observation Summary: Recent avalanche activity

Route: Rescue Creek to South face of Penrose
Observation Summary: On the hike up Rescue Cr. trace amounts of new snow were blown off of melt-freeze crusts into small drifts and the skin track.  Afternoon cloud cover kept the solar intensity down. Variable conditions up high on exposed areas with rime and sun crusts. The sheltered skiing was great!
Weather Summary: Cleared up around 10:30 then high clouds moved in for the afternoon. Winds above 6500' were SW 5-10

Route: Mostly traveled on N-E-S facing slopes at low and mid elevations.
Observation Summary: Day off ski. Mostly found dry snow with no surface crust, no signs of instability except a wee sluff on one turn, and good views. We stayed on sheltered slopes below 6800 feet to avoid any freshly formed wind slabs or freshly imposed wind effects on the surface snow.
Weather Summary: T-1 cm new snow, rimed or graupel. Clear/ few skies in AM, with increasing clouds early afternoon. High overcast by mid afternoon. Winds last night/ early AM left pine needles and other debris on the snow surface. Almost no wind today, however.

Route: From Rescue Creek trail, skinned up to SE saddle of Penrose to the summit. Skied the E face chute back down.
Observation Summary: Evidence of a melt freeze crust lower in the Penrose bowl (observed up to ~5500ft). No wind slab or scouring was evident until about 6800ft up the ridge and what had formed was very thin. The line we skied on the E face had well preserved light and dry snow that easily sluffed while skiing. As we skied lower we hit the MF crust in patches but did not affect quality of turns. Good day out!
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Clear and sunny day turned to OVC by about 2pm. Good visibility entire day. Temps were moderate and winds were light.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed. Would be curious about facet growth/weak layers forming near Xmas rain crust through time.

Observation Summary: Thin sun crust on exposed south aspects.  Noted thick crust throughout ascent by pole strike at about 5 inches below soft snow.  Evidence at approximately 6300 ft of small roller balls with only one small propagation, likely occurred yesterday.  No roller balls noted above this and none new today as it did not warm enough by the time we exited the south aspect.  The riding quality was excellent until about 4500 feet where it turned to heavy snow on a stout crust.  Did note several small loose dry sluffs in steep upper terrain, did not run far
Instability Comments: One member of our group reported finding a glide crack that looked like it opened with the rain event, some new snow covering.  Notes on NE/E aspect at approx 6300 ft. We steered well clear of this.

Route: Climbed Skiers right of Marian Creek. About .3 miles from Marion Lake, climbed to northern ridge via established skin track. Descended NE Aspect Ridge to Dickey Creek Road
Observation Summary: Dug pit on NE aspect of slope in Dickey Creek drainage.  Compression test showed no signs of instability. Crust later was well established.  Identified a 50 yard length glide crack upon descent. Appeared to be from rain cycle with new snow accumulation from recent storm cycle. Navigated to a new route clear of avalanche path below crack.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Instability Comments: Glide Crack about 6000 to 6500 feet down NW aspect

Observation Summary: A generally stable snowpack that matches a recent obs and forecasts.We poked around in steeper rocky terrain above 6800ft. Locations adjacent to rocks in steep terrain did not harbor the Christmas crust and the snowpack is weak, thin, and faceted.We spent extra time discussing our mindset pre tour and throughout our tour we discussed how conditions matched the forecast. We felt confident “stepping out” a bit more as we explored steeper terrain today.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Just as we began to discuss the solar impact (started to see small tree bombs lead to small roller balls) on a E facing slope in the AM the clouds and wind rolled in and the heater turned off. We observed swirling moderate- strong wind gusts transporting snow at and near ridgelines. These gusts were inconsistent.
Instability Comments:
No signs of instability observed.

Some loose, cohesiveness snow moved on steeper slopes. It was isolated and non threatening to skiers.

Observation Summary: Some may say that the Middle Fork isn’t consistent. The sun was out today, which was confusing. Danger was Low, which seems atypical for early January. Yet despite those wildcards, we still got to schwack up 2000ft feet of crust covered logs and boosh before the snow got better, so it felt just like normal. Nothing much to report on the stability front. Some wind affect up high on E and NE aspects. More sheltered, flatter S aspects were fully slush at the surface around 6k ft, so there’s another crust in the making. Sky was either clear or overcast; no precip; winds were light to moderate on ridge tops.

Route: Central Flathead Range
Observation Summary: Another nice day to be out. Skied polar aspects up to 40 degrees without signs of instability.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Filtered sunshine until about 1pm. Skies gradually became overcast later in the afternoon with very light snowfall starting around 230pm. Light winds with a few moderate gusts from the southwest during the day.
Instability Comments: Good stability today. Noted some tree plop triggered wet loose on a steep east facing slope @ 6200'. Appeared to have occurred yesterday. The glide on great northern has not increased in size since last obs from Jan 2nd, however bedsurface is now covered by blown in snow.

Route: Went right up the gut of Skiumah, summited the ridge at the back of the drainage, just above the observation location at 7000 feet.
Observation Summary: Variable crust and faceting surface snow at all elevations from the valley to the ridge. Shady areas had drier faceting snow which was great riding. The snow was not as dry and sugary as the earlier season depth hoar we observed. More sun exposed slopes had a thin crust at the surface, approx 0.5 cm thick in most areas where presentAt our NE aspect there was minimal wind loading and minor cornice growth.
Weather Summary: Beautiful sunny day with great views all around. Clouds built around 2:30 and very light snow fell from 3:30 to 5:00
Instability Comments: No instability observed.

Route: Big North facing steeps
Observation Summary: Lots of skers out and about. Met multiple parties skiing in drainages for first time.Skied steep N facing aspects up to 7800 feet on Saturday and Sunday. Trail breaking was easy even on steep terrain. West winds were depositing snow on East facing slopes most of the day Sunday. New drifts were still soft .
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Not the best visibility to ski steep North facing slopes, but good enough. Light winds both days with light flurries closing in by the end of the day Sunday.
Instability Comments: Saw a few old crowns and glide cracks on various slopes above 6 grand

Route: Toured up Rescue Creek to the saddle between Pinrose and Nayak.
Observation Summary: Between 6000-6400 ft, shaded snow was soft and loose. Small loose sluffs observed in this zone. From 6000 ft to about 5400 ft there was a significant rime crust at the surface with soft somewhat sugary snow below. The rime crust was easy to punch through. The buried freeze crust seemed solid. From about 5800 ft to roughly 5600 ft the rime crust became very hard and icy. Signs of wind blown snow all over the drainage. Some of that wind blown texturing occurred in the rime/frozen crusts. Overall snow felt stable.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken skies, little to no wind. Overall snow felt stable. Signs of an incoming but delivered nothing, and skies cleared again.
Instability Comments: Shooting crack in the surface rime/newer snow at about 5400 ft (North aspect - 33 degree slope angle). Crack propagated roughly 40 ft. and was confined above the freeze crust.

Route: Up skiuma creek proper
Observation Summary: beautiful views
Weather Summary: started the day under clear cool skies in the teens, around 1 pm dark clouds moved in. Mild to moderate winds out of the south/southwest 5-10MPH

Route: All over our ski terrain the past few days
Observation Summary: a stable snowpackmultiple CTN scores in various locations within our terrain
Weather Summary: warm
Instability Comments:
Really a very stable snowpack overall!
Some minor "slow" sloughing in steeper (30-40 degree) northern aspect slopes.

Observation Summary: Decided to make a go at the north face of Penrose, thinking it might be a good time.  We hoped the crust would see us through, but ended up in knee deep, drifted snow above the Kristmas Krust around 5800ft and bailed for less complex terrain and flatter angles. Lots of evidence of wind affect and plenty of formerly wet debris making for frozen obstacles at middle elevations. We broke a very small pane while skinning up a windloaded gully later in the day. Sheltered terrain held the best skiing. Sky was partly cloudy, with light winds all day. No precip, and either around or below freezing temperature wise.

Route: Skied the northwest face of Diamond peak through the main gut
Observation Summary: D3 wet slide 2000’ path length. From Christmas. Took down full sized trees. Stretched  almost to valley floor

Our party skied between Essex and the Continental Divide today, on the S side of the highway. We found about 5 cm of this week's new snow along the road, and toured up to 7700'. We found the rain crust at 6400' but didn't try to look for it any higher. Skied glorious soft and smooth snow on a West face. Despite the sunshine the day felt cooler than expected given the light but fairly consistent breeze- never strong enough to transport snow. Surprised by how well preserved this week's snow was, even up high. At the end of the day there were rollerballs from trees shedding, around 6200' and below. I imagine lower S and W faces will have a crust tomorrow after the warmup... before it warms up again.

Route: Up Tunnel Creek to 5400'
Observation Summary: Lower East slopes of Grant were pretty blown out by the soaker cycle.
Instability Comments: Roller balls and a small D2 slide observed on a south facing solar aspect at 5500' around noon.

Route: WMR to Big slide to Haskill Basin road
Observation Summary: Dreamy, creamy turns. Then endless rollerballs and pinwheels. Real wet and sticky snow continued til approx. 4000ft
Weather Summary: Sunny
Instability Comments: 11:45 am witnessed a waterfall of rollerballs and pinwheels on the east side of the face, skiers left

Observation Summary: Headed up aiming to flirt with the edge of the first skook chute. Quick assessment at 5950' on a SE aspect on 25 degree slope found 160 cm HS and about 40 cm down was the very very stout Christmas crust. Pulled off column at that layer in the shovel sheer and CT27 RP in column test. It was right at noon and light fluff upper snow was fairly unaffected by sun. First time on this side and too early and ended up in elkweed instead of the first chute but okay.

Spent the day in the alpine above tunnel. While in rescue yesterday we found multiple breakable crusts in the surface snow but none of this was present here. We did find some wind board on exposed saddles and occasional moderate wind with some transport. Though the majority of east and north slopes are still skiing very well. The ice crust seems to be harder to find above 6k. No surface hoar seen. Impressive seeing the recent wet slide debris. We did not witness any point releases or wet slide.

Route: Road and trail/some ridge walking
Observation Summary: Serious short wave radiation going on until some thin clouds stopped/slowed the tree drippings at mid elevations. Open south facing slopes were getting moist but not too sticky by around 12 pm..  I guess Al Gore was right.
Weather Summary: Below freezing ambient temp until mid morning with a slight inversion.
Instability Comments: Harder to find than a parking spot in Larch lot

Observation Summary: Sunshine and calm to light winds made for a great day to get up high. Late Dec. crust was about 35 cm down, 2-3 cm thick, and pencil hard at my high point at about 7500'. Minimal, spotty surface hoar growth, mostly confined to glades above 5000'. Where the snow on top of the late Dec. crust isn't stiffened by the wind it's mostly facets, made for slippery skinning at times but great turns in sheltered areas, especially below 6000'. Wind affected snow showed no signs of instability (cracking).Plenty of D2s and at least three D3s that ran during the Christmas warm-up and rain in Paola Creek. Most of the activity was off the Tunnel Ridge side.
Weather Summary: Mostly sunny, calm to light winds.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed

Observation Summary: We visited a notoriously windy spot just west of Marias Pass to look for evidence of recent or current wind slab formation. No slabs were found, and only small amounts of wind-affected snow were noted on the surface. Excellent visibility provided beautiful views of the surrounding high peaks, where we did not observe any plumes of wind-blown snow. Our other objective was to gather evidence concerning the distribution of the Christmas Crust, which we found extended to at least 7500' in this area.
Weather Summary: Consistent light to moderate winds but zero wind transport. Warming temperatures and plenty of sunshine.

Observation Summary: South end of the whitefish range, east aspect, 6300’. Large whumpf and shifting felt under foot near edge of the slope.

From digging a pit nearly to ground on a S/SE aspect @ 7,400 ft we found results from an  ICT 17 sliding 85cm below the surface, with an overall depth of 250cm. On this aspect there was a fair amount of wind transport/cross loading creating this substantial slab sitting atop a faceted layer nearly 3ft down. While there was no obvious sign of instability on the surface, the deeper layer issue, and lack of visibility made us rethink our original objective, and go with a safer anchored/sloped pitch.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Gray
Instability Comments: Weak layers found lower in the snowpack when tested extensively.

Observation Summary: In the northern Flathead the cloud deck was pretty stubborn at 7k. Between 5500 and 7k we found about 30 cm of snow above the crust with variable breakable crust, which thankfully didn’t affect skiing too much. Minimal sluffing. Minimal wind. No surface hoar. Also notice some really interesting ripples in the snow, as seen in photo

Went out to big slide to try out a center punch test to report back to Rob. While not a multi day epic in the alpine, “it goes”, as they say. No signs of instability. No precip, no wind, obscured sky. Approx 20-30cm of recent snow atop the supportable crust. Could see old wet loose debris, but nothing recent.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: In the ping pong ball much of the day, no new precip, no wind.

Observation Summary: No signs of instability observed. No signs of slab formation on top of rain crust.
Weather Summary: The clouds never broke. Summit of Elk Mtn remained in clouds throughout the day. Swirling Wind gusts to ~20-30 mph were transporting snow at ridgelines. Wind transport of snow was intermittent with gusts.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed

Route: Beacon Park laps  and Canyon Creek
Observation Summary:
Got into the beacon park at WMR for monthly training.  Make use of these resources if you haven't yet! There is one located on the summit near the top of Chair 1.  There is a second park located roughly 2/3 of the way down Grey Wolf on the back side.  Both are accessible to snowmobiles.
-Followed training with a quick lap through the canyon to check on surface conditions, sluffing, and usage.  
-Found the area well utilized, with any remaining untouched snow sluffing easily on steep slopes.  However, these were small in size and easy to manage.
-Did not find surface hoar
Weather Summary: S-1 began around 1430 hours with cloud deck lowering to the valley bottom. Winds gusted from SW.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability.

Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: We enjoyed pleasant weather with broken skies, ambient temperatures of -2° C, and calm, gusting, light WSW wind.
Instability Comments: We did not observe any obvious signs of instability today aside from some small surface loose snow movement on a wind-loaded loaded steep roll over while descending.

Observation Summary: Beautiful day in the alpine. No wind. A bit warmer today with high clouds and a couple sunny holes throughout the day. Skied S, W/NW, and NE aspects. Skied NE from our high point of 7700'. All aspects we skied had dense pow ranging from 20-50 cms stuck onto underlying rain crust. Crust became much softer and almost nonexistent at times above 7550' on north face. West aspects had sun crust of variable thickness and hardness. Spotted one glide crack on south face at 6600'. Nothing moving even in steep terrain.

Played the game called "Avoid the Sun Crust" and had some success. We triggered a very small loose dry avalanche running on the rain crust in steep north facing terrain. It was very small due to being in small terrain, I can only assume bigger terrain will run much larger. Besides that, no instabilities to report.

Route: Approached on the road from the North Fork.  Ascended the SE ridge.
Observation Summary:
The skinning was the most laborious part of the day, as the lower elevations had 5-7 inches ontop of a supportable crust.
-The crust/ice layer was buried by 45 cm of Fist hard pow at 6900'.
-Just below the summit we found the crown of a D3 size avalanche that had occurred last week. The failure plane was a density change approx 100 cm from the ground and 70 cm from the surface. This was well below the dreaded ice layer from Christmas.
-Skiing was fantastic, we felt comfortable on steeper and more complex terrain.
Weather Summary: Grey bird at its best.  You couldn't see the debris piles until you hit them.  No snow or wind.  Pow is aging.
Instability Comments: The stability today was great.  We saw no signs of recent avalanches or instabilities.

Route: Snow Slip to Running Rabbit, out Goat Lick Path.
Observation Summary: We did not observe any signs of instability.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Visibility was limited

Observation Summary: Our group travelled on west through northeast facing slopes today and found the west facing slopes to be affected by a thin sun crust that mildly affected skiing quality. At our high point of 6200 feet (northeast slope) the snow height was 235 cm. We didn't dig too deep, but found that the super soaker crust was 5 cm thick at this elevation and buried by 60 cm of snow. The settled powder skied great on north facing slopes!
Weather Summary: Cloudy with on and off light flurries throughout the day. Above 5600 feet there was a light breeze coming from the west.

Route: To 7450 ft, in search of upper elevation pit site that wasn't wind drifted.
Observation Summary:
12/27 ice extends above 7450 ft in this drainage. 
The snowpack on this upper elevation SE facing slopes was well-consolidated above and below the ice. The 11/22 interface here is much less distinct and alarming than 1000-1500 ft lower; it looks more like the snowpack on a northerly aspect at that elevation. Early winter seems to have been too cold even for sun crusts at this elevation prior to yesterday (Monday 1/2).
Below ~ 5000 feet, the 15-25 cm of recent snow is faceting and sits on a stout crust. Once buried, that may pose a problem. 
The crust at the surface made for loud skiing and was easier to manage on northerly slopes
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Just a little too far north to get sun today. Foggy above ~5200 ft in AM; cloud bases lifted to ~ 6400 ft late afternoon. Mostly calm, except a few light and cold winds from the southwest above 7000 ft. S-1 at times. Cold.
Instability Comments: none

Observation Summary: Skied NE to NW terrain between 6800 and 4600 ft. More westerly terrain had a surface crust from yesterday’s solar but all else was 20-50 cm of dense dry powder fairly well glued to the rain crust. Little wind effect, mostly cloudy skies and early morning sun holes turned to light snow (2-3 cm) throughout the day. Sporadic surface hoar was slowly buried by the day’s flurries.

Route: •	Ski toured on ridge between Shed 10.7 and Shed 11. Continued up the SW ridge of Running Rabbit Mtn. to the South Summit. Descended Upper Shed 10 gallery into the Shed 10 Path proper.
Observation Summary: No obvious signs of instability observed.No active windloading. Signs of previous windloading evident on easterly/ northeasterly aspects.Facets are forming on the interface between most recent snow accumulation(s) (10-15”) and the Christmas rain crust. This crust is ~2” thick at 6500’ and then thinned to less than half an inch at our high point at 7300’.Surface Hoar, up to 8mm, was observed on shaded aspects between ~5,500 feet elevation and ~6,200No recent avalanche activity observed.Examined previous avalanche activity, already reported, in the Shed 10 Path. oThis avalanche, as previously reported, released in the Shed 10 Feeder Path on the evening of December 20/21st.This avalanche entrained dry snow, was fast moving, and terminated at 4500’ elevation above the rail grade (HS-N-R4-D2.5-O). o After today’s evaluation, this avalanche’s size was larger than originally estimated during highway observation on 12/21/22 and runout distance was also substantially greater than had been previously estimated.
Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with very light snowfall with a trace of accumulation throughout the day.•	Light SW winds above 5500’ elevation.•	Daytime highs reached into the 20s °F on the Canyon floor and into the mid-teens (0F) above 6000’ elevation.

Route: Up from Strawberry around Wildcat Lake
Observation Summary: We enjoyed skiing despite the crust finding that it was surprisingly skiable.
Weather Summary: A real beauty of day out in the Swans with very calm winds, cool temperatures, and clear skies that eventually transitioned to scattered cloud cover by the afternoon.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today. We could just make out a handful of crowns left over from the Xmas rain-on-snow event, but these were largely filled in with recent fallen and drifted snow. Debris piles could also be seen, but you had to look hard. The crowns were primarily observed below cliffs in steeper aprons or bowl terrain features.

Route: Toured up W ridge to 600' into E side
Observation Summary: Should have brought the ski crampons!  W aspects had widespread signs of wind scouring from 5000' to 7000' and difficult travel in steeper spots with 10-30cm over the Xmas ice crust.  Excellent travel on E side, surface snow faceting and staying fast.
Weather Summary: Mostly calm winds with chilly L-E developing in PM.
Instability Comments: No new avalanches or signs of instability.  1 glide crack or glide avalanche (12/26ish), 200' wide on E aspect at 6800', observed from across valley.

Route: Central Flathead Range
Observation Summary: Good day to be out. Was looking forward to seeing more carnage from the Super Soaker Cycle on our high peaks, but the abundant recent snowfall has disguised many of the events.
Weather Summary: Enjoyed calm sunny skies above a broken/patchy status layer.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability today. Skied four laps north through east facing up to 45 degrees (approximate max angle based on mapping products, not measured).

Route: Drove from Kalispell, went east along HWY2 ... oh, you meant the SKI route ... peaks in Fielding Creek.
Observation Summary: No avalanches, no signs of instability, lots of sun, lots of people, and two dogs ... on Shields ... in the park ...
Weather Summary: Temps. were just below freezing, no wind, and SUN -- south and west slopes were cooking by 1 p.m. up to 6,500 ft. (trees shedding/dripping, roller balls).
Instability Comments: None.

Route: Travelled to 6700 feet on south through west aspects.
Observation Summary: Started the day with some oh so sweet sunlight, ended in a pingpong ball. Overall great riding conditions.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Instability Comments:
-One test slope out of many resulted in small soft slab 4 inch deep by 10 feet breaking on interface within new snow running a harmless distance.
-Test results on SW aspects at 6500 feet resulted in ECTN22 score showing vertical fracture on rain crust 40 cm down.
-Found a particularly shallow location to dig and received ECTP20. This location was 40 degrees in steepness, had a good catchers mitt, and was 100 cm deep on a south aspect at 6200 feet. This location did not have a stout crust and consisted of basal facets with a soft slab sitting on top. Propagation happened within faceted snow.

Route: Rescue Creek to Penrose
Observation Summary: Conditions on shaded aspects were stable. Above 7000', solar south facing aspects were releasing tree bombs and the snow was somewhat heavier to 7800'.Observed some old debris (D2.5) that ran to the ground, presumably during the rain event.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: 28F at hwy 2 with clear skies in the morning. Cool temps throughout the rescue crk valley up to the inversion layer boundary at 7000' with above freezing temps higher. Calm, pleasant weather.
Instability Comments: None observed

Route: Ascended shaded aspects on a sunny day to ski steeper angled slopes below 6000'.
Observation Summary:
Forgotten boots squashed a day in the alpine. A sunny day in the alpine that is. After a long ride back and forth we punted to some low-hanging fruit closer to home. 
- No observed signs of instability below our high point of 6000 feet. 
- 6 to 8 inches of snow sits on the recent Christmas Crust. This snow is beginning to facet. 
- We observed wind-drifted snow in a drainage to the north, but felt no evidence of wind at our elevation.
- We skied down onto an older crown. This avalanche failed at the ground. The debris did not run far, but looked like it packed a punch. 
No signs of instability and a supportable crust made us feel comfortable skiing steep, avalanche terrain.

Route: South face of the peak just south of sixmile.
Observation Summary: Performed ECT with a result of ECTX. We wailed on the top for a bit and couldn't get it to go. I was able to get the block off by levering the shovel behind the column. It fractured at the same depth as the cord had cut. We did not see any avi activity. There were quite a few snowmobilers high pointing the SE face of sixmile peak. Most of the mountain above 6800 feet had a thick rime layer, but there were places where the wind was lower and a small amount of surface hoar had formed. Very little cornice growth observed at our location, but the main summit of sixmile may have had more. Lower elevations around 5000 ft were a little crusty on top of the fresh snow, presumably from some warming and refreeze.
Weather Summary: Cloud bands scattered between 6500 and 7000 feet.
Instability Comments: No instability observed

Observation Summary: We traveled to the east side of our advisory to a notoriously windy location. We chose this site to look for wind-affected snow to substantiate the wind slab problem. Winds appeared nearly non-existent over the past few days, and we did not find any fresh slabs. While there, we documented the distribution and strength of the Persistent Weak Layer at mid and upper elevations. We located the Persistent Weak Layer and found it to be rounding and strengthening in all three pits.
Weather Summary: Mostly cloudy with blue sky occasionally poking out on the east side of the Divide. No wind, even at 6900'
Instability Comments: Surface snow did not show signs of recent transport. Recently developed cornices cracked under the weight of a ski stomp.

Route: Up rescue creek, descended gully on east face of Penrose
Observation Summary: Persistent surface crust of variable width and strength on entire route. Below surface crust, snow was sugary and unconsolidated all the way to the rain crust. Cornices weren't particularly hard and we couldn't find any cohesive wind slabs, so we felt comfortable dropping off a steeper couloir on the east face of Penrose. Saw a few wet loose slides that had ran during the rain/warming cycle.
No wind, variable thin sun crust on solar aspects.  No wind slabs at elevation, no sluffing,. A lot of people out fulfilling their New Years resolutions

Route: The Notch down to Black lake and lakes N of Black
Observation Summary: Today felt like Low danger near treeline and Low below treeline based on terrain we observed.  No visibility into alpine.  Pretty fun fast skiing.  Settlement cracks widespread noted around treewells.
Weather Summary: Winds L-N.  Cloud ceiling lifting from around 6000' to 7000'.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability despite wailing on 35-40 degree small test slopes near treeline.

Observation Summary: Sledded up Lost Johnny and played around on SE to NE facing terrain between 5800 and 6600 ft. Snow quality improved dramatically above 6000 ft and the super soaker crust seemed to disappear above that. 30-45 cm of soft snow. Some wind effect on SE slopes. Super fun riding with great traction. Sidehilled a number of short but very steep (40-45 degree) east facing slopes and observed no cracking and minimal sluffing.

Route: Goulie to the top of Chicken Bones
Observation Summary: 125cm snow pack with the latest ice crust 40cm down. Crust was about a half a cm thick with some faceting rounds on top of it. It was non reactive in the CT and ECT.  ECTX at 6400', east aspect.
Weather Summary: Pleasant and calm

Observation Summary: Our big New Year’s resolution was to put in the skinner more frequently for people complaining about everyone out there getting after their new resolution. No precip while we were out, and barely any wind. Southern aspects around 6800ft showed evidence of the hand of the wind and various discontiguous rime crusts. While the new snow above the recent rain event crust is gathering density, it seemed well bonded and unreactive in all our tests and travels. We didn’t see much sloughing on steeper aspects while exiting into Dickey Creek. Impressive old wet debris in the floor of Dickey from the last storm.

Route: Ascended to 6400 feet on Nyack Mountain.
Observation Summary:
We went out to assess the distribution of the recent rain crust and see the sensitivity of new/recent snow on this crust. The recent rain crust is HARD and supportable up to 6400 feet. New snow heights (settled powder) varied between 8 inches to 2 feet depending on elevation.
- Observed no signs of instability. - Extended column test revealed no results.- We found isolated pockets of wind-affected snow at lower elevations, while everything above roughly 5500' had no wind effect. We also observed wind-textured surfaces on the south-facing side of the basin. We contributed this all to localized channeling of winds in the basin. Slabs were unreactive to ski cuts on very steep break overs. - At 6400 feet we dug to the ground to assess buried November Facets. Facets are large but clearly rounding. The weak layer was 1F hard. This layer is 270cm (8 ft 10 inches) below the surface at 6400 feet. 
Due to the depth of the November weak layer, the hard and supportable crust, and that new snow was unreactive in all tests, we felt comfortable skiing very steep terrain below 6500 feet. 
Photos later.
Weather Summary: Calm winds, overcast skies, below freezing temperatures.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.

Route: After skiing fast, dense pow on a couple South facing runs we climbed up and across the drainage. We noticed 2 large glide cracks directly above our ski tracks from the previous run.
Observation Summary: Good riding conditions on All aspects. Trail breaking was difficult at lower elevations on sleeper terrain because new loose snow wanted to slide off crust layer.
Weather Summary: High clouds made for a moody day. Calm winds and just below freezing temps made the day enjoyable.
Instability Comments: Saw 2 glide cracks stacked 1 above the other on Dorothy slide path.

Route: Toured in the drainage connecting Upper Whitefish Lake and Diamond Peak.
Observation Summary: We observed old (>48 hrs) slab and wet avalanches, a
Weather Summary: No snow. interstate clouds obscured views of surrounding terrain for much of the day
Instability Comments:
Observed evidence of wet slide cycle in the drainage connecting Upper Whitefish Lake and Diamond Peak.

Observed crowns from several (5-6) slab avalanches which appeared to occur > 48 hours ago on W facing slope at aprox 6,500 feet in steep rocky/ cliff band terrain in the Diamond Peak drainage above Upper Whitefish Lake. These were observed from afar - across the drainage and appeared to be D1-1.5 in size.

We observed no cracking, collapsing, and no active wind loading. Cornices have developed on ridge lines and lee slopes near ridge lines are clearly loaded from past wind events.

Observation Summary: Recreation day into terrain with no obs yet for the season. Minimized overhead hazard by moving uptrack from usual route through runouts/ tracks to edge of runouts because we didn't know how high the crusts/ ice extended. Skied upper elevation slopes less than 30 degrees with no overhead hazard. After test pit, skied some of tracks and runouts on descent. Fast, consistent skiing all elevation bands.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Some blue patches in the overcast late morning, some thin clouds with hints of blue in the afternoon. Light SW winds at upper elevations. No blowing snow or precip.
Instability Comments: No signs of instablility.

Route: Trail
Observation Summary: HS was 60-80 cm around 4000 ft and sunk the whole probe in (245 cm) at 7000 ft..  still need more but rain crust was buried under at least a foot or so at upper elevations.  Wind drifts/cornices on ridge line did not seem too sensitive but they were also not very big.
Weather Summary: S1-2 most of the day with fair visibility and moderate wind gusts on ridge tops featuring some dusts devils and light drifting.. glop factor of 2 out of 5 below 5000 ft but I was also lazy today and did not  leave the car until 930, so..

Observation Summary: Went out to the WMR sidecountry in search of soft snow in moderate angles. Found between 10-25cm of new atop the crust. Winds were light, with trace precip and temps below freezing. No signs of instability were noted while we were out.
No recent avalanche activity in any aspects.
Weather Summary: Cloudy with intermittent snowfall.
Instability Comments: None observed

Observation Summary: We performed an ECT with a result of ECTX, backing up the low danger forecast at this elevation and aspect. The wet slide debris from earlier this week has been covered by about 10 cm of new snow as well, making for decent skiing.
Instability Comments: No instability noted

Observation Summary: We observed light to moderate snow fall on-and-off throughout the day with light to moderate winds at times. Skiing was pretty fun up high and the crust became more apparent on our descent.
Temperature (F): 30 at Inn parking lot at 9 a.m.
Weather Summary: Light to moderate snowfall throughout the day. Light wind with gusts at times. Below freezing but felt warm.
Instability Comments: Unconsolidated, variable snow on top of a stout crust. Seems like a soft slab on top of the crust may have slid in 35+ terrain.

Route: Groomers to Canyon Crick.
Observation Summary: Instability? None whatsoever. Highmarkers? Yes.
Weather Summary: Temps. cautiously hovered around freezing, but stayed steady just below that ugly 32.
Instability Comments: None to mention.

Route: Central Flathead Range
Observation Summary: Today we selected terrain options on the North and East side of the compass. Partially due to good structure that our group observed yesterday on E facing, and partially due to bad structure on south facing (large depth hoar) observed by others elsewhere in the region. Our planned route took us onto a ridge that unfortunately was experiencing significant wind loading. (steady moderate breeze with strong gust out of the west). We skied one run through forested east facing at middle elevations to test the water. Our second run took us to higher elevations. We avoided an open NE facing bowl that was being actively loaded. We favored timbered N facing terrain until we were well below ridgeline, where we felt comfortable venturing into more open terrain. No signs of instability on either descent.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Broken skies and cool temps at upper elevations. Spring-like convective flurries deposited two or three inches throughout the day. At times snowfall intensified to an inch per hour, but it would only last 10 minutes or so. Ridgetop winds were moderate out of the west with strong gust. Upper elevations westerly slopes adjacent to ridgelines were stripped down to the Kris Kringle Krust.
Instability Comments: We observed one loose dry avalanche triggered naturally by a small cornice fall. We discovered the event around noon and the debris looked very fresh, so we expect that the slide occurred some time this morning. The cornice collapsed onto a NE facing slope at approximately 7000' and triggered a slough that ran at least 400 vertical feet. The cornice chunk was still visible below the terminus of the slough and had broken into a few pieces the size of maytag washing machines. There was not a discernable crown near the top of the slough, so there may not have been a wind slab pocket associated with this event despite significant loading that was occurring today on the same slope. Lower down on this same ridgeline, we stomped on numerous small cornices (none of which were above consequential terrain). These cornices cracked easily within the most recent layer of storm snow, but the older/denser portion of the cornice remained intact.

Observation Summary: No obvious signs of instability aside from some  isolated cracking that didn’t extend far in kick turns, and in ski turns below 6000’. Winds were calm all day, and vis was low so we didn’t see much terrain that we didn’t ski.
Instability Comments: Cracking on a few kick turns up higher in more wind-affected terrain.

Observation Summary: Several slides from days past; no instability in new snow outside of minor sluffing.

We observed no obvious signs of instability (i.e. no recent avalanches, cracking or collapsing).We observed a generally strong, deep snowpack with weak snow near the ground that is currently unreactive and appears to be strengthening. We found no wind slabs along ridges at 7000 ft. We observed minimal wind loading that only started later in the day. Large column stability tests resulted in only partial propagation (ECTN) near holiday crust with hard force.

Route: Multiple laps: WMR boundary- Canton Creek
Observation Summary: Weather and snow conditions matched forecast, but new snow throughout the day seemed to total 4-6in, which was more than predicted. At times snowfall rates were 1-2 in/ hour.We observed several skier triggered loose snow avalanches (sluffs) that were large enough to knock a skier over/ pin a person to a tree through the day. These were caused by unconsolidated/ loose snow on the surface and we found them easy to manage by avoiding obvious terrain traps and steep convex roll overs. Observed no signs of slab formation above the crust. All surface snow was unconsolidated.

Great foggy skiing in 25-35 cm mid density snow sitting on top of the rain crust on the SW face of Werner. Little wind except for ridge tops with 10-20 mph out of the SW. Dug a pit in a shallow angle (27 degrees) sheltered spot at 6300 ft to see what was happening beneath the ice crust. Total snow depth 150 cm, CT20 Q3 and ECTN17 at 30cm (new snow) not slabby enough to be much of a concern, 1 cm knife hard ice crust below that with 5 cm moist snow then another softer ice crust below that (ice crust sandwich), cold snow below that, nothing noteable until 120 cm down where the faceted November snow lurks. CT failed (teetered upon isolation) in one column and CT28 on another. ECTX on the same layer but we could pry off the whole slab without much effort. Skied great snow on 25-35 degree slopes. No cracking, settling or whumphing observed.

Route: Columbia falls to Middle Fork via reliable auto and skis
Observation Summary: Current stability seems good. No cause for concern in our snowpit today. We kept things pretty mellow though because that was our plan. Goals were to explore some new terrain, play it cool, and be grateful that the recent storm finished cold with nice powder. Best riding was found above 6000' on lower angle slopes. Steeper slopes weren't as good at hiding the crust. Feeling optimistic about the recent metamorphosis. Sky juice for the win!
Weather Summary: Cloudy skies, cool temps, steady light snowfall, and light winds from the SW throughout the day. 2-3 inches of new snow at upper elevations by days end. Around 2pm winds were increasing and some gentle snow transport was observed at ridgetop above 6000'
Instability Comments: Good stability observed today. Evidence of the "Saint Nick Soaker" cycle seen on numerous steep slopes at middle and lower elevations.

Route: Mid elevation terrain - mostly SE, W, N, and NW. Skinned one short pitch of 30+ slope on W aspect. Skied one well-buttressed 30+ slope with OK runout on SE. Avoided convexities. Comfortable digging pit on 30* N slope.
Observation Summary:
Explored a variety of aspects at mid elevations to assess extent and sensitivity of the 11/22 persistent slab structure, along with near-surface conditions after Santa's Super Soaker hosed us. Except it didn't. Barely felt the crust/ ice lens. Great riding.
But... the Christmas crust varies in hardness and thickness kinda randomly, and slopes with a shallow snowpack retain a distinct persistent slab structure.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Foggy, with cloud base 5-5500 ft. Calm until late afternoon, when light south winds started.  S-1 to S2 snow showers, peaking early afternoon. No evidence of wind drifting.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability. Except for those pesky column tests.

Observation Summary: Headed to Marion looking to keep things mellow and sniff out some soft snow. The supportable crust went to the top of Repeater Ridge, with 25-10cm of new snow atop it. We saw limited loose dry sloughing on steep micro terrain, but it didn’t carry onto middle 30s aspects. Winds were consistently light, with mod/strong gusts on ridgetops. Towards the end of our day, a lighter wind skin was starting to affect the surface. Sky was obscured, with 10cm of precip falling during our time out (S2 max). Temps were below freezing.

Dug a pit on a W aspect around 6600'. 2 meter deep snowpack with the November PWL buried a meter deep. The facets were very wet, they would stick into a ball when closing them inside your hand. 2 rain crusts were left from the rain storm. ECTX and we conducted a PST on the Nov Facets, the column propagated right at 50%. We were still not confident enough in this layer to stick our necks into avalanche terrain, but happy to see it starting to heal a bit. Kept our slope angles below 30 degrees on the way out.

Route: •	Ski tour into the bottom of Shed 6, then up the ridge between Sheds 5 and 4D, and onwards to Shed 7 East and West.
Observation Summary: •	Objectives included: get an up-close look at the avalanche activity that has occurred over the past 10 days, assess the current wind loading, examine the snowpack structure, and assess stability in this area.
Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with light snowfall and about 2” accumulation during the day.•	Moderate SW winds above 5500’ elevation.•	Daytime highs reached about freezing on the Canyon floor and the mid to low-20s °F above 6000’ elevation.
Instability Comments: Generally stable

Route: Just off of the road up to Blacktail Moutain.
Observation Summary: Overall we observed a reactive snowpack within our pit. Outside of the pit, we were able to create some collapsing and cracking but not without trying. Felt pretty unstable and kept our angles low but seems that healing is in the future.
Evidence of the large avalanche cycle with generally stable conditions since!
Weather Summary: Calm W winds throughout the day with overcast skies and the occasional sucker hole of sunshine.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability observed today in the post-rain event snow. We did observe evidence of the widespread avalanche cycle that occurred during the warm/wet/rain on snow events of the last few days.

Route: Up SW face to 7300 feet and descend by same route.
Observation Summary:
Overall impressed with quality of skiing considering the extemely variable weather over the last two weeks. 
         -Rain Crust from the Santa Soaker extends up to at least 7200 feet on SW aspect  At this elevation it was 0.5 cm thick and topped by  60 cm of recent snow.
         -Below ~7000 the crust was 10cm thick and not yet fully frozen with wet snow directly above and below.  
         `-No results on large column tests in pits at 7200 feet and 6500 ft.
More photos to come.
Weather Summary: Calm day.  Wind gusted from the West while on summit at 1300 hours.

Observation Summary: I enjoyed a day off holiday stroll looking at debris piles courtesy of the recent warm, wet weather. At first glance, it appeared that there was not a lot of avalanche activity associated with the storm in this drainage. It wasn't until I traveled on runout zones that I could determine the extent of the action. Most south-facing avalanche paths slid at least once during the storm and deposited fan-shaped debris piles, filling most of the runout zones. I did not travel on northerly terrain and can not verify they slid. Several large wet debris piles were easily identifiable from a distance on southerly aspects.
Weather Summary: Overcast skies throughout the day with flat light. Temperatures were near freezing at low elevations. and slightly cooler at mid-elevations. Light winds transported minimal snow at all elevation bands.
Instability Comments: The recent snow cracked under the weight of my skis in areas where the winds had drifted it. This snow varied between 4-8 inches thick and rested on a moist, weak crust.

Route: While ascending Whitefish Mtn resorts east uphill backcountry route along the top of Expressway, spotted a sizeable crown across the way in the neighborhood of Half Moon. WSW to W aspect, about 6000ft. Didn't see any evidence of human tracks near it; appeared to be natural release.
Observation Summary: Perhaps irrelevant, other than testing of the slope in observation, I witnessed two young adults ski the out of bounds slope with the small 10f crown. 1st skier went and stopped in a steep open spot about halfway down the run. Then skier 2 went and skied to the bottom of the run, where they both eventually gathered with hoots and excitement at the bottom of the slide path. Skier two said "that's right where we wanted to be," (assuming they started from near top of Goolie?). Standing from a safe zone about 50 yards away, I hollered over to ask if they realized they were standing in the middle an avalanche zone. Skier 1 (girl with a small backpack) responded, "oh yes, I have my beacon." Skier 2 (boy with no backpack) responded with a shrug of his shoulders. I proceeded to watch them (with one eye closed?) as they both further descended into the steep V shaped terrain trap that lies below Half Moon. I continued on my way.
Weather Summary: Overcast stable atmosphere, calm winds, with recent heavy warm snow event.
Instability Comments: No other instability observed, other than another much smaller crown (10ft) but similarly deep, on similar aspect and elevation, but further NW and on the other side of Half Moon (out of bounds open slopes easily viewed from bottom of chair 5)

Observation Summary: Obscured skies limited views of my objective, the slide paths on the west face of the Apgar Range.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)

Route: Up summer trail from notch; return.
Observation Summary:
In our limited travels, cornices at the ridge crests were the most glaring hazard. Little evidence of storm slab, no wet snow hazard, and persistent slab appears to be...napping. But those were very limited travels, with very limited visibility. 
Images and media to come.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Calm except at ridgeline, where winds were light with moderate gusts from the south-southwest. Foggy. Snow showers much of the day. S-1 to S1, with a few bursts of S2.
Instability Comments: Away from cornices, managed to crack a few hard, recently-formed drifts at the edge of tree wells. Otherwise, no cracking or collapsing. No sluffing on a steep, mid-elevation, northerly test slope.

Route: We traveled in the northern Swan Range to attempt to inventory any recent natural activity. We traveled various aspects and ascended low-angled ridgelines up to 7200'.
Observation Summary:
Today's riding conditions exceeded expectations. We found anywhere from 6 to 16 inches of right-side-up new snow. This new snow was dense and creamy to ride on.
- Observed a few natural wet snow avalanches at lower elevations. These ranged D1 to D1.5 in size. Generally small due to the terrain they were on. Our hope to see anything at upper elevations was shut down to exceptionally poor visibility. 
- Above 6000 feet where new snow depths neared roughly 8 inches we began to see cracking around our skis. These cracks traveled relatively short distances, but were significant enough to break soft storm slabs above our switchbacks.
- Our first audible collapse was at 6550' feet on a small sub-ridge. Between 6550' and ~7200' we felt 7 loud collapses. The most significant one was just below a summit on leeward slope at roughly 7200'. 
 
photos/video later.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Steady light snowfall. Periods of rime and graupel. Moderate winds on the Swan Crest at 7200'. Winds diminished significantly just off ridgelines.
Instability Comments: We triggered 7 LOUD collapses. These collapses traveled long distances away from us. We triggered them on northerly and southerly aspects between 6550' and 7140' in elevation. We observed cracking with nearly all collapses. We suspect these failed on November facets due to how wet the old snow was beneath the new snow, making the Dec 22 interface unlikely. We did not investigate the collapse above 7000 feet. I'm unsure what layer this may have failed on. We easily initiated shooting cracks and were able to trigger small slabs on unsupported slopes above kick turns in the skin track.

Route: Road observations from Hwy 2
Observation Summary: Ongoing rain and warm temps broke down the 12/24 rain crust and we had several wet loose avalanches below 5000' today with debris gouging to weak snow on  ground in several locations. These were generally limited to steep slopes (40 deg. plus). A few propagated as ragged edge slabs too, also failing on weak basal layers or the ground. Sizes were mostly D1-1.5 with a few D2s and one D2.5. Multiple events on steep cut banks above the Middle Fork, the rail grade, and Highway 2 with a couple larger slides in terrain higher above the Canyon.
Weather Summary: 0.45" rain overnight and another 0.80" today on the Canyon floor. Snow levels dropped to about 6000' around 1000 hrs and another 4-6" snow today up there with Moderate SW winds.

location: Blue Rock Cut, between Crystal and Stanton Creeks on US Highway 2
Observation Summary: Reported overnight.
Natural avalanches (12/26) reported from snowcat operator near Styker Ridge.
Weather Summary: It was raining at 6500’ and all the way up to 7100’. The temperatures increased with the elevations. At 3:30pm, it was 34 degrees at 4000’ and it climbed to 37 degrees at 7100’. 12/27: There was 2” overnight of new wet snow at 5500’.

Route: Road observations from Hwy 2
Observation Summary: Few more avalanches with a trend to larger magnitudes today. The slabs appear to be failing on the Dec 20th storm layer and not on the Nov. facets. But did not actually visit any crowns.Rain all day with 0.48" since 0600 and 1.27" total precip (mostly as rain) on the Canyon floor since the evening of the 24th. Freezing levels topped 7000' by noon today and it looked to be raining up to about 6000' with mixed precip above that.

Route: Road travel 3200-6400'.  Touring 5800-6400'.
Observation Summary: Despite limited visibility to ridgelines, we observed widespread rain-driven soft slab activity on 12/26.  With the added weight of the 8 or so inches of new snow by 12/28 capping the thick blue rain layer, the interface below the moist slab remained reactive.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Felt sustained Moderate winds night of 12/27 but did not observe wind-effected terrain.  Gust to M on 12/28.  Steadily dropping temperatures with intermittent precip often S-1 to S2.
Instability Comments: 1 small collapse on 2/26 at 6000' E. 1 large (felt 100' away) rumbling collapse on 2/27 on an E slope at 5800', initiated by stepping out of a ski and boot penetrating to waist in a rain runnel. Unsure if these failed on Nov PWL down 90cm or down 40cm just underneath moist snow.

Route: Traveled ridge to ghoulies. Descended to notch to get a closer look for any natural activity in Skook chutes. Overserved no avalanche activity.
Observation Summary:
I went out for a short day to assess snow surface conditions prior to a possible rain on snow event for tomorrow (Monday). 
- Quality riding is still found despite the warm temperatures and slightly inverted surface snow. I generally found about a 2 to 5 inch slab above softer snow. This slab was 4-finger hard and stiffer where winds drifted into a thicker slab.
- I was able to initiate some isolated cracking and able to get one small slab to fail on a wind drifted pillow. 
- 5000 feet appeared to be the transition zone where liquid precipiation turned to snow. 
- Surfaces at low elevations were moist -> wet. All dry above roughly 5300 feet. I observed no freezing rain crust.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Rain up to roughly 5100 feet at around 2pm. Snow showers with moderate rates above 6000 feet.
Instability Comments: Able to trigger some shooting cracks and one baby slab on a wind drifted rollover.

Route: Road observations from Hwy 2
Observation Summary: Rapid warm up and rain last night triggered some slab (up to size D2) and wet loose (up to size D1.5) avalanches last night/early this morning. The freezing rain formed a nasty 5-10 mm thick rain crust on the Canyon floor that capped the snow surface and suspect this limited the avalanche activity. At least for now. Uncertain to what elevation this crust extends? No large avalanches failing on the Nov facets. We'll see how it goes the next couple days.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Observed blowing and drifting snow above 6000' today, so thinking the crust may not extend that high. On the Canyon floor temps started just below freezing, but then warmed by late morning and had light rain for much of the day.

Route: Camas Rd. plus some schwack, bailed, forgot umbrellas/board shorts.If you have skis on your feet, but you don't ski, is it still skiing?
Observation Summary: Precipitation in the form of rain drops.
Weather Summary: It was raining. Obvious temp. inversion from "dang it's pretty warm-ish" at 3,200 ft. (gloppy snow) to dang-it's-noticeably-a-lot-warmer at 4,200 ft (totally saturated snow, trees shedding/dripping). No X-mas cookies for us. :(

Observation Summary: Temp inversion, 10 degrees in Essex and low 20s at 7,000 feet. Light to moderate winds at the ridge from the West and East, back and forth at around 14:00. Trail breaking was deep, and the low and mid elevation snowpack was more unconsolidated than 6-7k where winds have thickened the fresh snow. We kept our slope angles conservative which also made for slow travel.  No shooting cracks or wumpfing observed.
Instability Comments: Saw a couple crowns through the limited visibility on a steeper north aspect. Hard to tell timing of these crowns but they were 1-2 feet deep and did not look very wide.

Route: Toured retirement bowl and further down the ridge from Ghoulies.
Observation Summary: We were pleasantly surprised the freezing rain did not actualize in this area and there was no precipitation after the early morning snow. Winds were calm until picking up out of the southwest after 2pm. Saw minimal snow transported by wind, most of the surface snow seemed to need stronger winds to transport it due to it's density. There was 2-3 inches of new dense snow on top of the less dense snow from the previous cold front. We kept things mellow with the forecast and our decision making was verified by a large whoomphing collapse later in the day on the skintrack. I'll be hoping for no more large collapses this evening as Santa lands his sleigh on our rooftops.

Route: Up the trail, up to the ridge, repeat, out the trail.
Observation Summary:
Day off and warm enough to get out. Did not dig any profiles.
Thanks to Dr. S and his daughter for their community service breaking trail!
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Breath freezing on beard on the way up the nordic trails mid morning. Jacket damp from freezing rain on the way down. Distinct inversion, particularly as the day progressed. Felt a push of warm, humid air midday. Southwesterly winds picked up early afternoon, with some blowing snow - mostly out of trees. Snow surface above about 5500 feet already stiffened from previous wind/ warming so not much actual loading.
Instability Comments: No cracking in surface slab on slopes 30 to  35*; no whumpfing.

SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Light to moderate snow until 1500. Cold air temperatures.
Instability Comments: Today's storm slab was touchy and widespread at low elevations.

Route: Sixmile Lookout trail to south ridge to Sixmile Mountain summit. Descended SE face. Returned via skin track.
Observation Summary: HST 150 cm at 1982 m on NE aspect.
Weather Summary: Cold Wind transport observed on summit ridge. 7 cm total snowfall during 5 hour tour. Temp increased to -2 by end of tour at 1400.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability.

Route: Up highway 2 to Marias Pass and return.
Observation Summary: Took advantage of clear skies to scout slopes visible from Highway 2 for slides resulting from the midweek storm. Second objective to see if winds  associated with arctic front strafed mid elevation slopes. Yes to the slides; no to widespread mid elevation wind effects. Stayed in the truck except for Devil Creek study plot, where foot penetration is mid thigh despite some settlement.
Weather Summary: Clear, calm, and cold. temp at Devil Creek study Plot -35* at 1330. HS 68 cm. HS 89 cm in Essex. Temps along corridor generally -25 to -15*.

Observation Summary: An observation of the west face of Columbia Mountain from the valley floor along with wind-affected snow from overnight and current winds.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Wind speeds tapered off mid-morning through early afternoon. Winds increased in speed in the late afternoon transporting substantial snow.
Instability Comments: Recently formed wind slab consistently cracked underfoot. Cracks failed up to 18 inches below the surface on a layer of softer snow.

location: JFS Canyon - Running Rabbit and Snowslip Mountains
Route: Windshield observations from Hwy 2
Observation Summary: Observed three large (D2-2.5) avalanches that ran yesterday/last night during the storm. Also noted several small (D1) soft slabs, 3-5" thick, 10-40' wide that ran on steep cutbanks above the Hwy. Snowfall totals yesterday on the Canyon floor topped out at 22" with 0.78-1.00" water. Snowfall ended by about midnight last night.
Only a test!

Route: Out of WMR boundary to Old Man's then a couple laps towards the Canyon.
Observation Summary: A whole bunch of new snow and consistent snowfall throughout our tour.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: S1 - S2 snowfall throughout tour and minimal winds noticed.
Instability Comments: All instability seemed confined to small predictable loose dry sluffs on slopes steeper than 30* during our short tour. Wouldn't be surprised if storm slabs develop by the end of the day (or tomorrow) as it would only take a touch of wind or change in temperatures to shift the balance.

Route: Upper Whitefish Rd/ Stryker Ridge
Observation Summary: A slab that slid on the Stryker loop.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: High wind and cold
Instability Comments: Our groomer went out on Stryker Loop Last night and the was a 2 foot slab that slid on the trail in the section where they logged on the back side of the Stryker loop. Lot of fresh snow.

Observation Summary: Small loose dry slides and a snowpack that was behaving more like a storm slab.
Instability Comments: Loose dry problems abounded.

Observation Summary: Yummy cold smoke being transported by gusty winds forming cornices and soft slabs on ridges and north facing slopes.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Wind picked up as the day went on.
Instability Comments: Touchy storm slab on leeward slopes along ridge lines. Shooting cracks did not propagate far in the blower pow.

Route: The dog hair derby
Observation Summary: A whole lot of new snow out there. At least 2 feet and counting since Saturday. Dry and fluffy as can be, and similar to condition reports from Sunday, not a breath of wind today.We were able to enjoy ecstatic skiing in the deep powder snow on planar slopes less than 35 degrees. We stuck mostly to slopes 30 or less, but encountered a few short pitches of 30-35. Despite the very low density quality of the new snow. It was not easy to make turns on slopes less than 25 degrees. Downhill trenching was its own kind of fun though.We did not observe or trigger any slides, but we were also intentionally avoiding terrain steep enough to produce. Mission accomplished. But given the rapid loading and signs of instability, I would be shocked if there were not sizable loose dry sloughs and storm slabs initiated across the range today.Camas Road Temporarily Closed as of 530pm-12/20.
Weather Summary: Cloudy and Cold. Very heavy snowfall most of the day and especially after 4pm. Practically windless all the way to our ridgetop highpoint of 5900'By contrast, winds were raging on the canyon floor, especially near badrock. I thought we might have taken a wrong turn and ended up in Browning, Montana.
Instability Comments: Widespread storm slab instabilities due to significant rapid loading. 12+ inches accumulated at 3800' between 9am and 5pm (1.5 inches per hour). Would estimate up to 2 inches per hour at mid elevations (2-3 feet accumulated since Sunday) Short cracks and localized sloughing appeared with nearly every step by late afternoon. We remotely triggered small sloughs and mini fractures on nearby pillows on logs, trees, rootwads, etc. Also watched similar activity initiated by tree plops. Slopes nearing 35 were showing ripples from shear stress by late afternoon (picture attached).

Route: Laps off ghouly point
Observation Summary: Snow still seems mostly unconsolidated and boot,ski,pole penetration is.. most of the snowpack in many places.  Wasn’t really near any steep slopes and didn’t feel the need to be.
Weather Summary: Frost nip danger moderate today

Route: North ridge of Peak 6437', Noisy basin flats on the snowmo. 1-4pm quick look.
Observation Summary: Persistent slab still lurking as top problem. In the afternoon, storm snow was in its transition, loose dry not running far in but still not settled into a slab as most kick turn-corners indicated. Limited physical tests with just a few hundred foot ridge ski. jI wanted to see the 1.5-2 week old avalanches in Crown Bowl more closely than through the trees, but didn't like the idea of entering that terrain with the overhead hazard. Zoomed in photo will do.More good riding, enjoy it before Wednesday!
Weather Summary: Surprisingly calm winds but report from other party was that ridgetop winds on Aeneas were whipping, probably moderate.
Instability Comments:
One small collapse along windward side of N-S ridge at 6000', seemed like a shallow spot and sure enough. Dug 50' away where I did not think the collapse had extended to. HS 106
Other party who did a much larger tour to radio repeater did not experience collapsing or cracking, no new avalanches observed.

Route: Too early
Observation Summary: Loose snow avalanches were the main concern. The top 6" moved easily, naturally and while skinning then would entrain more snow available for sluffs. Overall small sluffs again because we were not in open terrain.
Weather Summary: 6-8 " of new dry snow accumulated overnight.Snowed 4 inches while we were out.Light wind in Wahoo drainage Snow picked up on our way out!
Instability Comments:
If it wasn't for the bush anchors we would not have felt safe where we were skinning.
Lots of sluffing, natural point release sluff avalanches would run until it was terminated by the alders.
No cracking, lots of sluff.

Observation Summary: Steady snow fall and for the most part fairly light wind with occasional gusts and minimal transport. We certainly felt no evidence of wind slab or drifting while skiing. About 6-8” of glorious snow that liked to sluff . Storm intensified as the day went on

Route: 300 ft uphill of forest service road below Elkcalf mountain
Observation Summary: Performed ECT with a result of ECTP 21. The depth hoar at ground level failed and propagated across the entire column. If something were to go in this area and aspect it could bring down the whole seasons snowpack of about 90 cm in this area.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Observation Summary: See above
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: 5-6 inches of very low density snow accumulated at 4000 ft. at parked truck in the app. 5 hours we were skiing. Blower!!!!
Instability Comments:
We intentionally triggered two very small and very soft slab avalanches on small test slopes on the leeward side of a ridge. Both avalanches were 3 to 4-inch thick and only ran about 10 ft. vertical because of low slope angle (see image). Sluffs were easy to trigger and would likely have run far had we chosen steeper slopes.
We experienced 3 very localized and barely audible collapses on an ESE facing slope with shallow (less than 3 ft.) snowpack around 6000 ft.. Localized cracking in one location on same slope.

Observation Summary:
Blower. Over the head. Where is the snorkel? Choking on face shots. All of those sayings applied to today. We were thrilled to see snowfall accumulations exceed forecast totals. Roughly 8 inches of snow fell throughout the day without a breath of wind. Hard to imagine after driving in and out of Badrock Canyon. 
 - New snow sluffed easily in steep terrain. 
 - A soft slab problem started to form later in the day. We triggered one small soft slab on a benign piece of terrain. 
 - November facet layer still showed unstable results in a pit on a NE aspect at middle elevations.
We stuck to mellow slope angles above roughly 5500'. Below that elevation, we felt comfortable skiing steeper terrain due to the lack of a slab above the November facet layer.  We lucked out and found some bushless low-elevation slopes to ride on.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Moderate (S2) snowfall rates for most of the day. Winds calm.
Instability Comments: Felt one audible collapse on a south aspect at around 5400 feet. It was a localized collapse that my partner did not feel or hear 20 yards away.

Route: Up Canyon Creek, in the general area of whiteout east of the radio towers.
Observation Summary: ECT 12 showed slab falling off at facet layer which was 40cm down out of 95cm snow pack.
New or Recent Snowfall (cm): 2in in last 24hr snowing on our way out.

Observation Summary: We headed to Sixmile keen to keep our angles low and spirits high. Skies were overcast or obscured. We had some precip, but not more than a couple Cms. Winds were light, except on the ridge tops, where they were moderate from the SW. Temps dropped during the day but were below freezing at all times. We got only one isolated collapse around 6000 ft on a SSW aspect. A pit on the same aspect at 6200ft was unremarkable, with no propagation or much in the way of failure between more recent layers and the facets beneath. (CT14 Q3 15cm down, ECTN 14 same layer) North aspects over the upper ridges showed strong evidence of lots of wind before the last few inches fell; all of this lighter snow will be available for transport with the incoming storm. At lower elevations, there’s lots to dodge if you’re not on the trail.

No collapses, no instability with skiing. About 1” of new snow during the afternoon with minimal wind. Dug one pit, depth 100 cm at 6k.  No propagation. The double crust but seems to be breaking down, or at least less pronounced compared to last week. Bring on the next storm…

Route: Parked at the dam, rode up Dorris/Silver, across Otilla to Hash then stayed lower in elevation on the way to Strawberry.
Observation Summary: Isolated snowpack collapse, upper elevation, leeward slope, N-NE facing aspect.
Weather Summary: Noticed a temp drop mid day, overcast, minimal wind.
Instability Comments: Another rider with a stuck sled, while wiggling the sled free I experienced a woompf about 15-20 feet away.

Route: Huckleberry Trail to Ridge
Observation Summary: Quite pleasant snow conditions overall. We stuck to a ridge ski out to the summer trail where it crosses a saddle. The new snow was extremely low density and will probably start to move with a bit of wind.
Weather Summary: Overcast, winds calm to light from SW, light snow.
Instability Comments: No signs of instability observed.

Route: The Fire Mosaic Traverse
Observation Summary: We traveled on the shady side of the compass today. Climbing and skiing North through East facing slopes. We were surprised to find our famed weak layer of November unreactive in pit test. We isolated 3 long columns with no results on this layer. Not satisfied or convinced by that outcome, we conducted a propagation saw test that collapsed the weak layer after 40cm, but only traveled 27cm before arresting the failure.
