We stuck to our original plan of skiing slopes at or less than 30 degrees given the poor structure that is evident. Perfect low angle conditions out there. 4-5 inches of delightful dry powder on a supportable base. It's wicked fast and fun.
Weather Summary: Calm cloudy day in the mountains. Light snowfall all day with very light winds from the west. 24 hour snow amounted to about 1 inch by days end. Ridgetop temps hovered around 16 F
Instability Comments: This was my first tour this season where I observed no obvious signs of instability. We dug a full profile on an ENE facing slope at 5800' and interrogated our persistent weak layer (@43cm) like a mob boss, but couldn't get it to talk today. (ECTN27 @ 93cm, ECTX, ECTX, and PST 40/100 (Arr). The overall structure still looks fishy, so maybe this particular pit wasn't a snitch? Curious what others found out there today?

Route: Went out in search of sheltered, sub 35 degree skiing. Dug a pit and enjoyed some great, soft turns
Observation Summary: Snow surface and vegetation are covered in rime above 5, as well as PP in top 6 cm of snowpack above 6000'. We noted a sun crust several cm under the surface of the snow, isolated in exposed areas away from trees below 6000'.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: We were surprised with near-constant light flurries throughout the day, becoming heavier and fluffier as we descended at 3pm, with about 1-3 cm of new snow observed. Winds were calm to light and from the SW.
Instability Comments: Little indication of instability on our route. November facet layer lay 60 cm below the surface of the snow and did not fail during the non-"wail on it and see if it breaks" portion of our instability tests. No whumpfing, collapses, cracking, or recent avalanche activity observed.

Route: Ghoulies to Old Man's
Observation Summary: High quality snow sliding.  No red flags but same universal poor structure as revealed by sticking a pole handle to ground at mid elevations or a probe at upper elevations.  Right side up until the bottom gives way.
Weather Summary: S1-S2 0800-1100h, then S-1 flurries rest of day.  L-SW winds, no signs of transport.
Instability Comments: Did not observe any recent avalanches or signs of instability.

Observation Summary: We kept it short and sweet today with a quick ghoulie's lap. We did notice some large sparkly surface hoar around 6600 ft on the south to southeast slopes we were on. We began noticing it a few hundred feet below 6600, and the crystals seemed to grow as we ascended. We estimated the largest ones to be around 1 cm long. Great riding conditions!
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Periods of overcast and patches of blue skies. Light winds coming from northwest. No overnight precipitation.

Route: Up west-facing ridge to point near crest of Flathead Range
Observation Summary: Aimed to get up high in the Flathead Range and start getting a feel for upper elevation conditions. Didn't tangle with shady slopes except where treed, because slope angles too steep. Whumpfing collapses kept us on edge and on our toes ascending. Stuck to slopes less than 30 degrees except 2 short pitches with small consequences. Shallow, sometimes unsupportable snow kept us on edge and light on our boards descending.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Light snow - S-1 to S1  with obscured skies until early afternoon. Then decreasing clouds, a few breaks, and some valley fog. Remarkably, only a few breaths of wind.
Instability Comments: Radial cracks (mostly < 1m, a few to 2m) while breaking trail at low and mid-elevations. Widespread collapses between about 5500 to 6500 feet, with a dozen or so audibly rolling long distances. A couple heart stoppers. Collapses seemed most frequent when height of snow 50 to ~80 cm.  Few whumpfs on shady side of ridge, though this terrain more treed.

Route: Ascended a low-angle southerly ridge and returned via the same route.
Observation Summary:
We poked around to see if we could find a melt-freeze crust from the 12/11 warm-up while keeping an eye out for wind slab: 
-The crust seemed pretty spotty at lower and mid elevations.  It was most noticeable in a clearing at 5600’.  Here it was 1/2" thick and covered by 3" of snow. It broke easily under our skis. -Winds were calm and we observed no signs of recent wind loading.-We found no obvious signs of instability.
In the end one quiet day was not enough to change our behavior and we chose to descend a low-angle ridge.
Weather Summary: Visibility varied.  Clouds began to break at 1600 hrs.
Instability Comments: No obvious signs of instability.  This route has seen a lot of snowmobile traffic.  It is possible that the weak layer had already been collapsed.

Route: 4000-6800' on W and NW slopes
Observation Summary: Excellent skiing on planar 25-33 degree terrain.  We saw the two crowns previously reported in Crown Bowl in Noisy Basin from afar.  They looked massive but no other slides observed.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Remarkably calm winds up to L SW around the ridge.  Obscured early in day, S-1, turning to Broken mid afternoon.
Instability Comments: Only got 1 collapse at 5500' on a NW aspect with two people feeling it on the skintrack.  The height of snow was 80 cm right there while other spots at that elevation band were around 130cm HS.

Observation Summary: After spooky results in a SE facing pit last week I wanted to see what the north aspects looked like.  Skied down to Big Trees and did a quick column test at 5800' on a North facing slope and got a CT21 Q2 at about 12" down. Snowpack total was 145cm. No noticeable crust like the south facing slopes. No signs of instability. Just great skiing!
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Route: •	Road observations and a ski tour up the Shed 11/10.7 ridge and higher to the upper starting zones of Shed 10.7 and Path 1163.
Observation Summary: •	No recent avalanche activity observed.•	It was hard to confirm from a distance, but it appears the large avalanche on the SE face of Snowshed Mtn (observed on Dec. 4th) ran full path to Sheep Creek.•	Fresh crop of surface hoar growing on the snowpack surface starting around 5500’ and becoming more developed and widespread as we climbed higher.•	Dug a snowpit and conducted a full snowpack profile on the ridge just west of Path 1163 at about 7000’ elevation (see pit profile). Poor snowpack structure here with two propagating results in an Extended Column tests on the Nov. facets about 30” down. Also, a second propagating result about 10” down on the 2nd test.•	Multiple hasty and hand pits revealed the same story here as in the eastern end of the Program Area – a widespread poor snowpack structure with a consolidating slab 1-3+ feet thick resting on top of a sometimes-reactive layer of facets formed back in November.
Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with occasional very light snowfall. Winds were calm or occasionally light from the west.•	Daytime highs reached the low-20s °F on the Canyon floor and the high teens °F above 6000’ elevation.
Instability Comments:
• We ski cut multiple wind-loaded test slopes above 5000’ on the Shed 11 ridge resulting in several short (2-6’ long) shooting cracks in the uppermost 4-8” of snow consisting of recent wind slab (photo).
• One “rolling thunder” audible collapse that carried a couple hundred feet across a slope at 6800’ on a southerly aspect on the ridge just west of the Shed 10.7 path.

Observation Summary: Deceptive snowpack. Supportable, with shallow ski penetration, so feels solid and gives little feedback of instability. Structure, however, remains problematic. Consolidating slab over rounding facets that propagate in tests, sometimes irregularly. Shallow patches could be likely trigger points. And this time of year, plenty remain.
Weather Summary: Blustery N winds with shallow drifts on ski hill, some dunes on ridge to Gooleys. Mild in sun on sheltered slopes. Snow surface wind packed from previous SW winds.
Instability Comments: 1 small collapse.

Observation Summary: Gathered with some folks to ski a few laps off of ghoulies point. Explored some fresh pow over near big trees and Kona trees. We heard whoomphing several times throughout  the day; the collapsing did not appear to be specific to aspect but was found in a number of places throughout our travels. Our intention was to ski mellow pow and low angle terrain with our current understanding of the snowpack and potential instability. Whoomphing was the primary warning sign observed for the day.

Route: WMR- Skook via typical summer trail route
Observation Summary: Before we left the house today we made a plan to keep things "chill" by avoiding terrain steeper than 33'. We discussed managing convexities and planned to go to a zone where we would not be tempted into or exposed to steeper terrain. Our observations matched recent observations and forecasts. We observed collapsing (whoomphing) in the snow, and found the Nov facets to be a reactive layer in a snowpack test.We enjoyed checking out the facets, skiing some mighty fine POW, farming a slope, and listening to other parties converse on the radios. An enjoyable, stress free, day in the woods was had.
Weather Summary: S1, light snow
Instability Comments: Observed collapsing (whoomfing) from the skin track. Other parties reported hearing whoomfing, too.

Route: Warm temps in the morning made the snow down low feel a bit manky on the snow machine ride in, but when we parked at camp misery it was fluffy again. Skied n facing runs between 7200 and 5800'
Observation Summary: With new snow and moderate winds this weekend we have been cautious of finding the Tipping point in our snowpack.Been enjoying the great conditions
Weather Summary: Light windsLight snow
Instability Comments:
2 large crowns in crown bowl, no much debris below it but around 3' deep and 50-100 feet wide.
Noted 2 audible collapses while touring.
No cracking while skinning or skiing.

Route: We skied off a ridge off Huckleberry Mountain in GNP.
Observation Summary: Mostly, we found some great turns, good coverage, and some surprise good views.
Weather Summary: The clouds were multilayered and the gray gave way to some blue skies briefly. There was some snowfall throughout the day but little to no accumulation. Very calm day on the ridge with no wind to mention.
Instability Comments: We heard one small, very isolated whoomph down in a drainage close to the trail. No whoomphing, cracking, or much reactivity on the ridge or where we skied just off the ridge. We got a wind slab to move from ski cutting small test slopes but without propagation. We did have some slough to manage while making turns. Some wind scouring and some forming cornices on the ridge line (many of which had been knocked or released naturally prior to our arrival, none with consequence).

Route: Summer trail mostly
Observation Summary: S1 most of day with ridge top winds transporting new snow onto leeward (NW) slopes.  Visibility slowly deteriorated  from very good to pretty good.
Weather Summary: Normal Montana fall weather.
Instability Comments: The instabilities were definitely around but we’re not jumping out at me.  Stomped through a wind loaded test slope and observed no cracking, collapsing or anything.  Did get a CT21 on the November facet layer right in the middle of the pack.  Slope was NW facing at about 6200 feet.  HS was 132 cm and column fractured 70 cm below that (62cm from ground).

Route: Gotta pay to play and don't get night bushed without a headlamp.
Observation Summary: Close call skiing SE facing up to 35 degrees. We made plans to consider skiing slightly steeper terrain today, while aiming to utilize terrain features (ribs ridges, etc) and apparent anchors (tree stands) to theoretically decrease our level of exposure to the Persistent Slab problem. After experiencing a worrisome whumpf during our first descent, our confidence was shook along with powder pillows on trees up to 200 feet away. On our second lap we utilized slope angle as our primary margin of safety control which was a lot more effective than our previous tactics. Good news is that the powder is equally nice across many slopes right now.
Weather Summary: Mostly Overcast day with light snow/mist in the morning and moderate snowfall briefly late afternoon. Light/moderate blustery winds from the SSW above 5800'.
Instability Comments: Our party experienced a few small collapses at lower and middle elevations today and one BIG loud collapse that shook trees up to 200 feet away. No associated cracks on the slope observed after this event. The pin point map location of this observation indicates the location of this whumpf (approximately 5900-6000 feet, SE Facing). This occurred while our group was gathering at a spot with good visibility of our descent plan. We managed to carefully pick our way down a rib 25-35 degrees without further incident, but in hindsight would have had a much better time sticking to slopes less than 30 degrees without overhead. We proved this theory on our second lap and wished that we had just stuck to the avalanche doctor's prescription this morning.

Route: Enjoyed a rare, quiet weekend day at Marion Lake Backcountry resort.Our party traveled up the Marion trail to repeater ridge.Skied N facing and S facing out.
Observation Summary: Over the past 2 days touring in the MF the snow has appeared to have settled out some The new snow over the past 5 days has drifted into a slab at higher elevations, but stubborn to trigger. The riding has been good on all aspects besides exposed ridgelines. We were able to stomp on some small cornices to put some weight on the slope below with no outcomes.
Weather Summary: Mostly overcast skies, sun popped out just couple of times, wind was light till 1500 then winds picked up and it started snowing moderately. Really hope we pick up an inch overnight.
Instability Comments:
No signs of instability.
No cracking or whoomphing.

Route: It involved lots bush dodging, log clambering, and hole wallowing. And eventually, great low angle powder.
Observation Summary: Day off tour in terrain that's rarely accesible this time of year, though "accessible" is relative. Avoided slopes more than 32*; slope cut adjacent to/ at the edge of crossloaded NE-facing slope about that steepness produced no cracks or collapses. Didn't dig, except to dig myself out of holes around logs.
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: Showery precip off and on all day. Mostly S-1 snow showers. Some freezing mist - not rime - at ridgelines early afternoon. Gusty south winds with some blowing snow at ridgelines.  6-7" new snow since 12/5. About an inch overnight and through the day.
Instability Comments: A few whumps in low elevation openings. 3  whumpfs on the windward (S) side of a ridge around 5200', one on the 9th person across that section of skin track. A few short radial cracks (1/2 ski length) while breaking trail.

Observation Summary: "Spendin' time on me"--yes, the whumpfs certainly are making us do that. Toured in the WMR adjacent backcountry looking to keep angles mellow. Lots of whumpfs in widespread distribution with no observed cracking. A minimal wind skin was developing at higher relative elevations from the light southwesterlies that were blowing through most of our tour. West aspects were more sheltered. Sky was overcast, with light to no precip and below freezing all day.
Instability Comments: My whumpfs, my whumphs my whumphs my whumpfs--check it out. -Fergie in Avy 1

Observation Summary: Compared to skiing at Marion a couple days ago the snow pack seems much more settled here, with depth of only 100 cm at 6k and one hasty pit in SE aspect did not reveal the double crust layer. Intermittent snow squalls and limited wind made for a calm day of skiing. Stark contrast to the car vs bus collision we came across on the at home !

Route: South of WMR. Altitude range from 6,600 Ft to 5,700 Ft.
Observation Summary: Pretty stable conditions on slopes less than 35 degrees. On top of the ridges you clearly saw the influence of South/South West winds, which most likely loaded North/North East aspects, which I avoided, as most likely they would fail on steeper slopes.
Weather Summary: Light winds from the SE and temps between 25 to 28 degrees. Small snowfall started at 2PM.
Instability Comments: No instability observed on South, East and South West aspects. North aspects were loaded with snow drifted by the wind, however didn’t test them.

Route: Up and down Shed 6/7 shoulder to dig pit profiles on S and N aspects of Snowslip ridgeline.
Observation Summary: Lower elevation conditions have vastly improved from last week with a supportable base of snow beneath overnight new snowfall - making bush skinning much more efficient and bush skiing far less terrifying. Travel is still best above 5000'.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Light gusting moderate S winds and S-1 to S1+ snowfall with mixed graupel forms throughout our tour from 10 am to 230pm.
Instability Comments: We observed one small cornice fall avalanche on the Shed 7 West path (D1-R1) while ascending the ridge, found shooting cracks in preferentially wind loaded pockets and initiated 3 audible snowpack collapses in isolated rocky areas.

Route: Was that first skinner off the nordic trail connecting to the goods?  Took the summer trail, skied SE slopes and sampled first 300' of a N rib (gets thick).
Observation Summary: Excellent riding with one sign of instability. Traveled in 25-35deg terrain.  Off and on good visibility under cloud ceiling and no naturals observed in 40-50deg N terrain across the valley.  Felt like a moderate hazard day with our persistent slab.
Weather Summary: Mod SW winds on ridgeline.  Brief but intense large graupel ~4mm+.
Instability Comments: Notably only one collapse felt at 6300' SE and we passed through many openings with similar characteristics.

Route: Went out in Apgar to dig a pit and gauge our current level of entrenchment. Our initially planned route included traveling underneath some potential avalanche terrain, so we took our plan B and left the hiking trail to skin up a sub-35 degree ridge as soon as we encountered the faceted weak layer from November, which was fairly widespread above 4600' on our SE-SW face. We continued to ascend until our pit site at 5300', during which we experienced some isolated whumpfing. After doing a full pit, we returned to the trailhead.
Observation Summary: Only Class 1 data was very isolated whumpfing on SW-SE slopes. Very small collapses, but absolutely worth noting, especially as it confirms our current avalanche problem. Pit tests showed that this faceted weak layer (buried about 50 cm down at 5300' on a SE slope) is still easily reactive.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: 5-15 cm of new snow, with SW winds L gusting M and capable of transporting snow, although we did not visually observe blowing snow. Temperatures ranged from .5 C at 3800' to -3 C at 5300'. Skies ranged from broken to overcast, with occasional light snow at S-1.
Instability Comments: Isolated whumpfing, with minimal collapse, noted between 4600' and 5300' on SW-SE slope. No recent avalanches observed, although due to the exposure on our planned route we were not able to get into upper elevations to observe more established avalanche paths. No evidence of wind loading on our route -- although with L SW winds gusting to M, and 5-15 cm of new snow from last night available for transport we would expect wind loading to occur on leeward and crossloading aspects.

Observation Summary: I went for a tour on the eastern edge of our advisory area with a Glacier Park Ranger. I chose this location to observe the wind slab distribution and sensitivity in an area known for its winds. This was FAC's first chance to observe the snowpack structure, including the faceted crust, in southern Glacier Park.
Weather Summary: Snowed off and on throughout the day with 2" accumulation. Moderate winds in the morning with a moderate amount of wind loading at all elevations.
Instability Comments: Cracking was common below 6000' in wind-sheltered locations. Cracks extended down to the faceted crust 12-16" below the surface. Cracking was widespread in fresh wind slabs formed overnight and through the morning at all elevations. Only 1 collapse was observed at a sheltered mid-elevation site.

Route: Goulie to top of Chicken Bones
Observation Summary: Dug a pit at the top of Chicken Bones, SE aspect 6500'.  90cm snowpack with the Nov. crust right in the middle of it. 45cm down had an ECTPV. That's right, the whole block slid right off the crust as soon as my cord cut through it.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)

Route: •	Road observations and a ski tour up the Shed 6/7 ridge then up and over into the Shed 5 path.
Observation Summary: •	No avalanche activity observed.•	We ski cut several wind-loaded test slopes on the Shed 6/7 ridge and all but one was unreactive with only 4’ long shooting crack (photo). This was in a fresh wind slab at around 5200’ elevation on an easterly aspect. No collapses today. •	Dug a snowpit and conducted a partial snowpack profile in the upper reaches of the Shed 5 avalanche path (see pit profile). Found a poor snowpack structure with a layer of buried surface hoar (observed on the surface on Dec. 4th) about 12” down and the Nov. facets layer of despair 28” down.•	Extended Column Tests resulted in one propagating and one non-propagating result with moderate force on the surface hoar and the Nov. facets propagated twice with hard force.•	Multiple hasty and hand pits revealed the same story – the poor snowpack structure appears to be widespread with a consolidating slab 1-3+ feet thick resting on top of a sometimes-reactive layer of facets formed back in November.
Weather Summary: •	Overcast skies with occasional light snowfall. Winds on the Canyon floor were light from the west. Winds above 5000’ were light to moderate from the southwest and were occasionally transporting snow.•	Daytime highs reached the high 20s °F on the Canyon floor and the high teens °F around 6000’ elevation.

Route: Keep recycling that skinner to Marion until it's buried!
Observation Summary: Notes- 30 degrees at 4 p.m. at the Walton, thankfully temps. didn't hit the "magic mark" even at the canyon bottom - Heavy snow from 7-4, hard to get a read on how much due to blustery winds, but no less than 6 in. - Last week's snow on exposed southern aspects got pretty fried Sunday apparently, now a district dense slab ... with about a foot-and-a-half of new on top- Winds were gusty and swirling, a lot of snow transport, but seemingly in every direction (skin track half-filled after each lap)- Some shooting cracks in the new snow (wind skin), but nothing moving downhill ... yet
Weather Summary: Stormy.
Instability Comments: Shooting cracks in the storm slab, but nothing moving downhill (much).

Observation Summary: We went to the Whitefish Range to better understand the snowpack and investigate a reported avalanche east of WMR. We've been tracking a Persistent Slab problem and today confirmed that the PWL is still a player in the Whitefish Range. The avalanche we investigated failed on a steep, convex rollover just below a ridgeline. It was large enough to bury, injure, or kill a person. We spent the remainder of the day digging hasty pits on various mid-elevation slopes. These pits confirmed that this weak snowpack structure is widespread in the area we traveled.  We were conservative, skiing slopes 30 degrees or less, but more importantly, it was powder.
Weather Summary: We traveled in mostly sheltered terrain and observed only small amounts of wind transport.  Light snow fell throughout the day.
Instability Comments: Low visibility prevented us from seeing the surrounding terrain. Cracking was noted only at ridgelines open to the wind.

Route: Traveled to 7000 feet on south facing ridge line.
Observation Summary: Overnight low density snowfall made for great riding conditions in low angle/wind protected terrain. Collapses in the snowpack were frequent displaying long shooting cracks over 6 feet in locations and low rumbling whomps. Winds were moderate and easily transporting low density snow onto north and east aspects. Kept to slopes <30 degrees and had lots of great turns.
Weather Summary: Overcast, S1 most day (10:00-16:00). 12 F at Six Mile Trailhead with overnight snowfall 10 cm at 4200 ft. Winds moderate above 6000 feet from SW. Hard to estimate new snow above 7000 feet due to wind effect. 50 cm ski pen in locations.
Instability Comments:
-Loud rumbling whomps were frequent.
-Cracking observed while traveling over loaded pockets and in conjunction with slab collapse.

Route: Road riding BTL OAA hip hop hooray.
Observation Summary: Frequent audible collapses on sides of road in untouched snow.  Not the loudest I’ve heard and most of them did not produce any visible cracks but feedback is feedback.
Weather Summary: S1 or less.  No accumulation during tour.  No wind.  Temps in teens.
Instability Comments: widespread audible collapses that were not loud or unexpected but definitely made me glad I was not center punching up Mt Grant.

Route: Fern Creek
Observation Summary: Now in winter's figurative adolescence, our weak angsty snowpack is still behaving sensitive to all of the recent growing pains. As in many recent observations, we were greeted by numerous loud whumpfs throughout our tour. At middle and lower elevations, the overlying slab is still relatively soft and unconsolidated. However at about 5800' and up, we found wind scouring and a stiffening slab. Stability test identified this persistent slab problem as stubborn on a NW facing slope near ridgetop, but the consequences are high. This overlying slab is now 2.5 feet thick and propagating characteristics prevail. We avoided encounters with instability during our descent by traveling on slopes less than 30 degrees. The skiing was angelic and stress free.
Weather Summary: Cold temps and light snowfall throughout the day. Moderate winds observed at upper elevations mid afternoon
Instability Comments: Numerous Whumpfs experienced throughout our tour. Mostly at Low/Mid Elevations. Above about 5800', we were ascending an exposed mid slope ridge which presented wind effect and a stiffening slab that seems more stubborn to sudden collapses.

Route: East Route - Ghoulies - Further Out into the Woods
Observation Summary: Lovely day for a tour with sunshine, blue skies and fresh(ish) snow! Given the recent avalanche activity and reports of widespread collapsing, we kept it mellow and stuck to slopes < 35* without wind effect and found great soft skiing all day.
Weather Summary: Inversions make for cold start and finish temperatures!
Instability Comments: Our party observed 3 separate collapses during our tour. Two while on the skin track on a northerly forested slope at ~5600' and another inbounds at WMR while exit skiing just past the T-bar base. No cracking and no recent avalanches observed in the immediate vicinity of our tour. We did see the natural avalanche crown in the Skook Chutes (previously reported) and thought we could see a large crown on the SW face of Heavens Peak?

Observation Summary: Recycled the skin track up to Marion. Did some pruning on the way in and special shout out to the east glacier couple taking time out of their day to cut and remove downed trees.  Dug one pit, SE aspect, 6500’. Total depth of 140cm. ECTP22 at 90 cm down.  We dug at this location because we noticed yesterday’s party had triggered some notable shooting cracks, where this 90 cm slab moved downhill by about 1-2 cm, as seen in picture. Overall calm day with enjoyable settled pow.

Route: Mostly skied picnic lakes area - east facing.
Observation Summary: It was surprisingly stable, and the snow was fantastic.
Instability Comments: Didn't get any movement on test slopes, and didn't get any cracking anywhere.  Didn't really get any collapsing either - maybe one small whoompf.   It was surprisingly stable, and the snow was fantastic.

Observation Summary: Day off looking to get out of inversions and into moderate (< 30*) terrain with good snow.
Weather Summary: Inversions, with 5* at car to start and end the day, but warmer above - teens to low 20s? Light east winds in AM, then burst of gusty easterly winds early afternoon that briefly drifted snow above 6000. Plumes off nearby peak at 7400 ft. Valley clouds, some stratus late morning, then bluebird.
Instability Comments: Frequent whumpfing on open, low-angled, southerly slopes above about 6000 ft, though primarily on slopes with no evidence of wind drifting/ scouring. Did not see any shooting cracks.

Observation Summary: Abundant crowns were observed throughout our tour (12+). The majority of them were suspected to have released during the 11/30-12/2 storm cycle. Others were hard to discern as recent, but certainly post-storm.  Destructive size ranged from D1-D2.5, at elevations from 6200'-7500', and on N/W/E aspects.One commonality noted was the localized nature of the crowns. Large, planar slopes would yield "pockets" of avalanche crowns, as opposed to ripping wall-to-wall. The recent storm snow is beginning to settle, but still largely cohesion-less and wind affect at upper elevations appeared minimal, although wind transport was beginning to occur during the PM hours on our descent. It felt pretty obvious to avoid steep, avalanche prone slopes due to the collapsing and recent avalanche activity.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Weather Summary: A slight inversion existed during the AM hours. A large cloud deck existed South of our location and sunshine was filtered. Temps remained below freezing all day.
Instability Comments: Collapsing/whumphing was frequent between 5000'-7000' (high point) on all aspects. A few of these collapses visibly shook vegetation as far as 30 yards away. Shooting cracks were observed at ridge-line locations where wind-stiffened snow was present.

Route: Walked out to old man’s/retirement.
Observation Summary: 2 avalanches were observed. One above beaver ponds that ripped near the ridge at a convex roll over. Debris ran into the trees. Approx 30 feet wide. Could not get a good angle to see depth. A second avalanche was observed in the skook chutes. It appears to be a natural release.
Instability Comments: No collapsing observed.

Observation Summary: Headed out to the Apgars on a cold, clear day and dodged the inversion that was visible south in the Flathead. We had a cautious mindset given the basal facets lurking, and were immediately confirmed in this with audible collapses throughout our day. Shooting cracks were only present in wind affected surface snow, and isolated in distribution. We dug on a relatively sheltered NE aspect at 6Kft. Failures occurred below the most recent storm snow with moderate force, and at the newer snow/facets interface with hard force, but did not propagate (HS 110cm, CT11 Q3 30cm down, CT24 Q3 60cm down, ECTN 11, ECTN 24 on respective layers). Ridgetop winds were light but steady from the SW all day. Skies were clear; no precip or active snow transport during our tour. Of note, upper elevations showed the development of small surface hoar atop the snowpack.
See other fields.
Instability Comments: Consistent whoompfing at and around skis while skinning but did not observe shooting cracks in terrain we were skiing.

Route: Road observations along Hwy 2 as well as a ski tour up the ridge above the Burnout, Shed 4D, Shed 5, and Shed 7 avalanche paths.
Observation Summary: Poor snowpack structure with a couple audible collapses and unstable test results in snowpits. Multiple avalanche crowns observed in nearby Flathead Range.
Weather Summary: •	Glorious day with clear skies, abundant sunshine, and calm or light N winds.•	A minor temperature inversion with daytime highs only reaching 10-14 °F on the Canyon floor and the mid to low-20s °F around 6000’ elevation.

Route: On trail
Observation Summary: Good visibility and decent coverage allowed for a full day of skiing in good dry snow.
Cloud Cover: Broken (Mostly Cloudy, more than 4/8 but less than 8/8)
Weather Summary: Was expecting wind but encountered none at all until nearing treeline/6000 ft.  Cold start in AM but felt mild/comfortable by afternoon.
Instability Comments: Isolated cracking at mid elevations

Route: Up south ridge of Six Mile to just barely NE over sub peak ridge.  Fast soft fun in mid 20's degree terrain.
Observation Summary: Consistent red flag collapses felt in mid elevations for persistent slab.  Windslab higher, uncertain of reactivity.  Great ski quality especially for lower elevations.  Still felt buried crust to 6700'.
Weather Summary: On S ridge, most of the snow available for transport had already been moved, cross loaded W to E.  At 1000h, observed isolated blowing snow on Swan crest further south.  Dropping temps once high clouds set in around 1100h.
Instability Comments: Did not observe any avalanches. More widespread whumphing both in southern clearings and wind effected slopes between 5800' and 6700'. Probably because of snowpack variability from wind, collapses were less common at upper end of that 900' band. 20-30cm hard windslab found at 6800' NE.

Route: Skied east aspects above Marion Lake.
Observation Summary: Alder, stumps, surfy, unconsolidated pow, some sun, more alder.
Weather Summary: Intermittent sun, minimal to no wind.
Instability Comments: Frequent, intense whoomph-ing, but no shooting cracks, nothing moving, no other signs of instability. No skier-triggered slides (or sluffs) or natural slides observed. Skied some steeper lines and rollovers with no activity (east-facing aspects). Things may get interesting if the sun shines and the temps. rise to near freezing tomorrow ...

Observation Summary:
We traveled to western Glacier Park to observe storm snow totals and the distribution of the mid-November weak layers.

The recent storm deposited over a foot of snow at low elevations and two-plus feet at mid-elevations.
 We found a widespread layer of facets and a faceted crust near the base of the snowpack in all hand pits and hasty pits on sunny aspects. 
The weak basal layers gave us feedback through collapsing/whumphing multiple times. Some of these were alarmingly loud.
The surface snow is beginning to become denser, but the slab is still too soft in many areas to produce cracking.
Wind effected snow was observed as low as 5500 feet. The cornice growth from southwest winds is quite impressive for December 3.
On the drive home, we noted that the west side of the Swan Range (Columbia Mountain) had been raked by recent southwest winds.
Weather Summary: Cool and mostly cloudy with light southwest winds.
Instability Comments: Multiple collapses were observed in undisturbed snow. Several of those were loud and rumbling. We had minimal cracking due to a relatively soft surface slab.

Route: East Route, Whitefish Mountain Resort
Observation Summary: While skinning up the East Route at 8:30AM on 12/2, skinner noticed skier dropping into Haskell Slide face. Skier made a few turns before remotely triggering an avalanche (see pictures) above the skier. Skier was observed overtaken by the avalanche towards the bottom of the face, but was not swept off their feet. Pictures captured of skier at bottom of run, crown, and avalanche path.

Route: Snowgo is under repairs, so with the chosen goal of exercise foremost, it was mostly a road ski.  Full coverage and no whoop-dees yet= 10/10 road ski conditions.    Toured 5800-6100' W aspect.
Observation Summary: Stayed in low elevations but did not see signs of a natural cycle on E and W aspects.  Felt many collapses once off the road, W aspect.  No shooting cracks but that is probably from lack of slab structure in overnight snow.  Did not observe signs of wind transport, but 20+cms of very low density new snow available.
New or Recent Snowfall (cm): 20 cm low density above 30cm high density, HST50
Weather Summary: Calm to Light winds.  Nil precip in AM to S-1 in PM.  Steady temps.
Instability Comments: Felt 4-6 whumphs on NW-SW small clearings.  Did not dig to verify which weak layer these failed on.

Route: Looking to investigate skier-triggered slide from earlier today.  Got to within 50 m but not comfortable getting closer.
Observation Summary: We're not in MT anymore, Toto. CO snowpack - weak, reactive snow near the ground, with soft, rapidly-loaded slab above. Slab still seems to need some settlement/ stiffness to produce more slides.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)
Cloud Cover: Obscured (Sky not visible due to fog or non-cloud layer)
Weather Summary: OVercast below about 6000 ft; foggy/ Obscured above. Felt calm below summit, though weather station showed some wind.
Instability Comments: Deep, wide whumpfs - 3 in the 1st 50 yards of skinning off piste. Cracks 20-40 feet away. Fewer collapses in terrain with previous ski tracks. Saw many long cracks from ski patrol mitigation (slope cuts and explosives).

Route: US Highway 2 Canyon Obs.
Observation Summary: 12/1/2211:30Overcast Very Light Snowfall Temp: 8 FRH: 34%Wind: very Light (Easterly)Total Snow Depth: 42cmDevil Creek;
Weather Summary: Very light snowfall Very light easterly windTemp 8FPrevious drifting of snow at Canyon floor elevations from east winds.
Instability Comments: No avalanche activity observed or obvious signs of instability. Previous drifting on roadway and right of way from easterly winds.

Observation Summary: Widespread whumpfing below 6000', less above. Remote triggered one slide above road on exit.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed  Collapsing Observed (widespread)

Observation Summary: Triggered one small but deep slide that left rider partially buried in debris.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Caught in Avalanche
Weather Summary: 38" or more of storm snow.

Route: Up Shed 6/7 Ridgeline
Observation Summary: We observed 30-50cm of new snow on our tour from 4300' to 6500' with a consistently subtle storm slab "upside down" structure to it. Areas of wind effect (along the ridgeline) produced frequent localized cracking (photo attached). We noted widespread collapsing at a variety of elevations and areas of both protected and wind affected snow. The new storm snow covers up many hazards, especially at lower elevations, that still make travel and skiing quite spicy!
Weather Summary: We observed inverted temperatures ranging from 5*F to 18*F, with light S winds, overcast/obscured skies, and S1-S2 snowfall rates throughout the day.
Instability Comments:
We saw no natural avalanches nor triggered any on test slopes. We observed localized shooting cracks (2m or less) frequently on our tour above 5300' in areas with any wind effect. We observed 5 snowpack collapses in a layer of well-developed facets near the bottom of the snowpack in both forested (protected) an open (wind affected) terrain.

It seemed that the structure was primed for easy avalanche triggering but needed a more consolidated slab formed for widespread propagation. A little wind or warmer snowfall could quickly tip the balance.

Route: Skied east of WMR.
Observation Summary: Kept the skiing low angle again and had no instability while skiing
Weather Summary: Snowed steadily all day with wind gusts on ridge lines blowing in our skin track.
Instability Comments: Several collapses.

Route: Climbed the Shed 6/7 ridge to the top of the Shed 7 East avalanche path, then moved up into the upper reaches of the Shed 7 West path. Descended the Shed 4D/5 vicinity. Objectives included: look for any recent avalanche activity, observe snow depths and general snowpack structure in this area, and note the extent of recent wind loading.
Observation Summary: Previous wind transport and loading was notable at elevations above 5000’, primarily on easterly aspects with new wind slabs up to a foot or more thick. Ski cuts produced some minor cracking (2-4’ long) underfoot.
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Cracking Observed (isolated)  Collapsing Observed (isolated)
Weather Summary: Some blowing snow noted on distant ridges above 6500’ as westerly winds picked up in the afternoon. Mostly overcast skies with occasional very light snowfall (S-1). Calm winds or light northerly winds clocking to the SW by the afternoon. Daytime highs reached 10 °F on the Canyon floor and hovered in the single digits °F around 6000’ elevation.
Instability Comments: Noted some minor whumpfing and collapses on the underlying facets on the Shed 6/7 ridge in areas with shallow snow depths between 5500-6000’ elevation.

Route: Traveled into the Apgar Range for some baseline data. Made my way up McGee Creek in hopes of skiing some familiar glades, but red flags redirected the days itinerary. Bad news was that the glades I hoped to ski (30-35+ degrees, same aspect) were closed for the day. Good news was that feedback for this decision was easy to come by. Who needs intuition when you've got Red Flags? Not wanting to completely bail without taking a closer look at the snowpack, I redirected my route towards a less steep (20-30 degree) sub ridge that was moderatly timbered and searched for a good pit site.
Observation Summary: If tomorrow's forecast verifies, I'd bet that human triggered avalances are likely on southernlies. And if I had a dollar for every whumph I heard today, that would be a handsome bet.
Weather Summary: Morning started crystal clear before becoming overcast by mid day. Calm winds observed all day at low-mid elevations.
Instability Comments: Caught wind of my first Whumpf around 5000ft on a SW facing slope. The culpret is facets/surface hoar buried a foot or so beneath the surface. Gradually ascending the valley, I continued to hear more and more whumpfing, same weak layer. Whumphing continued again with the ocasional shooting crack. No cracks visably traveled further than 20 ft, but some of the whumphs jostled vegetation up to 100 feet away.

Route: Climbed the SE ridge of Running Rabbit Mtn adjacent to and east of the avalanche path known as Second Slide to a high point at 5600’ elevation. Descended the ridge to the west of the path.
Observation Summary: Previous wind transport and loading was notable at elevations above 5000’, primarily on easterly aspects with new wind slabs up to a foot thick. Post-holing and stomping on several wind loaded test slopes resulted in no cracking or slab releases.
Weather Summary: Winds clocked to the east-northeast overnight but were mostly light and minimal wind loading was observed today. Mostly overcast skies with rare breaks showing some blue sky. No precipitation.Daytime highs reached 10-15 °F on the Canyon floor and hovered in the single-digits °F around 5000’ elevation.

Route: Ascended South ridge to sub peak at 6600'.  Quick circuit throughall aspects at this elevation then returned to trailhead via same route.
Observation Summary:
Checking out the weekend snows:

Observed no recent avalanches.
Initiated shooting cracks and collapsing on aspects SE through W from 5600'-6600'.
Large and small column tests failed with medium force on early-novemeber facets and surface hoar
Slabs above this weak layer were up to 12" thick. 
Found sensitive wind slabs 2-8" thick on SE aspect (from recent SW winds)
Current winds were from the NE. Blowing snow was enough to fill in skin tracks but had not yet formed reactive slabs.

Route: East of Ghoulie Point
Observation Summary:
FAC staff visited the southern Whitefish Range to assess near surface instabilities and snowpack structure.

We found 7-8 inches of new snow above 6000 feet. Most of this fell Sunday and was accompanied by several hours of moderate southwest winds with strong gusts.
Despite Sunday's breezy conditions, the snow surface remained soft and generally lacked cohesion in most locations. 
Shooting cracks and collapsing were common on an open east aspect at 6200 feet. We were able to trigger narrow soft slabs in the new snow intentionally. Slides failed on a layer of weak facets 8-12 inches below the surface. Due to the soft surface slab, slides only propagated 10-15 feet wide.
We looked at a rider-triggered slide on a north aspect at 6500 feet. This avalanche failed on the same layer of weak facets about a foot below the surface. Propagation was limited by the soft surface slab to 25 feet or so.
The snowpack structure in this area is poor and will probably prove problematic once a sufficient load arrives. Besides the facet layer, most of the pack consists of weak snow. Removing boots from skis resulted in sinking crotch deep and humorous wallowing. 
Riding was generally quite fun in all aspects.

location: Swan Range - West Side (Flathead Valley access)
Route: Traveled up the Switchback trail, gaining the ridge to work on the weather station. We descended down to Camp Misery and then road, back to the truck.
Observation Summary:
We made a plan to continue work on the Mt. Aneas weather station and feel out the new storm snow:

We encountered two small, natural slab avalanches that failed during the storm.
We observed widespread shooting cracks and collapsing especially between 5000' and 6500' and in wind affected terrain.
Our party remote triggered a small cornice on a test slope from 50' away.
Slabs were soft and generally 4-6" thick.
Hand pits revealed the weak structure we've been documenting over the last two weeks:  facets above and below the early November crust.

We elected to group ski down a ridge then picked our way through a short, treed slope (~30 degrees) noting more cracking and minor sluffing. We skied off into the sunset with dry snow down to 4000'
SIGNS OF INSTABILITY: Avalanches Observed  Avalanches Triggered  Cracking Observed (widespread)  Collapsing Observed (widespread)

Route: Within the ski area boundary before opening day.
Observation Summary:
I visited the southern Whitefish Range to collect baseline snowpack observations following a two-week dry spell. 

Cold, dry weather is weakening snow near the surface, in the middle of the pack, and layers near the ground. Faceting occurs in soft snow near the top and bottom of the pack, with slabs throughout the pack decomposing.
Shifting winds left slabs of drifted snow on all aspects above 6400 feet on slopes open to the wind. These slabs are old, generally supportable to skis, and stubborn to triggering. However, they are all underlain by weak cohesionless snow, which may prove problematic when substantial snowfall returns.
The surface consists of weak, cohesionless snow in areas sheltered from the wind. On steep slopes greater than 40 degrees, I could trigger small loose dry sluffs.
Riding conditions remain relatively good despite the lack of recent snow.
Weather Summary: A benign weather day. Moderate temperatures, no wind, and little sun.
Instability Comments: Good stability with loose dry avalanches is the only concern.

Route: Ascended across southern aspects to 6200' and returned via same route.
Observation Summary:
Stagnant air advisory = surface hoar?:

Foud only pockets of surface hoar in the best growing locations (open canopy, sheltered from sun) at low and mid elevations.  
Tromped through some thin/hard (2") wind slabs above 5500'.  These cracked easily under the weight of a skier. 
Noticed widespread faceting of the surface snow
Snowpack at 4000' was 8" of facets with a brittle crust ~ 2" down.
Snowpack at 6200' was 24" consisting of surface facets with faceting rounds beneath.
Weather Summary: Wx: Cirrus clouds approaching from West

Route: Headed up North Broken Leg Mountain to a max elevation of 6000' to get a first look at this winters base at mid-elevations.  Also wanted to see what sort of a surface any new snow will fall upon as as we finally transition out of this groundhog day weather pattern.
Observation Summary: Above 4900' rime was thick on the trees, shrubs, and still standing tall dried beargrass flowers.Travel was difficult due to the rediculous tangle of downed trees in some areas.  Slippery trunks and collapses into the voids under trees made for even more fun.Traverses across even low slope angles were difficult due to the sandy/sugary nature of the surface snow not accepting an edge.This snow profile is hopefully the first of many from the snow study plot established at this location.

Weather Summary: Encountered the stratus deck at 4900' and did not experience it's upper limit.Hardly even a breath of wind all day under OBS and then X skies.Some beautiful crevice hoar observed amongst the exposed rocks.

Route: Quick trip to Red Meadow area. Road is driveable to upper Whitefish Lake.
Observation Summary: Rain crusts, wind effects, a few old crowns.

I stretched my legs on a short day-off walk in the Apgars. 

The snowpack varied between 8 inches at 3950 feet to 14 inches at 5500'.
Recent cold temperatures have weakened surface snow and older snow near the ground. Most of the pack in this elevation range is Fist hard and cohesionless. There is a strong 1-inch thick crust in the upper third of the pack, which is generally supportable to skis. Please see the Snowpack tab for more information.  
The snow surface remains cold in terrain sheltered from the sun. 
The Going-to-The Sun Road is closed at Apgar this winter season, and driving the Camas Road is currently the only game in town for accessing winter recreation in west-central GNP.
Weather Summary: Overcast day with little wind, no precipitation, and moderate temperatures.
Instability Comments: Good stability

Observation Summary:
We traveled to the Aeneas Ridge weather station to troubleshoot a problem with a temperature and relative humidity sensor. Visiting this area gave us a general sense of the snowpack distribution and structure in a location favored by this season's early-season snowfall.

Below 5000 feet, there are 4 - 8 inches of snow on the ground. Above 6000 feet, 30 - 40 inches in areas sheltered from the wind. 
Evidence of last week's westerly and recent northeasterly winds is visible in most aspects above 6000 feet. All varieties of wind-affected snow were enjoyed, including sastrugi, hard slab, soft slab, windboard, and cornice formation. 
Recent atypical northeasterly winds formed reactive 2 - 4 inch slabs on westerly aspects. Westerly winds from last week left stubborn slabs on the north and east aspects. 
Our snowpit on a northwest aspect at 6700 feet confirmed the pack is currently right side up, i.e., the snowpack near the ground is stronger than the snow closer to the surface. Please see the Snowpack tab for more details.
Variable snow conditions and plenty of surface obstacles such as brush, rocks, and down trees kept us on our toes.
Weather Summary: Bluebird day with cool temperatures, no wind, and plenty of sunshine.
Instability Comments: Reactive 2-4" slabs on westerly aspects from recent atypical winds.

Observation Summary: Triggered small soft slabs on the skier's right side of Crown Bowl.
